experiments.title: How adding the donation amount to the donate button on mobile impacted conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding the donation amount for mobile donors will improve conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/15/2025 - 10/27/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of adding the donation amount to the donate button for mobile users. We hypothesized that showing the donation amount at the point of conversion would reduce friction by reinforcing the user’s selection and thus would increase conversion rate.
We had run this test earlier in the year with different results. For this experiment, we ran it on a Gaza-specific donation form that was getting traffic due to recent news in the area.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test assessed whether showing the donation amount on the "Donate" button increased mobile donor confidence and completion rates.
Key Results:
The conversion lift was statistically significant — the overall conversion rate increased by 37% at the 99% confidence level. Revenue Per Visitor (RPV) also trended upward by 12%; however, this change was not statistically significant, as it did not reach the 95% confidence threshold.
What the Results Suggest:
Displaying the amount on the button helps mobile donors commit with more confidence.

experiments.title: How adding a monthly callout impacts conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a visual call out for monthly giving for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/17/2026 - 09/26/2026
With monthly giving a big focus for Save the Children, we want to test whether a graphic call-out highlighting the impact of monthly giving would increase conversion rates. We ran 3 different variants for this test, only for desktop traffic (since the buttons stacked on mobile):
Control
Variant 1- monthly call out text below the monthly/one-time array button
Variant 2- monthly call out text above the monthly/one-time array button
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test aimed to assess the impact of adding a visual monthly callout on the donation form. We hypothesized that this would strengthen the proposition for monthly giving.
Key Results:
This test showed that the visual monthly callout made the recurring option more salient and easier to process. By reducing cognitive friction and highlighting it as a “default-worthy” choice, users were nudged to see monthly as the standard, or even preferred, behavior.
Rather than deterring one-time donors, the callout acted as a commitment anchor. Donors compared themselves against the recurring option and felt reassured in choosing either. The result is a “lift all boats” effect: greater clarity increases confidence, which increases giving.
This suggests that visual emphasis on giving types does more than shift behavior between options. It can grow the whole pie by lowering decision friction and reinforcing giving as the normative action.

experiments.title: How adding a impact statement below the donate button affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an impact statement below the donate button for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because highlighting the potential impact of their gift at the donate button would act as an extra layer of reinforcement and encourage the user to donate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/12/2025
CaringBridge had a 2 day match. For this match, we wanted to test adding an impact statement below the donate button that highlighted that their gift would be doubled during this match. The statement read as follows –
Your $x gift goes twice as far, keeping this a free platform for patients, caregivers, and families to receive support and coordinate care every single day throughout a difficult health journey.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the impact statement below the donate button did not have the intended effect of increasing donor conversion rates. In fact, the treatment group experienced a significant 21.5% decrease in donations compared to the control group. This result was statistically significant at a 99.7% confidence level.

In future experimentation, it's important to carefully consider the messaging and placement of impact statements or any additional content on donation pages. It's possible that the impact statement used in this experiment was not compelling enough or may have caused confusion or distraction for potential donors.

There was a decrease in revenue as well. There was an 18% decrease in revenue with a 41% level of confidence

Control - $17,443

Treatment - $14,520

Moving forward, it may be beneficial to test different variations of impact statements, adjust the placement of the statement on the page, or even consider other strategies to emphasize the impact of donations. Additionally, gathering feedback from donors through surveys or qualitative research could provide valuable insights into what resonates with them and what motivates them to donate.

Overall, while this experiment did not yield the desired results, it provides valuable insights that can inform and improve future fundraising strategies for CaringBridge.

experiments.title: How adding value prop to the donate button impacts donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding additional value prop to the donate button on Care Net’s main donation page will achieve more donations because it will encourage users to click the donate button. It will remind them why they are giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/29/2025 - 12/08/2025
Care Net’s donation pages typically have a donate button that says “Give Now” under that it says “Amount: $XX.” We were interested in seeing what would happen if we replaced the “Amount: $XX” with additional value prop. We replaced it with “I want to protect life.” We ran this experiment on their main donation page because that’s where they see the majority of their traffic and we knew this test would have a better chance of validating.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment demonstrated the powerful impact of mission-driven language on donor behavior. By replacing the default “Amount: $XX” label on Care Net’s highest-traffic donation page with a value-based statement — “I want to protect life” — the test reframed the giving decision from a financial transaction to a personal expression of identity and belief. The treatment produced a 44% lift in conversion rate, increasing from 18.7% to 27.0%, with 99.3% statistical confidence, confirming that the effect was both meaningful and reliable.
This result suggests that donors are more likely to complete a gift when the call to action connects directly to their values and sense of purpose, rather than focusing on dollar amounts or mechanics. In essence, emphasizing the “why” of giving at the point of decision appears to activate emotional motivation and a sense of alignment with the organization’s mission. For Care Net, this insight reinforces the importance of using identity-based and mission-oriented framing not just in broader messaging but in microcopy across key conversion points. Future tests could explore how different variations of purpose-driven language—tailored to distinct donor motivations—further influence giving behavior.

experiments.title: Will Testing Different CTAs in Facebook Ad Copy to Increase Donor Conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a direct ask like “Click to Give Bibles” for Facebook audiences will achieve higher donor conversion than a softer “Click to Learn More” call-to-action because because clearer donation language reduces ambiguity and increases motivation to act on the giving opportunity.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/08/2026 - 01/30/2026
Bible League Canada was preparing to launch a new set of Facebook ads focused on a clear value proposition: asking users to donate to place Bibles with waiting believers around the world. In previous campaigns, some ad variations ended with “Click to Learn More >>”, while others used a more direct ask of “Click to Give Bibles >>”.
To better understand which type of call-to-action language drives higher donor conversions, the team set up a simple A/B test. The only element changed between versions was the final line of the primary ad copy:
Control: “Click to Learn More >>”
Treatment: “Click to Give Bibles >>”
Everything else — the ad creative, targeting, and budget — remained the same. The ads were shown to a broad Facebook audience, including existing donors, subscribers, lookalike audiences, and open targeting.
The big question we wanted to answer was: Does a direct ask improve donor response by providing clarity and urgency, or does a softer, curiosity-based CTA encourage more engagement that leads to giving?
experiments.keyLearnings:
We ran this test for 23 days, and found that the direct, action-oriented call-to-action language significantly outperformed the softer alternative in driving donations through Facebook. Ads that ended with “Click to Give Bibles” led to 117.6% more donations than those ending with “Click to Learn More,” with a statistically significant 98.6% level of confidence. This is a major win for Bible League Canada and strongly supports using clearer giving language in our future ads.

When we dug further into the data, we found that this stronger performance was driven by both increased engagement and improved conversion:
The direct CTA generated 5.5% more ad clicks compared to the control, trending toward statistical validity at 92.7% confidence.
 	Those who clicked the Treatment were also twice as likely to convert into donors, with a 1.75% instant conversion rate for the Treatment compared to 0.88% for the Control.
the Treatment also showed a higher average gift
$52.99 CAD
Gross Cost Per Donor dropped from $170 (Control) to $87 (Treatment)
 	ROI more than doubled, from 0.25 (Control) to 0.61 (Treatment)

experiments.title: How including a call-to-action in the headline of the donation page affects conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that adding a clear call-to-action directly into the donation page headline would increase donor conversion rates because it would make the request immediate and emotionally explicit at the moment of decision, reinforcing the donor’s role and purpose in giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/18/2025 - 12/30/2025
This experiment ran during The Heritage Foundation’s year-end fundraising campaign, when donors were already primed to give and donation page traffic was high. The campaign messaging emphasized urgency, high stakes, and the importance of acting before the calendar year closed. At this point in the funnel, visitors had already clicked through with intent, making the donation page headline a critical moment to either reinforce commitment or introduce friction.
Earlier research and experiments suggested Heritage supporters respond strongly to calls for action and personal responsibility, raising the question of whether explicitly placing the ask in the donation page headline would improve performance—or inadvertently overwhelm donors who had already decided to give.
The test ran on the donation page headline itself. All visitors arriving at the donation page were randomly assigned to see either the control headline or one of two CTA-embedded headline variations. This placement tested the impact of headline framing at the final decision point, immediately before donors selected a gift amount and completed their contribution.
We wanted to understand whether making the ask unavoidable at the top of the donation page would increase conversions—or whether it would feel redundant or overly aggressive for donors already in the act of giving. The two treatments tested distinct psychological levers: identity alignment (“stand with Heritage”) versus action-hero framing (“join the fight”), both rooted in prior survey insights about donor motivation, urgency, and self-perception.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment produced statistically valid results, and the outcome was clear: including a CTA directly in the donation page headline decreased performance. Both treatment variants underperformed the control in conversion rate, average gift, and revenue per visitor. Treatment A saw a significant drop in conversions (-19.7%) with high confidence, while Treatment B also underperformed, though less severely.
The control headline—descriptive but non-directive—performed best across all key metrics. This suggests that once donors reach the donation page, they do not need to be asked again. Introducing a fresh CTA at this stage likely created friction or pressure, disrupting donors who were already prepared to act.
Moving forward, we will avoid embedding overt calls-to-action in donation page headlines and instead use that space to reinforce context, stakes, and meaning rather than issuing another ask. CTAs appear to be more effective earlier in the funnel—on landing pages, emails, and homepage messaging—while donation pages perform better when they assume intent and reduce cognitive load.
This experiment reinforces a broader principle: clarity and restraint matter most at the point of conversion. For high-intent donors, the role of the donation page is to remove barriers, not escalate persuasion.

experiments.title: How adding an impact callout to a pre-selected monthly tab affects recurring donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a callout on the monthly tab, highlighting the impact of monthly giving for donation page visitors, will increase recurring donations because the callout will help users understand the potential impact they can have on the FUS mission and encourage them to make a recurring donation.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/26/2025 - 10/08/2025
Franciscan University has content offers of their video series’ running on Facebook, which perform really well. They get a lot of donations from these offers, so we wanted to look into ways we could get more recurring donors from audiences coming to the donation pages.
For this test, we had the monthly tab pre-selected and we added a callout to hover above the tab that had language about how monthly giving was the most impactful way they could help FUS continue doing their mission.
This was tested on the donation page of the offers currently running for them in Facebook.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:
1. Adding a callout on the monthly tab highlighting the impact of monthly giving led to a 1.5% increase in Recurring Donations for All Traffic at a 100.0% level of statistical confidence.
2. However, this increase in recurring donations was accompanied by a 211% decrease in Transactions with a 96% level of confidence and a 411% decrease in One-Time Donations with a 100% level of confidence.
In future experimentation, it is crucial to carefully weigh the benefits of increasing recurring donations against the significant decline observed in one-time donations and transactions. Further analysis of why one-time donations and transactions dropped could reveal ways to promote recurring giving without causing large declines in other donation types.
Test different callout messages or positions to optimize recurring donations without hurting other metrics. Segment the audience by behavior and preferences to tailor the approach and maximize total donations.
Continue experimenting with new strategies and closely monitor all donation metrics. Aim to increase recurring donations while maintaining overall donation levels.

experiments.title: How adding the donation amount to the donation button impacted conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding the donation amount for potential donors will achieve change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/12/2025 - 06/16/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of adding the donation amount to the donate button. Our hypothesis was that adding the amount to the button when people went to submit their donation would remove some of the anxiety when donating.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Results:

-       +31% statistically significant increase (99%) in overall conversion rate.

-       -25% non-statistically significant decline (85%) in overall RPV.

-       -70% statistically significant decline (99%) in recurring donor conversion rate.

-       -46% non-statistically significant decline (94%) in recurring RPV.

Although there was a lift in overall conversion, driven by mobile users, there was a significant decline in revenue. This test suggests that calling out the donation total on the last step causes friction for certain audiences and also gives them another opportunity to question their giving amount.

Recommendation/Implementation:

Given our priority for recurring giving, we will not be implementing the variant.

Desktop:

Control: 58.5% conversion rate

Treatment: 50% conversion rate--a 15% decrease with a 64% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor also decreased by 35%, with an 88% level of confidence.

Mobile

Control: 36% conversion rate

Treatment: 64% conversion rate, which is a 74% increase with a 99.9% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor also increased by 33%, with a 75% level of confidence.

One-time gifts:

Control: 36% conversion rate.

Treatment: 53% conversion rate--a 47% increase with a 99% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor decreased by 24%, with an 82% level of confidence.

Monthly gits:

Control: 9.5% conversion rate

Treatment: 2.9% conversion rate--a 70% decrease with a 99% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor also decreased by 46% with a 94% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a slide up with a gift array performs against a pop-up with a simple donate button
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering people specific gift asks for the fund drive will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/30/2025 - 08/13/2025
During a special fund drive for OPB, the public media broadcasting station for Oregon and Southwest Washington, we wanted to test how a standard pop-up with a simple donate button (what OPB traditionally uses during a fund drive) would perform against a slide-up that included specific gift arrays.
This test was run on article pages only; a separate test was run on the homepage.
experiments.keyLearnings:
There were some exciting results from this experiment. The unique fundraising environment for this test is not something that cannot be easily repeatable, unless there is another urgent/emergency campaign. That being said, the increase in conversion rate from the slide up gift array version is huge and statistically valid. The results above can be directly attributed to people clicking the button/array and completing their gift.

A couple of interesting downstream results to be aware of:
The control version had a slightly higher average gift than the treatment version; given the lower volume of gifts on the control, that could be the influence of a larger gift or two, or the fact that we were showing donors a gift array up front in the treatment version, leading them to give a lower average gift (gift arrays on the actual donation forms were the same for both control and treatment).
 	Regarding the overall influence both had on total giving, the gift array version had a slightly larger influence, showing a 23% increase in conversion rates overall with a 96% level of confidence. This means that people who saw the gift array version and then made a gift at some point to a donation form on the website (not necessarily the one linked directly from the pop-up) were 23% more likely to convert.

experiments.title: How including a button in an email appeal affects click-through rate for donors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a button to an email for donors will achieve an increase in click-through rate because it provides a clear, visually distinct call-to-action that reduces friction, draws the reader’s eye, and makes it easier for people to take the intended next step.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/26/2025
The Heritage Foundation is running its annual Leader’s Club campaign. The second email of the campaign offered a free ebook called “Flags of Our Fathers.” Within this email, they wanted to test how changing the raw URL to a button would affect the click-through rate to the landing page. This experiment ran on one email, and this segment included donors to The Heritage Foundation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a button to an email for donors can significantly increase the click-through rate. In this case, the treatment with the button resulted in a 59.2% increase in clicks compared to the control group with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence.

This result confirms the hypothesis that a clear, visually distinct call-to-action, such as a button, can reduce friction, draw the reader’s eye, and make it easier for people to take the intended next step.

In future experimentation, we should continue to test the effectiveness of different call-to-action elements, such as buttons, in emails targeted to donors. By consistently analyzing and optimizing our email designs, we can maximize engagement and ultimately drive more conversions for The Heritage Foundation’s campaigns.

experiments.title: How including a button in an email appeal affects click-through rate for subscribers
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a button to an email for subscribers will achieve an increase in click-through rate because it provides a clear, visually distinct call-to-action that reduces friction, draws the reader’s eye, and makes it easier for people to take the intended next step.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/26/2025
The Heritage Foundation is running its annual Leader’s Club campaign. The second email of the campaign offered a free ebook called “Flags of Our Fathers.” Within this email, they wanted to test how changing the raw URL to a button would affect the click-through rate to the landing page. This experiment ran on one email, and this segment included donors to The Heritage Foundation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a button to an email for subscribers resulted in a significant increase in the click-through rate. The treatment group, which received the email with a button linking to the ebook "Flags of Our Fathers," saw a 25.6% increase in clicks compared to the control group, which had a raw URL link.

This outcome supports the hypothesis that visually distinct call-to-action buttons reduce friction, draw the reader's eye, and make it easier for people to take the intended next step. Moving forward, this learning should be applied to future experimentation by emphasizing the use of clear and prominent buttons in emails to encourage higher engagement and click-through rates. Additionally, A/B testing different variations of buttons, such as color, text, size, and placement, can further optimize email performance and drive desired actions from subscribers.

experiments.title: How adding an impact statement below the donate button during a Challenge Grant affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the copy on the donate button and adding a statement highlighting an impact statement for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in revenue and donations because highlighting the impact of their gift and reminding them that the impact of the gift will double would encourage users to donate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/04/2025 - 08/06/2025
Time of Grace has a challenge grant every year in August. This allows users to double the impact of their gift up to the goal amount that TOG sets, with each challenge grant highlighting a problem that TOG is trying to tackle. For this test, we wanted to test changing the copy on the donate button and adding a statement below the donate button highlighting the impact of the user’s gift. The statement was dynamic and would pull in the amount that the user was planning on donating. So for example, if the user were to donate $25, the statement would read as “Your $25 gift connects more people with the healing and freedom found only in Christ when we are equipped to confess and confront the struggles we don’t talk about.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the copy on the donate button and adding a personalized impact statement below the button significantly increased revenue and donations. The treatment group, which included the updated copy and impact statement, saw a substantial 187.7% increase in revenue compared to the control group. This result was statistically significant at a 100.0% confidence level.

It is also important to note that we saw a slight increase in transactions as well, making sure that this experiment did not cause a decrease.

In future experimentation, it is crucial to continue emphasizing the impact of the donation and the concept of doubling the impact through the challenge grant. Highlighting the tangible outcomes of a user's donation can motivate them to contribute more and feel a stronger connection to the cause they are supporting. Personalized impact statements based on the user's intended donation amount can also be a powerful way to make the donation feel more meaningful and relevant to the donor.

Overall, this experiment demonstrates the importance of effectively communicating the impact of a donation to potential donors and how small changes to the donation process can lead to significant increases in revenue. These learnings should be applied to future experiments and donation campaigns to continue optimizing donation processes and driving more support for Time of Grace Ministry.

experiments.title: How including a button in an appeal email affects email click-through rates for non-members
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a visually prominent, action-oriented button that says “Double My Gift” will increase email click-through rate because it draws more attention than a plain-text hyperlink, creates a clearer and more compelling call to action, and reduces friction by making the next step more obvious and easier to complete.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/01/2025
The Heritage Foundation conducted this experiment as part of their President’s Challenge matching gift campaign. This campaign was designed to drive donor engagement by offering a limited-time opportunity to double the impact of a donor’s gift through a matching challenge. The email in question was the first send in the campaign sequence, making it a high-leverage moment for influencing donor behavior and maximizing initial response rates.
Given the high stakes of the campaign’s early momentum and the importance of clearly communicating the match opportunity, the Heritage Foundation sought to test whether different call-to-action (CTA) formats would affect click-through and conversion rates.
The test was deployed via email, targeting the Heritage Foundation’s non-member list.
Both versions of the email—control and treatment—were linked to the same donation landing page but differed in their CTA presentation.
The experiment was driven by a hypothesis rooted in behavioral and conversion rate optimization principles: a visually prominent, action-oriented button might outperform a plain-text hyperlink in driving user action.
Key motivations included:
Improving clarity and visibility of the CTA: While the raw URL in the control version is technically functional, it lacks the immediate visual weight and action language of a button. The team suspected that this might cause the CTA to be overlooked or feel less urgent.
Reducing cognitive friction: A clear, concise button like “Double My Gift” directly mirrors the donor’s intent and may simplify decision-making.
Encouraging mobile-friendly design: Buttons often perform better on mobile devices where touch interaction is key, and plain-text links can be harder to tap.
This test is a good example of using micro-optimizations in email fundraising design to explore how seemingly small changes can have an outsized effect on user behavior and conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that using a visually prominent, action-oriented button with clear and compelling language can significantly increase click-through rates in email campaigns. In this case, the treatment with the button saying “Double My Gift” outperformed the control with a plain-text hyperlink, resulting in a 42.0% increase in clicks for all traffic.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing different elements of email design, such as CTAs, to optimize for user engagement and conversion rates. By focusing on creating clear, visually engaging CTAs that reduce cognitive friction and make the next steps obvious and easy to complete, organizations can increase the effectiveness of their email fundraising efforts. Additionally, considering the importance of mobile-friendly design in email campaigns, using buttons rather than plain-text links can further improve user experience and drive better results.

The test was deployed via email, targeting the Heritage Foundation’s member list.
Both versions of the email—control and treatment—were linked to the same donation landing page but differed in their CTA presentation.

Key motivations included:
Improving clarity and visibility of the CTA: While the raw URL in the control version is technically functional, it lacks the immediate visual weight and action language of a button. The team suspected that this might cause the CTA to be overlooked or feel less urgent.
Reducing cognitive friction: A clear, concise button like “Double My Gift” directly mirrors the donor’s intent and may simplify decision-making.
Encouraging mobile-friendly design: Buttons often perform better on mobile devices where touch interaction is key, and plain-text links can be harder to tap.
This test is a good example of using micro-optimizations in email fundraising design to explore how seemingly small changes can have an outsized effect on user behavior and conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the visually prominent, action-oriented button with the text "Double My Gift" did not perform as expected. In fact, it resulted in a 47.7% decrease in donations compared to the plain-text hyperlink used in the control version of the email. This outcome was statistically significant with a confidence level of 99.7%.

In future experimentation, it is important to remember that assumptions based on design principles and best practices may not always hold true for every audience or context. It is crucial to test different elements and approaches to see what resonates best with donors. Additionally, it is essential to prioritize evidence-based decision-making over assumptions, even if they seem logical on the surface. This result highlights the importance of testing variations and being open to unexpected outcomes to truly understand donor behavior and optimize conversion rates effectively.

experiments.title: How including a button in an appeal email affects email click-through rates for members
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a visually prominent, action-oriented button that says “Double My Gift” will increase email click-through rate because it draws more attention than a plain-text hyperlink, creates a clearer and more compelling call to action, and reduces friction by making the next step more obvious and easier to complete.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/01/2025
The Heritage Foundation conducted this experiment as part of their President’s Challenge matching gift campaign. This campaign was designed to drive donor engagement by offering a limited-time opportunity to double the impact of a donor’s gift through a matching challenge. The email in question was the first send in the campaign sequence, making it a high-leverage moment for influencing donor behavior and maximizing initial response rates.
Given the high stakes of the campaign’s early momentum and the importance of clearly communicating the match opportunity, the Heritage Foundation sought to test whether different call-to-action (CTA) formats would affect click-through and conversion rates.
The test was deployed via email, targeting the Heritage Foundation’s member list.
Both versions of the email—control and treatment—were linked to the same donation landing page but differed in their CTA presentation.
The experiment was driven by a hypothesis rooted in behavioral and conversion rate optimization principles: a visually prominent, action-oriented button might outperform a plain-text hyperlink in driving user action.
Key motivations included:
Improving clarity and visibility of the CTA: While the raw URL in the control version is technically functional, it lacks the immediate visual weight and action language of a button. The team suspected that this might cause the CTA to be overlooked or feel less urgent.
Reducing cognitive friction: A clear, concise button like “Double My Gift” directly mirrors the donor’s intent and may simplify decision-making.
Encouraging mobile-friendly design: Buttons often perform better on mobile devices where touch interaction is key, and plain-text links can be harder to tap.
This test is a good example of using micro-optimizations in email fundraising design to explore how seemingly small changes can have an outsized effect on user behavior and conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that using a visually prominent, action-oriented button with clear and compelling language like "Double My Gift" can significantly increase email click-through rates. The treatment group, which featured this type of button, saw a 22.1% increase in clicks compared to the control group that used a plain-text hyperlink.

Moving forward, this experiment highlights the importance of clear and visually engaging CTAs in driving user action. Future experimentation should continue to explore the impact of design elements, such as buttons versus plain-text links, in optimizing conversion rates. Additionally, leveraging insights from this experiment can help in creating more effective and engaging email campaigns, ultimately maximizing donor engagement and fundraising success.

experiments.title: How a $50 impact callout on a premium donation page affects average gift
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an impact callout for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in revenue and average gift because highlighting what the $50 gift could do for someone would encourage users to give a higher gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/03/2025 - 07/08/2025
For Time of Grace’s premium donation pages, we wanted to test some iterations of an impact callout to see how that would affect revenue and average gift. We did and A/B/C test here. For the first variant, the language was highlighting what the gift amount would do. The second variant was more about the impact of the gift. This test was done for Facebook audiences.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding an impact callout to donation pages leads to a significant increase in revenue. The treatment, which highlighted what a $50 gift could do for someone, resulted in a 72.0% increase in revenue for all traffic with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence.
We also saw a significant increase in average gift for both variants, with variant 1 having the higher average gift.
In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing the effectiveness of impact callouts on donation pages. This could involve further refining the messaging around the impact of donations and testing different ways to communicate the difference a gift could make. Additionally, expanding this test to other channels or audiences could help determine the broader impact of impact callouts on donation behavior. Overall, the results of this experiment highlight the importance of emphasizing the impact of donations in fundraising efforts for Time of Grace Ministry.

In future experimentation, we should consider the familiarity and popularity of different programs or campaigns when determining which call to action to feature prominently. By prioritizing content that is already well-known and loved by our audience, we can potentially increase engagement and drive more conversions. experiments.title: How adding the donation amount to the donation button impacted conversion rates.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding the donation amount for potential donors will achieve change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/12/2025 - 06/16/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of adding the donation amount to the donate button. Our hypothesis was that adding the amount to the button when people went to submit their donation would remove some of the anxiety when donating.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Results:
-       +31% statistically significant increase (99%) in overall conversion rate.
-       -25% non-statistically significant decline (85%) in overall RPV.
-       -70% statistically significant decline (99%) in recurring donor conversion rate.
-       -46% non-statistically significant decline (94%) in recurring RPV.
Although there was a lift in overall conversion, driven by mobile users, there was a significant decline in revenue. This test suggests that calling out the donation total on the last step causes friction for certain audiences and also gives them another opportunity to question their giving amount.
Recommendation/Implementation:
Given our priority for recurring giving, we will not be implementing the variant.
Desktop:
Control: 58.5% conversion rate
Treatment: 50% conversion rate--a 15% decrease with a 64% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor also decreased by 35%, with an 88% level of confidence.
Mobile
Control: 36% conversion rate
Treatment: 64% conversion rate, which is a 74% increase with a 99.9% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor also increased by 33%, with a 75% level of confidence.
One-time gifts:
Control: 36% conversion rate.
Treatment: 53% conversion rate--a 47% increase with a 99% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor decreased by 24%, with an 82% level of confidence.
Monthly gits:
Control: 9.5% conversion rate
Treatment: 2.9% conversion rate--a 70% decrease with a 99% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor also decreased by 46% with a 94% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding a sticky navigation bar on mobile affects clicks on Donate button
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a stacked navigation bar for mobile users will achieve an increase in clicks to the donate button because adding that stacked bar would increase clarity among the buttons and look cleaner.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/17/2025 - 05/20/2025
For CaringBridge, their mobile navigation bar consisted of multiple buttons where there was minimal space and all the buttons looked very small and stuck together. For this test, we wanted to change the layout of the navigation bar. We added a space above the original navigation bar and added the donate and search button on the extra space above the nav bar. The stacked part with the search button and donate button was sticky, so users could see it while scrolling through. We wanted to test if doing this would affect clicks to the donate button for mobile users.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

 	Adding a stacked navigation bar for mobile users led to a significant increase in clicks to the donate button (72.4% increase). This indicates that making the donate button more prominent and easily accessible can positively impact user engagement with this feature.
 	The navigation search button also saw a substantial increase in clicks (676% increase). This suggests that improving the visibility and usability of buttons in the navigation bar can drive higher interaction rates across multiple features.

In future experimentation, we should consider the following:

 	Continue to prioritize the visibility and accessibility of key features, such as the donate button, by strategically placing them in the navigation bar where they are easily noticeable and clickable.
 	Use A/B testing and other experimentation methods to iteratively optimize the layout and design of the mobile navigation bar to enhance user experience and drive engagement with important features.
 	Consider applying similar strategies to other areas of the website or app to improve overall user interaction and achieve organizational goals, such as increasing donations or promoting specific actions.

experiments.title: How should we layout the episode pages?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that moving the featured resource CTA higher on the page for the broadcast episode pages will achieve more traffic getting to the donation page because the CTA is more visible.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/01/2025 - 05/02/2025
Each day tons of people go to their broadcast pages to pick through the episodes that they would like to listen to. On the episode pages you can watch the show, read more about the guests, and then towards the bottom of the page you have the opportunity to get the resources that they talked about on the show.
The resources offer leds the user to a donation page where they can get the resource with a gift. We thought, if we rearrange the page to make the resource CTA more at the top we could drive more traffic to the donation page and get more transactions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that moving the featured resource CTA higher on the broadcast episode pages significantly increased the traffic to the donation page. The treatment group, which had the CTA placed higher on the page, saw an impressive 18.9% increase in CTA button clicks for all traffic compared to the control group. This difference was statistically significant at a confidence level of 100.0%, indicating that the results are very reliable.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing the placement of CTAs on the page to maximize engagement and conversion rates. Additionally, it would be beneficial to further analyze user behavior to understand why the higher placement of the CTA led to more clicks. This insight can help in optimizing the design and placement of CTAs across other pages on the website as well.

Overall, the success of this experiment highlights the importance of CTA placement in driving user action. By utilizing this learning and continuing to test and optimize CTAs, Focus on the Family can further improve user engagement and increase donations on their broadcast episode pages.

experiments.title: Does adding a monthly donate button in the main navigation impact conversion rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a second monthly donate button for potential donors will achieve an increase in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/17/2024 - 02/06/2024
In this test we aimed to increase the overall conversion rate by introducing two separate donation buttons, leading to distinct forms for one-time and recurring donations. We hypothesized that users would be more inclined to donate if they received a more explicit message about the available donation options, reducing ambiguity. Additionally, pre-determining the type of donation before entering the donation form would minimize friction during the process.
Our main hypothesis was that including an additional button specifically for recurring donations, in addition to the standard red donate button, is likely to have a positive impact on our overall conversion rate. Not only will it reduce the number of cancellations after the first donation, but it will also make the donation process more convenient for potential donors who prefer to make recurring contributions. This will ultimately lead to increased qualified donors and a higher conversion rate.
In this experiment we had 2 treatment variants; the first had a ‘donate’ button that led to the default combo form (one-time default), while the ‘give monthly’ button led to the same form, but with monthly pre-selected.
The second treatment had the same functionality for the ‘donate’ button, but the ‘give monthly’ button linked to a form that only offered recurring giving as the main option (there was a secondary link the form to give one-time).
experiments.keyLearnings:
Data analysis revealed that both variants substantially increased overall revenue and conversion rates. The improvements were primarily observed in desktop users, indicating that the changes implemented significantly impacted this segment. Significant results from this segment were:
Variant B:
-       +45% statistically significant lift (98%) in desktop one-time conversion rate.
-       +126% statistically significant lift (99%) in desktop one-time RPV.
-       +118% statistically significant lift (96%) in desktop recurring conversion rate.
Variant C:
-       +42% statistically significant lift (98%) in desktop one-time conversion rate.
-       +92% statistically significant lift (99%) in desktop one-time RPV.
-       +146% statistically significant lift (98%) in desktop recurring conversion rate.
These results support our hypotheses that (1) Offering users two distinct donation buttons, leading to separate forms for one-time and recurring donations, can increase the conversion rate. (2) A more explicit message about the available donation options, with less ambiguity, can further improve the likelihood of user conversion. (3) Pre-determining the type of donation before entering the form may also reduce friction during the process.

experiments.title: How does a closed menu and floating action button impact flywheel metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a sticky, floating action button for authors that allow them to open their flywheel menu at anytime will achieve an increase in these “manage my page” actions because reduces the space and friction of the current menu, while also prioritizing these flywheel options in a way that remains on the page easily.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/21/2025 - 02/24/2025
A key metric for CaringBridge is the Author flywheel. These are a list of menu items labelled “Manage My Page” with critical actions like sharing their page, making a post, starting a Ways to Get Help, and more. In previous testing, we’ve found that all of these metrics are improved by simply having this menu open when the author arrive on their page in mobile. However, CaringBridge feared this extra step of having to close it would be friction for the user. As an alternative, our teams developed two iterations of a Floating Action Button that stayed sticky on the screen and would open this action menu.
The control, kept the current menu open as is.
Treatment 1 closed the menu, but added the floating action button.
Treatment 2 completely removed the menu, and adding a floating action button.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. The control, which kept the current menu open, outperformed the treatments that introduced a floating action button to open the menu. This indicates that the existing menu layout may be more effective in prompting authors to take actions on their page.

2. Ultimately, the FAB did not translate into increased actions such as sharing the page, creating a post, or accessing page settings. In fact, some actions experienced a significant decrease in clicks with the FAB.

4. It is crucial to consider the impact of design changes on specific actions and metrics. While the FAB may attract attention and clicks, it may not always lead to the desired outcomes.

In future experimentation, it would be valuable to further investigate the reasons behind the decrease in key actions with the FAB implementation. Understanding why users may not be taking certain actions with the FAB could provide insights into the effectiveness of this design element and how it can be optimized.

experiments.title: How a benefit-focused headline and CTA affects email conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that providing a more detailed and benefit-focused headline and CTA for landing page visitors will increase the sign-up rate because it clearly communicates both the value of the course and the immediacy of access, making the offer more appealing and actionable.
experiments.summary:
Ended On:
MPR is offering free access to their Econ 101 online course. They wanted to find a way to increase email conversion rate so we decided to run a radical redesign on the landing page by:
Providing a more detailed and benefit-focused headline
Providing a more detailed and benefit-focused CTA above the form
Moving the “About section” below the form
Treatment A featured the first two changes while treatment B featured all three changes.
This test ran for 3 weeks and was shown to 50% of traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that providing a more detailed and benefit-focused headline and CTA on the landing page significantly increased the email acquisition rate for all traffic by 18.9% with a 96% level of confidence. By clearly communicating the value of the course and the immediacy of access, the offer became more appealing and actionable to visitors, leading to a higher conversion rate.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue focusing on the messaging and design elements that directly impact the perceived value and urgency of the offer. By prioritizing benefit-focused headlines and CTAs, we can enhance the overall user experience and drive higher conversion rates. Additionally, it would be beneficial to explore other elements of the landing page, such as the form layout, color schemes, and imagery, to further optimize the conversion process. Regular testing and iteration based on data-driven insights will be key to continuously improving the performance of the landing page and maximizing email acquisition rates.

experiments.keyLearnings: Using a logo and CTA button, instead of hyperlinked text, drove a 133% higher click-through rate. experiments.title: How an identity call to action on an exit intent impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an identity CTA “Yes, I can be one of the 3 donors” for users encountering the exit-intent during a 2-day match will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it empowers the donor with autonomy to answer the question through the CTA “Yes” and emphasizing identity rather than action “I can be one of the donors”.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/21/2025
During CaringBridge’s holiday appeal, we tested a variety of identity CTA’s throughout the campaign. In this particular experiment, we tested a CTA on the 2-day match exit intent “Yes, I can be one of the 3 donors.” On the Tribute Widget, we saw a similar autonomy CTA emphasizing that the reader was willing to give $25 significantly improved the conversion rate. In this iteration, the language focused more on donor identity ” I can be one of the donors” rather than focusing on a giving amount. We believe this autonomy and donor identity treatment will increase conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the identity CTA "Yes, I can be one of the 3 donors" for users encountering the exit-intent during a 2-day match did not achieve an increase in donor conversion rate as hypothesized. In fact, the treatment actually saw a 39.0% decrease in donations for all traffic with a high level of statistical significance.

This outcome suggests that in this particular context, emphasizing donor identity and autonomy through the CTA was not as effective in converting donors compared to other approaches. Throughout the holiday appeal, autonomy and identity language required specific parameters in order to be effective; working well in some instances but decreasing results in others. In the future, it would be worthwhile to determine the variable and discover the commonality between when autonomy succeeds and when it detracts from CaringBridge's audience.

Overall, it is important to take away from this experiment that not all hypotheses will yield positive results, and it is essential to continually test and refine strategies based on data and insights gathered from experiments.

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an affirming CTA for users viewing a tribute widget will achieve an increase in donor conversino l because it empowers the reader with the autonomy to answer a question in the affirmative, reinforcing their gift.
experiments.summary:

During CaringBridge’s 2-day match appeal, the Tribute widget copy would be updated to include mention of the match opportunity in addition to highlighting the usual $25 callout. experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:
1. Affirming CTAs can positively impact donor conversion rates: The treatment, which featured an affirming CTA "Yes, I can give $25" resulted in a significant 32.5% increase in donations compared to the control. This indicates that empowering the reader with an affirmative question can reinforce their decision to give.
2. Statistical significance: The results showed a high level of statistical confidence at 98.9%, indicating that the observed increase in conversions is likely not due to random chance.
It's worth noting that this impact was seen during the match opportunity, however, it performed flat when run as an evergreen experiment. However, it's possible that the affirmative language benefits most from a sense of urgency.

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a button CTA for email subscribers will achieve a higher engagement because it is a clear and obvious place to click and take action.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/05/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
Each month PTV promotes a featured resource. For November 2024, this is the book “A Place Called Heaven”
The main CTA in PTV featured resource emails is to make a generous gift of any amount and receive the resource as a thank you. Periodically we will do a PS where the subscriber can make a larger donation to receive a bundle that include the monthly resource.
For this email, we decided to test the PS vs a button CTA to request the book.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a button CTA for email subscribers resulted in a significant increase in engagement compared to the PS (post-script) CTA. The treatment group, which included the button CTA, saw a 74.0% increase in clicks for all traffic with a 100.0% level of statistical confidence, indicating that the button CTA was more effective in encouraging subscribers to take action.
While the control offers a PS to make a larger gift, we interestingly saw a slightly higher average gift for the treatment version $28 vs $30.
For donations we saw no change between the control or treatment.
This test is intriguing, not only that the button received more clicks, but that we did not see a decrease in revenue or gifts given.

experiments.title: How a pop-up inviting one-time donors to convert their donation to recurring impacts recurring donor conversion on donation pages
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a popup inviting one-time donors to convert their one-time donation to a recurring gift will have a more significant impact and will increase recurring giving by making the value proposition of a recurring gift more appealing and transparent to the donor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/22/2024 - 07/31/2024
This organization uses a tabbed donation form to enable donors to select either a one-time or a recurring donation. Last year, they tested adding a callout to the recurring tab to highlight the greater impact of becoming a recurring donor. This validated an increase in recurring giving and became a part of all their donation pages.
We hypothesized that we could further increase recurring giving on the page by firing a popup when someone clicks the donate button on the form. This popup would invite donors to give a smaller recurring gift to have a bigger impact than the higher one-time donation they had selected. The form would automatically ask for a gift that was 75% of the value of the one-time donation originally chosen.
We ran this experiment on instant donation pages and the donation pages of their one-time donor-focused July Campaign. Traffic was split 50/50.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The popup inviting one-time donors to convert their donation to a recurring gift significantly increased recurring donations by 129.4% for all traffic with a high level of statistical confidence (99.6%). This indicates that emphasizing the value proposition of recurring donations can effectively incentivize donors to choose ongoing giving.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue exploring ways to highlight the benefits of recurring donations and nudge donors toward choosing this option. Additionally, leveraging popups strategically to interrupt the donation process and present compelling offers can be a powerful tool in increasing donor engagement and conversion rates.

Most importantly, we found that the popup had no statistically significant impact on overall donor conversion, so we were not sacrificing one-time donations by running the popup. Because of the significantly higher retention rate and lifetime value of recurring donors, this increase in recurring giving could have significant impacts on donor revenue for this organization.

experiments.title: How pulling the Follow button out of a hidden dropdown in mobile impacts site follows
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that pulling the Follow button out of the hidden dropdown menu for users in mobile will achieve an increase in Site Follows because it makes the call-to-action more visible, eliminating the potential friction of having to search for it while also highlighting its importance .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/19/2024 - 08/20/2024
When visiting a CaringBridge user’s profile, key calls to action for the visitor are “Follow,” “Share,” and “Ways to Help.” However, in the mobile view we realized that all off these calls to action are hidden inside of a dropdown. Only after the user clicks “Show your support for [Name]” does it then reveal these prompts to follow their page, share it, or view their Ways to Help page.
We hypothesized that this was causing unnecessary friction and likely resulted in much of the audience not knowing that these actions are even present. We believe that by having this dropdown open by default in the treatment so that a user receives all of these options for the profile page, that it will increase Site Follows along with interaction to the other buttons. Having this dropdown open to reveal the Follow, Share, and Ways to Help in mobile was the only change in the treatment. The user also still had the option to minimize this dropdown and close out the options if preferred.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that making the Follow button more visible in the mobile view led to a significant increase in Site Follows, Dropdown Share button clicks, Dropdown Follow button clicks, and Dropdown Ways to Help button clicks. By pulling the Follow button out of the hidden dropdown menu and displaying it prominently, we were able to eliminate potential friction for users and highlight the importance of this call-to-action.

In future experimentation, we should consider the impact of visibility and ease of access to key calls-to-action on user engagement. By prioritizing important actions and making them more visible, we can potentially improve user interaction and achieve better results. It is important to continue testing and optimizing the user experience to ensure that the platform effectively encourages users to follow, share, and support those in need.

experiments.title: How a Sticky Follow Button Impacts Site Follows
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a sticky follow button that “pins” to the top of the page for users on a site profile will achieve an increase in site follows because it draws more attention to the button visually, but also highlights this as a primary call-to-action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/26/2024 - 08/01/2024
On CaringBridge site profiles, we wanted to re-test a sticky follow button that stayed pinned to the page when a user scrolled down. This had previously been tested in Caring Bridge 1.o, but not yet in Caring Bridge 2.0.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that implementing a sticky follow button that stays pinned to the top of the page for users on a site profile led to a significant increase in site follows. The treatment version, which included the sticky follow button resulted in a 14.7% increase in Site Follows for All Traffic with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence.

A takeaway from this experiment is more than just making an element more visually apparent, but what that communicates to the reader. Of all the actions that can be taken on a site profile, we're highlighting this is one that's important.

This strategy can be applied to other features or buttons on the site to capture users' attention and encourage them to take the desired actions. Additionally, it is important to continue testing and iterating on design elements, language choices, and user experience to optimize engagement and achieve the desired outcomes.

experiments.title: How a Facebook call to action impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the CTA to Download for Facebook traffic will achieve higher conversions because the action step is clear.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/15/2023
The Hoover Institution is the public policy think tank for Stanford University.
Hoover runs many offers through Facebook ads, therefore in an effort to increase donor conversions on content offers, we decided to test the standard Learn More CTA vs Download CTA
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the Call to Action (CTA) from "Learn More" to "Download" for Facebook traffic led to a significant decrease in donations. The treatment group, which had the "Download" CTA, saw a 56.1% decrease in donations compared to the control group.

This result suggests that the action step of downloading may not have been clear or compelling enough for the Facebook audience, leading to a decrease in conversions. It is important to note that this experiment achieved a high statistical level of confidence, indicating that the results are reliable and likely to represent the true outcome.

In future experimentation, it is crucial to consider the specific characteristics and preferences of the target audience when choosing and testing different CTAs. It may be helpful to gather feedback from the audience or conduct user research to better understand their motivations and expectations.

Additionally, it is essential to track and analyze the impact of any changes in CTAs on different metrics, such as conversions, click-through rates, and engagement. A/B testing allows for data-driven decision-making and helps identify what works best for maximizing the desired outcomes.

experiments.title: How adding a separate “Give Monthly” button on the website menu impacts donation conversions and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a “Give Monthly” button for the main website will achieve an increase in donations and overall revenue because it increases clarity for people wanting to make a gift to Care Net.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/25/2023 - 12/20/2023
Care Net has a robust recurring donor program that has grown significantly over the years. Recently, due to a variety of factors, they have seen a plateau in the conversion of recurring donors.
On their main website, they have a “Donate” button in the top right-hand corner. For this test, we wanted to see how adding a “Give Monthly” button next to the “Donate” button impacted three factors: one-time donations, recurring donations, and overall revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a "Give Monthly" button alongside the existing "Donate" button on the main website resulted in a significant increase in revenue for all traffic. The treatment group saw a 74.8% increase in revenue compared to the control group, with a high level of statistical confidence at 95.4%.

This suggests that the presence of a "Give Monthly" button provided clarity for people wanting to make a recurring donation, leading to a higher conversion rate and ultimately more revenue.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to further explore the impact of different call-to-action buttons and messaging on donation conversion. This could involve testing variations of the button design, placement, and wording to determine the most effective combination. Additionally, analyzing the data from this experiment can provide insights into the specific donor segments that responded most positively to the "Give Monthly" button, allowing for targeted marketing efforts in the future.

experiments.title: Will reducing the size of the express checkout button on the homepage donation page increase donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by reducing the size of the Express Checkout button for visitors to our homepage donation page will achieve a decrease in donor conversion friction because of the decreased size of the Express Checkout button competing for the donor’s attention while completing the donation process. because of the decreased size of the Express Checkout button competing for the donor’s attention while completing the donation process. because of the decreased size of the Express Checkout button competing for the donor’s attention while completing the donation process. because of the decreased size of the Express Checkout button competing for the donor’s attention while completing the donation process.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/14/2024 - 03/05/2024
The team have been doing some testing on the Express Checkout opportunity on various groupings of their giving pages. The team had just finished a test on removing Express Checkout on the main homepage donation page and the findings were very flat, but we wondered what would happen if we reduced the size of the buttons instead.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment on reducing the size of the Express Checkout button on the homepage donation page for Focus on the Family include:

1. **Increased Transactions:** The treatment group experienced a significant 21.3% increase in Transactions for All Traffic compared to the control group. This indicates that the smaller size of the Express Checkout button helped reduce conversion friction and encourage more visitors to complete their donations.

2. **Significant Confidence Level:** The experiment achieved a high statistical level of confidence at 95.7%, meaning that the results are reliable and not likely due to random chance.

3. **Significant Impact on One-Time Transactions:** The treatment group also saw a remarkable 229% increase in One-Time Transactions with a 96% confidence level. This suggests that the smaller size of the button not only increased overall transactions but also influenced more one-time donations.

Based on these findings, future experimentation for Focus on the Family's donation pages could consider implementing smaller-sized Express Checkout buttons as a strategy to improve conversion rates and encourage more donations. Further testing could delve into the impact of button size on recurring donations, donor retention, and overall user experience to continuously optimize the donation process and enhance fundraising efforts.

experiments.title: How a premium offer chat widget CTA on podcast pages impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a chat message widget to the podcast pages for visitors of FamilyLife Today® will create a more engaging and persuasive experience due to the interactive nature of the widget, therefore increasing donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/02/2024 - 07/31/2024
FamilyLife helps families bridge the gap between their intentions and reality, fostering closer relationships with God, each other, and their communities. Their FamilyLife Podcast Network, including the FamilyLife Today® podcast, offers engaging conversations to help families grow together with Jesus and strengthen their most important relationships.
Traditionally, FamilyLife has relied on an end-of-article “dear listener” message to promote their campaigns and offer a premium item to donors. We hypothesized that adding a chat message widget to the podcast pages for listeners and visitors of FamilyLife Today® will increase donor conversion rates because the interactive and personal nature of the widget creates a more engaging and persuasive experience through social presence and personal connection.
For this test, we selected their most popular premium offer, the book “Seeing Beautiful Again.” The Control was the traditional “dear listener” message, while the Treatment was a chat message widget. Traffic was split evenly between the two versions, with identical language used in both. The Control message appeared at the end of the article, while the widget popped up in the bottom right corner and expanded when clicked. The Treatment also included images of the FamilyLife Today podcast hosts, Dave and Ann, to simulate a text messaging experience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 29 days, we found that the chat message widget outperformed the traditional "dear listener" approach, achieving a conversion rate of 0.37% compared to 0.13% for the control. This represents a 187.9% increase in conversions with a high level of confidence at 98.8%.The results demonstrate that the interactive nature of the widget significantly enhanced donor engagement and conversion rates.
For future experimentation, we plan to test this approach during the Fiscal Year End (FYE) Campaign using a premium book offer, "Unoffendable" by Brant Hansen. We also aim to explore different design variations of the chat widget, evaluate other premium offers for their effectiveness with interactive features, and assess the widget’s performance across various sections of the website.
Overall, the key learnings from this experiment emphasize the importance of personalization, interactivity, and social presence in driving donor conversions. By leveraging these insights in future experimentation, FamilyLife can continue to optimize its donation strategies and create more impactful and engaging experiences for its audience.

experiments.title: How congruency in CTA impacted donor conversion on Facebook
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making the CTA consistent across an ad for Facebook viewers will achieve a higher conversion rate because this is congruency in the ask.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/02/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
Each month PTV promotes a different book as an incentive for donors to donate. A main channel for PTV in donor acquisition is Facebook. For July 2024, PTV’s featured item is Choose Your Attitude, Change Your Life.
In an effort to increase donor conversion we decided to test using the same CTA in the body, image and headline of the ad.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that maintaining consistency in the call-to-action (CTA) across an ad for Facebook viewers can significantly increase conversion rates. In this case, using the same CTA in the body, image, and headline of the ad resulted in a 74.8% increase in donations for all traffic, with a statistical confidence level of 97.1%.

In future experimentation, it is important to remember the impact of congruency in the ask. By consistently delivering the same CTA message throughout an ad, viewers are more likely to respond positively and take the desired action. This can lead to higher conversion rates and ultimately, more successful donation campaigns.

Moving forward, we should continue to test and optimize our ad strategies, keeping in mind the importance of message consistency and congruency in driving viewer engagement and conversions. By leveraging this learning in future experiments, we can further refine our approach and continue to improve our donor acquisition efforts for Pathway to Victory.

experiments.title: How additional body copy and a brighter button color affects email conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that additional copy and a brighter button color for eBook downloaders will achieve an increase in email conversion rate because it will decrease friction in the form submission process.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/12/2024 - 06/25/2024
In an effort to increase the email conversion rate on a landing page for a border crisis eBook offer, The Heritage Foundation wanted to test whether adding body copy explaining what the eBook will help the reader learn, as well as changing the form’s button color, would lead to an increase in email conversion rate.
The control page had three paragraphs of copy and a blue form submit button, and the treatment page had five paragraphs of copy including a more in-depth explanation of the value the reader will receive and an orange button.
This split URL test ran for 13 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment with shorter copy and a brighter button color for the eBook offer did not lead to an increase in email conversion rate. In fact, the treatment resulted in a 6.5% decrease in Email Acquired for All Traffic compared to the control. With a statistical confidence level of 96.0%, it can be concluded that the control page performed better in terms of email acquisition.

The

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully evaluate the changes being made to the landing page. While the hypothesis suggested that shorter copy and a brighter button color would decrease friction in the form submission process and increase conversions, the results showed otherwise. It is essential to test hypotheses thoroughly and be open to unexpected outcomes.

Moving forward, it may be beneficial to further investigate the factors that influenced the lower email conversion rate in the treatment. This could involve conducting surveys or collecting feedback from users to understand their preferences and behaviors. Additionally, conducting more A/B tests with different variations can help in identifying the most effective elements for improving email acquisition on the landing page.

Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of data-driven decision-making and the need to carefully analyze results to inform future experimentation strategies.

experiments.title: How leveraging a small image on a donate button to build affinity and alignment affects donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a small American flag to build affinity for potential donors to The Daily Signal will achieve an increase in conversion rate because the flag image will help align their motivation with the intended action.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/09/2024
The Daily Signal is The Heritage Foundation’s multimedia news platform. On their home page, and most other pages, the only call-to-action to donate to support the organization is located at the bottom in the footer section among many other site links.
They’ve previously tested adding a donate button to the main menu of the homepage here: https://app.winstonknows.com/e/K1qG
That experiment resulted in a large increase in traffic to the donation page because we provided an easier, more prominent path to the donation page.
The team had noticed an eCommerce site that, when selling merchandise for a particular team, put that team’s logo on the “add to cart” page to leverage the natural affinity between the buyer and the team.
To continue testing the donate button to increase traffic to the donation page, they decided to test a similar concept of adding an American Flag icon to the button.
They believed the flag image would help align a donor’s motivation with their intended action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a small American flag to the donate button on The Daily Signal's website significantly increased the number of clicks to the donation page. The treatment group, which included the flag icon, saw a 53.6% increase in clicks for all traffic with a 99.9% level of confidence.

Additionally, the treatment button saw a 22.8% lift in transactions with a 59.7% level of confidence. Although these results are not valid, they do indicate that overall more traffic to the donation page will increase the percentage of donations made.

Overall, this experiment suggests that incorporating patriotic or visually appealing elements can help build affinity with potential donors and encourage them to take action. In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the use of imagery or design elements that align with donors' motivations to see if similar increases in conversion rates can be achieved.

Additionally, it is important to consider the placement of calls-to-action on the website. The success of this experiment may also indicate that strategically placing donation buttons in prominent locations on the site can lead to higher engagement and donation rates.
