experiments.title: How does a button on a slide-out impact email acquisitions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We hypothesize that incorporating the form directly into the slideout will streamline the process, reducing friction and the number of steps required, potentially resulting in a higher number of sign-ups. Conversely, a slideout without a form may decrease the perceived commitment, thereby encouraging more sign-ups after users have gained additional information on the landing page
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/22/2024
In an effort to enhance email acquisitions for a content offer, an experiment was conducted for WUNC, a non-profit news radio station. This experiment was tested on the homepage of their website to determine whether prospects are more likely to sign up for an offer directly within a slideout or by being redirected to a landing page. The aim is to gain insights into the most effective strategy for maximizing email acquisitions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
1. The treatment, which included a link to direct users to a separate landing page, resulted in a 56.5% increase in Emails Acquired for All Traffic compared to an embedded sign-up form.
2. By requiring users to navigate to a separate landing page to provide their information, the treatment potentially decreased the perceived commitment, leading to more sign-ups. This suggests that after users have gained additional information on the landing page, they are more likely to sign up for a content offer
3. The results of the experiment showed a 100.0% statistical level of confidence, indicating that the increase in email acquisitions was not due to chance.
The key learnings from this experiment can guide decision-making in future experiments. It is recommended to continue testing different sign-up processes to further optimize email acquisitions. Additionally, user convenience and reducing perceived commitment can be important factors to consider when designing and implementing sign-up forms on websites.

experiments.title: How adding a donate button to a news platform site’s header affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donate button to the top header for site visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because more people will click to view the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/02/2024 - 04/10/2024
The Daily Signal is The Heritage Foundation’s multimedia news platform. On their home page, and most other pages, the only call-to-action to donate to support the organization is located at the bottom in the footer section among many other site links.
In an effort to increase the amount of traffic to the donation page from the home page, we previously tested adding a more prominently displayed donate button to the category menu at the top of the page on the desktop version, and in the navigation menu of the mobile version. This experiment resulted in a 42.8% increase in donations compared to the control. However, with a 53.2% level of confidence, these results can’t be relied upon to predict a valid outcome.
In this attempt to increase traffic to the donation page, we wanted to test whether adding the donate button to a more notable location at the very top of the page would lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. We also wanted to test whether adding a gift box icon to the donate button in order to match the “branded” style of the “Search” and “Menu” buttons next to it would lead to an increase in conversion rate.
The regular donate button and “branded” donate button appeared in the top header sitewide for both the desktop and mobile experiences. 50% of traffic saw the control with no button, and the two treatments received 25% of traffic each.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a donate button to the top header for site visitors led to a significant increase in traffic to the donation page. The first treatment saw a significant 2,694% increase in clicks to the donation page compared to the control group with no button, while the second treatment saw an even higher 2,866.2% increase in clicks at a 99.9% level of confidence. This result suggests that making the donation button more prominent and easily accessible can greatly impact the amount of traffic led to the donation page.
However, in terms of donations, the first treatment lead to a 9.3% decrease in donor conversion rate (at an invalid 14.5% level of confidence), and the second treatment led to a near-valid 64% decrease in donor conversion rate compared to the control.
While invalid, these results may suggest that by more prominently displaying the donate button and increasing traffic to the donation page, people who do click on the button may be less motivated to give than someone in the control experience who seeks out the donation page in order to support the organization.
Future experimentation is needed to understand what motivates visitors of The Daily Signal to make a gift to support their work. Experiment ideas include testing alternative copy on the donation page to motivate more viewers of the site to give, and testing an alternate icon that aligns more strongly with the organization's mission.

experiments.title: How using a plain image in a content offer Facebook ad impacted clicks compared to a designed image using an overlaid CTA
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a plain image in a content offer Facebook ad will increase clicks by making the ad appear more authentic because it looks like a post from another Facebook user
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/12/2024 - 03/20/2024
Focus on the Family uses free video series content offers to acquire new subscribers and donors. For one of their best-performing campaigns, they hypothesized that they could increase engagement by making the ad look like a post from another user. Users who share content don’t create “ads” with a custom photo with overlaid call to action text. Instead, they often post a plain image with some thoughts about it.
We created an ad treatment with no text or CTA overlaying the image. We used the ad copy itself to promote the free video series to users and invite them to sign up. We ran this against our control ad images that featured overlaid CTAs.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using an organic-looking Facebook ad design, which resembles a post from another user, can increase clicks significantly compared to traditional ad designs with overlaid call-to-action text. This approach creates a more authentic and engaging experience for the audience, leading to higher engagement with the ad content.
Moving forward, we should leverage this learning in future experimentation by incorporating more organic-looking ad designs in our campaigns. By focusing on creating content that mimics user-generated posts rather than traditional ad formats, we can build a stronger connection with our audience and drive higher click-through rates. Additionally, we should continue to test and optimize different creative approaches to further enhance our advertising strategies and maximize results.
For a future offer, we will test no ad copy and instead use an image with a clear, short call to action on a plain-colored background.
Impact on other metrics:
The treatment decreased the gross cost per click by $0.48 ($1.42 vs $190)There was no statistically significant difference in emails acquired during the experiment.

experiments.title: How placing an additional call to action higher in the copy of a fundraising email for members affects the click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Adding another call to action sooner in a fundraising email for current members will increase the click-through and conversion rate because they already support this organization and understand how their gift will be beneficial.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/28/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign. The email asked members to give, in return, they would receive a Members Card. The email also announces a matching gift opportunity. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if members would click if a call to action was placed earlier in the body of the email. Since members are familiar with the mission, values, and issues of the Heritage Foundation they wouldn’t need as much information to make an additional donation.
The treatment included a one-sentence call-to-action after the second paragraph of the email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:

1. Adding a call to action sooner in a fundraising email for current members can significantly increase click-through and conversion rates.

2. Providing a clear and concise call to action early in the email can effectively prompt members to take action.

3. Current members of The Heritage Foundation are more likely to respond to donation requests since they already understand the organization's mission and values.

Note: The treatment email also received more conversations, though not at a significant statistical difference.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing the placement and wording of calls to action in emails to optimize engagement and donation rates. Utilizing the knowledge that current members are more likely to donate, future emails can be structured to emphasize the impact of their contributions and make it easy for them to support the organization. Additionally, incorporating matching gift opportunities and other incentives can further motivate members to donate.

experiments.title: How making the Follow Site button “sticky” so that it stays onscreen impacts site follows
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing visibility of the Follow button by making ti sticky for users on a CaringBridge profile will achieve an increase in site follows because it not only means they’re more likely to see it, but it highlights this feature as important.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/12/2024 - 02/13/2024
For CaringBridge, site follows on a user profile is an important metric. It not only keeps friends and family better connected, it means they’re more likely to see the user’s posts and encounter key asks to support the site. In a previous iteration, we believed that highlighting this Follow button in the top right-hand corner with a bright orange color would make it stand out and more. This resulted in a huge increase in follows. For this iteration of the test, we hypothesized that taking this a step further and keeping the Follow Site button “sticky” so that when a user scrolls down the profile it stays in view on the right side-bar, would also increase visibility and site follows as a result.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:

Making the Follow button sticky on a user's profile led to a significant increase in site followers. The treatment group experienced a 42.8% increase in Site Follows for All Traffic with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence, indicating a clear win for the treatment.

From this experiment, we learned the importance of increasing the visibility of key features such as the Follow button to drive user engagement. This should prompt future ideas about key metrics and how if we want a user to take them, we should make it visible and easy to do so. Exploring other potential sticky elements on the site that could enhance user experience and drive key metrics.

experiments.title: How replacing a button with a link impacts the click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that replacing button cta with raw link cta for mass communications emails will achieve a higher level of clicks because it will make the email feel more personal.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/23/2024
Moody historically has used buttons and very heavily designed emails. We wanted to test if we could increase engagement with their emails by making them look very personal and not like a marketing email. In the first series of this design of experiments, we tested plain text vs. HTML with the plain text resulting in a massive lift in engagement.
For this experiment, we wanted to see if we could continue to make the email more personal by switching out the buttons with raw links.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the test did result in a valid decrease of clicks, I think we need to take this with a grain of salt.

This email test was meant to be the second test in a series of making the emails feel like a 1-on-1 communication instead of a mass marketing email. The first test was plain text vs. HTML, in which the plain text won with a 99.9% confidence level. This test was intended to be on a plain text template.

I believe we see a sharp decline in this specific format because a link is harder to see in the HTML version.

While this is still a valuable learning to see how the audience responds to this, I believe it will need to be retested to see if it produces the same result as a personal email.

experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/07/2023 - 11/09/2023
In an effort to increase site follows, we decided to create a treatment that would draw more attention to it. The current button seemed to blend in with the design and might get lost when someone is scrolling through.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Making the "Follow Site" button orange increased its visibility and led to a 6.1% increase in site follows. This result was statistically significant with a 99.9% confidence level.

These findings indicate that the hypothesis was correct and that changing the color of the button to orange successfully drew more attention to it, resulting in higher engagement and more follows.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to consider the following:

Continually test different design elements to optimize user engagement. Color choice may have drawn more attention to it but there is more testing that can be done around the value proposition copy surrounding the button.

experiments.title: How adding a second link impacted conversions for donors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a second link for email subscribers will achieve a higher conversion rate because there are more opportunities to click .
experiments.summary:

Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
This is an experiment we ran for PTV a couple of years ago, but we wanted to test again to see if the results still held.
We tested a second link in the email to see if it would increase engagement and down funnel donations.

The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

3 Of those that received the treatment, only 30% clicked the first link while 70% clicked the second link.

4 Donations saw a 14% increase on the treatment but only a 74% level of confidence.

 

Based on these results, the addition of a second link did not increase engagement.

experiments.title: How adding a separate “Give Monthly” button on the website menu impacts donation conversions and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a “Give Monthly” button for the main website will achieve an increase in donations and overall revenue because it increases clarity for people wanting to make a gift to Care Net.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/25/2023 - 12/20/2023
Care Net has a robust recurring donor program that has grown significantly over the years. Recently, due to a variety of factors, they have seen a plateau in the conversion of recurring donors.
On their main website, they have a “Donate” button in the top right-hand corner. For this test, we wanted to see how adding a “Give Monthly” button next to the “Donate” button impacted three factors: one-time donations, recurring donations, and overall revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a "Give Monthly" button alongside the existing "Donate" button on the main website resulted in a significant increase in revenue for all traffic. The treatment group saw a 74.8% increase in revenue compared to the control group, with a high level of statistical confidence at 95.4%.

This suggests that the presence of a "Give Monthly" button provided clarity for people wanting to make a recurring donation, leading to a higher conversion rate and ultimately more revenue.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to further explore the impact of different call-to-action buttons and messaging on donation conversion. This could involve testing variations of the button design, placement, and wording to determine the most effective combination. Additionally, analyzing the data from this experiment can provide insights into the specific donor segments that responded most positively to the "Give Monthly" button, allowing for targeted marketing efforts in the future.

experiments.title: How an in-line appeal that becomes a sticky bar with a small donation CTA and opt-in for an email reminder impacts donor conversion during a Calendar Year-End Campaign

rows.name: Dear reader urgency with Scrolling Sticky Bar

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an in-line appeal that becomes a sticky bar reminding visitors of the value they’ve received from this organization’s articles will increase donor conversion by increasing the appeal and exclusivity of the offer
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/13/2022 - 12/25/2022
Historically, this organization used a combination of sticky bars and slide-outs with messaging around the urgent need of helping youth experiencing homelessness or housing insecurity  and have tied this urgency back to either a donor goal or a revenue goal. During their calendar year-end campaign, they often use a sticky bar with a daily donor goal countdown to convert visitors to their article pages into donors.
They wanted to test this against a design inspired by one of Wikipedia’s tests where there is an in-line appeal that becomes a sticky bar when scrolling. Instead of emphasizing the matching challenge in effect and the donor goal, the in-line appeal would ask the reader to consider the value they’ve received during the past year from their articles and tell them that less than 1% of readers donate to make these articles possible. Once the reader scrolled to continue reading the article, they would be prompted with the following, “If we have given you $5 worth of truth about housing insecurity this year, take a minute to donate.” They would then be asked for a gift of as little as $5 to “keep these exclusive articles possible to provide guidance to transform lives.”
If the visitor clicked the button to give, they would go to the donation page with the amount they selected. They could also click, “maybe later” which loaded an embedded HubSpot form that allowed them to opt-in for an email reminder to give. Those who opted in would receive an email reminder before the end of the campaign to make their gift.
Our hypothesis was that the conversational and value-driven copy on the in-line design and sticky bar, coupled with the option to opt-in for an email reminder would increase donor conversion by increasing the appeal and exclusivity of the offer. We would also be tapping into the donor’s desire to not lose access to these articles by highlighting how few people give to make them possible.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new messaging and design crushed the control sticky bar with a daily donor goal. What is remarkable is that the avg gift was $29 despite the fact that the CTA was to make a $5 gift. This showed that readers felt that they were receiving much more value from the articles and content and wanted to make sure that it would still be available to them in the new year.

It's also worth noting that the dear reader/slide-up treatment brought in a $10,000 gift. We've pulled this out in the recording of this experiment so as not to skew the average gift or the revenue to give a more accurate picture of the overall results.

111 people opted in to receive follow-up emails to remind them about giving opportunities to this organization. We will be monitoring this segment to see downstream conversion as well as sending specific appeals to them reminding them that they asked to know more about ways to give at a later date.

We will continue to refine the messaging of this bar for future urgent campaigns as well as test into leveraging statistics of how few visitors/pledge signers/readers, etc donate to see if this messaging increases donor conversion on acquisition offer donation pages as well.

We also want to test the opening line, "we humbly ask you, don't scroll away" in other places to see if that conversational approach can improve conversion by keeping people on the page longer. For example, on an instant donation page after someone has requested an eBook or signed a pledge. Will that headline keep someone on the page long enough to read the donation appeal?

experiments.title: How strengthening the value proposition of a homepage donation CTA impacts clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding increased clarity to the value proposition language around the homepage CTA will achieve an increase in clicks to the donation page by providing users with a better understanding of why they should make a gift and what their gift will actually do.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/17/2023
CBN is a Christian news, media, and distribution organization with a mission to preach the Gospel and demonstrate God’s love for the people of the world. The homepage of their website features an image with a Call-to-Action (CTA) that encourages readers to make a gift to CBN and support their work to share Christ’s love with the world.
For this test, we wanted to test into the impact of enhancing the value proposition language around this CTA by adding additional clarity, appeal, and credibility. We ran this test with the following variants:
Control: This variant featured a short and simple headline that said “Sharing the Love of Christ Around the World” above the “GIVE NOW” button
Treatment #1: This variant was designed to give readers a better understanding of how CBN shares Christ’s love by explaining that the reader’s gift to CBN will provide humanitarian aid and the Gospel to people. The headline in this variant said “Your gift right now will reach people with humanitarian aid and the Gospel of Jesus”
Treatment #2: Like Treatment #1, this variant added clarity to the headline, but it also added body copy that explained the problem more, starting with “Millions of people still lack access to the Gospel, hot food, and clean water but you can help change that.” It also reassured readers that 100% of their donation would support CBN’s mission. The final change made to this variant was that it would not display the hero image to mobile users. Instead, the headline and CTA would be displayed on a simple colored background to make it easier to read on mobile devices.
Our hypothesis is that the additional clarity provided in Treatment #1 and Treatment #2 would cause more users to click the “GIVE NOW” button and go to the donation page. We theorized that Treatment #2 would perform the best in this experiment because it added the most clarity to the value proposition language and was designed to make that language easier to read for mobile users.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that enhancing the value proposition language around the Call-to-Action (CTA) on the homepage had a significant impact on user engagement. The treatment variant, which added clarity, appeal, and credibility to the value proposition, resulted in a 111.3% increase in clicks to the donation page.

These findings suggest that users responded positively to the added information about how their gift will be used to provide humanitarian aid and share the Gospel. Additionally, the inclusion of body copy that highlighted the problem and reassured readers about the use of their donation likely increased trust and motivation to give.

In preparing for this experiment, one concern we had was that the length of variation two could create too much friction and that web visitors would instead prefer the shorter copy of version 1. The results however found the opposite. The longer copy provided even more details about the impact of a donor's gift, leading to higher donor conversion. While running this experiment, we began testing these value propositions on the donation page as well. The visitors in those experiments were excluded from this one to keep the tests clean, but we will use those tests to further refine the value proposition provided for donating to CBN.

We attempted to dig into donor conversion on the main donation page, but had an issue with the UTMs being stripped on the traffic coming to the page from this CTA.

experiments.title: How emphasizing the “give button” in the main navigation affects donor conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding emphasis to a give button in the main navigation for all visitors to the site will achieve a greater number of donor conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/21/2022 - 07/16/2022
ABWE (Association of Baptists for World Evangelism) is a global family of ministries that exists to fulfill the Great Commission by multiplying leaders, churches, and missions movements among every people. ABWE wanted to test if adding more emphasis to a “give button” on the navigation would lead to a greater number of donor conversions. For this experiment, we tested adding contrast to the give button on the main navigation (the treatment). For the control we left the give button in it’s natural state.
experiments.keyLearnings:
As hypothesized, the treatment saw an increase in donor conversions of nearly 24%. The experiment received both enough sample size (32k) and a high enough level of confidence (99%) to validate. In order for experiments to be statically significant, both of these factors must be present. In this case, both elements were true, and the treatment won. Next steps in this experiment will be to run the treatment as a control, and test the placement of the emphasized give button.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Desktop visitors had a 19.9% increase in donations with a 93% level of confidence.
 	Mobile visitors had a 31.6% increase in donations with a 96% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How an in-line appeal that becomes a sticky bar with a small donation CTA impacts donor conversion during a Fiscal Year End Campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an in-line appeal that becomes a sticky bar notifying visitors of the threat of the federal abortion hotline for Care Net’s Pregnancy Decision Line will increase donor conversion by increasing the appeal and fear of loss.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/19/2023 - 06/30/2023
Care Net is an organization that provides support and resources to women and men considering abortion.
Historically, they’ve used a dear reader at the bottom of article pages with messaging around the urgent need in the pro-life space and have tied this urgency back to either a donor goal or a revenue goal. During their fiscal year-end campaign, they often use a sticky bar with a daily donor goal countdown to convert visitors to their article pages into donors.
They wanted to test this against a design inspired by one of Wikipedia’s tests where there is an in-line appeal that becomes a sticky bar when scrolling. The control and the treatment both had the same copy, but the placements were different: the control being at the end of the article and the treatment being at the beginning. On the treatment, once the reader scrolled to continue reading the article, the dear reader would then turn into a sticky bar with the same copy.
Our hypothesis was that the conversational and value-driven copy on the in-line design and sticky bar would increase donor conversion by increasing the appeal and exclusivity of the offer. We would also be tapping into the donor’s desire to not have a federal, taxpayer-funded hotline.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:
1. The treatment, which consisted of an in-line appeal that became a sticky bar with the same copy when scrolling, performed significantly worse than the control. It resulted in a 70.3% decrease in donations for all traffic, indicating that this design approach did not effectively convert visitors into donors.
2. Different visitor segments showed even larger decreases in donations. Returning visitors had an 828% decrease. These significant drops in donation rates suggest that the in-line appeal with the fear of loss did not resonate well with these specific segments.
Based on these findings, it is clear that the Wikipedia "before article appeal" approach used in the treatment did not effectively appeal to visitors to become donors. The results suggest that a different messaging strategy should be tested in future experimentation.
Instead of relying solely on the placement, other emotional appeals or value-driven messaging could be considered. For example, highlighting how many more people can be reached through the Care Net Pregnancy Decision Line might be a more compelling appeal for potential donors, or showcasing how this new federal hotline could impact people being able to find life-affirming care.
Additionally, it may be worth exploring different placements for the appeal. While the treatment placed the messaging at the beginning of the article, future experiments could test different placements such as the end of the article, in a sidebar, or in a pop-up overlay.

				× 70.3% decrease in conversion rate
				× 172.2% increase in average gift
									19.2% decrease in revenue

experiments.title: How using “Learn More” as a CTA impacted FB clicks on a direct ask
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the level of commitment in the CTA language for Facebook ad viewers will achieve an increase in clicks and donation page views because the subtle ask to “learn more” introduces less friction than the direct ask to “donate now”.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/29/2023
We were running a direct ask campaign on Facebook using the “Donate Now” button as the CTA and wondered if using a less direct CTA would help drive more donation page visitors. We kept all other elements of the ad the same and changed the button copy to “Learn More”. Our hypothesis was the “easier” option would create less friction by reducing the anxiety in the minds of the visitors of potentially making the full commitment without having enough information to make a decision first. If we could get them to give a “micro-yes” to learning more about the program we were asking them to support, we believed we could bring more visitors to the donation page from the ad.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the CTA from "Donate Now" to "Learn More" resulted in a significant increase in clicks and donation page views. The treatment version, which had the "Learn More" CTA, saw a 48.8% increase in clicks for all traffic compared to the control version with 97.7% level of confidence so the experiment results are valid. We also saw a 30% decrease in cost per click on the "Learn More" version.
This suggests that the direct ask of "Donate Now" may have created friction and anxiety for the viewers, causing them to hesitate or be reluctant to click on the ad. By offering a lower level of commitment with the "Learn More" CTA, viewers may have felt more comfortable and willing to engage with the ad.
When reviewing results, we also noted that one additional reason the treatment could have outperformed the control is that Facebook, by default, includes a subheader message when the "Donate Now" button is selected that says "Not affiliated with Meta" which could also be introducing more friction for visitors and introducing anxiety if the message appears suspicious or untrustworthy. This default subheader also replaces the available room to display the full headline so the treatment version has the benefit of showing the full headline of "Help equip future conservative leaders" which offers more clarity than the cut off version "Help equip future..."

experiments.title: How using “Learn More” as a CTA on a direct ask ad impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the level of commitment in the CTA language for Facebook ad viewers will achieve an increase in clicks and donation page views because the subtle ask to “learn more” introduces less friction than the direct ask to “donate now”.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/29/2023
We were running a direct ask campaign on Facebook using the “Donate Now” button as the CTA and wondered if using a less direct CTA would help drive more donation page visitors. We kept all other elements of the ad the same and changed the button copy to “Learn More”. Our hypothesis was the “easier” option would create less friction by reducing the anxiety in the minds of the visitors of potentially making the full commitment without having enough information to make a decision first. If we could get them to give a “micro-yes” to learning more about the program we were asking them to support, we believed we could bring more visitors to the donation page from the ad.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the CTA language from "Donate Now" to "Learn More" resulted in a significant increase in clicks and donation page views. The treatment version, with the "Learn More" CTA, saw a 67.7% increase in clicks for all traffic compared to the control version at 99.4% level of confidence so the results are valid. We also saw a 60% decrease in cost per click on the "Learn More" version.
This suggests that the subtle ask to "learn more" introduces less friction and reduces anxiety in the minds of the viewers. By giving potential donors an opportunity to gather more information before making a commitment, they are more likely to engage with the ad and visit the donation page.
To apply this learning in future experimentation, it is recommended to continue using the "Learn More" CTA instead of the direct ask of "Donate Now". This approach can help increase engagement and maximize the number of visitors to the donation page. It is also worth exploring other ways to reduce friction and anxiety, such as providing clear and concise information about the program and its impact in the ad copy itself, or utilizing storytelling techniques to create emotional connections with the viewers.

experiments.title: How using “Learn More” as a CTA impacted FB clicks on a direct ask
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the level of commitment in the CTA language for Facebook ad viewers will achieve an increase in clicks and donation page views because the subtle ask to “learn more” introduces less friction than the direct ask to “donate now”.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/29/2023
We were running a direct ask campaign on Facebook using the “Donate Now” button as the CTA and wondered if using a less direct CTA would help drive more donation page visitors. We kept all other elements of the ad the same and changed the button copy to “Learn More”. Our hypothesis was the “easier” option would create less friction by reducing the anxiety in the minds of the visitors of potentially making the full commitment without having enough information to make a decision first. If we could get them to give a “micro-yes” to learning more about the program we were asking them to support, we believed we could bring more visitors to the donation page from the ad.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the CTA from "Donate Now" to "Learn More" resulted in a significant increase in clicks and donation page views. The treatment version, which had the "Learn More" CTA, saw a 48.8% increase in clicks for all traffic compared to the control version with 97.7% level of confidence so the experiment results are valid.

This suggests that the direct ask of "Donate Now" may have created friction and anxiety for the viewers, causing them to hesitate or be reluctant to click on the ad. By offering a lower level of commitment with the "Learn More" CTA, viewers may have felt more comfortable and willing to engage with the ad.

When reviewing results, we also noted that one additional reason the treatment could have outperformed the control is that Facebook, by default, includes a subheader message when the "Donate Now" button is selected that says "Not affiliated with Meta" which could also be introducing more friction for visitors and introducing anxiety if the message appears suspicious or untrustworthy. This default subheader also replaces the available room to display the full headline so the treatment version has the benefit of showing the full headline of "Help equip future conservative leaders" which offers more clarity than the cut off version "Help equip future..."

experiments.title: How clarifying the call-to-action affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing clarity of the call to action while adding language to increase the “fear of loss” increase donor conversion rate over the existing treatment emphasizing the more general ask?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/20/2014 - 11/25/2014
The Tribute widget drive the majority of Caringbridge’s organic site revenue. It is small advertisement on a patient’s journal page that asks the visitor to make a tribute gift. With the volume of Caringbridge’s site traffic, improving the conversion rate of this widget would have an exponential impact.
A treatment was developed with the goal of clarifying the purpose of the gift. The idea was that if we can have visitors better understand the purpose of the gift, they will be more likely to make a gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The resulting treatment made multiple impacts along the path to conversion. By itself, the new widget language increased the number of clicks by 19.3%.

Not only that but this increased traffic was more qualified than the visitors that the control would drive since they had a better understanding of the purpose of their gift. As a result, the visitors to the subsequent page had a 52% increase in conversion and a 54% decrease in refunds.

The cumulative effect of all of these lifts resulted in an 85.9% increase in donor conversion.

experiments.title: How reducing the perceived investment of a call to action impacts account creation rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the call to action to “create a page” for ebook downloaders will achieve an increase in site creation rate because we are reducing the potential anxiety someone experiences when being asked to create a site. because  we are reducing the potential anxiety someone experiences when being asked to create a site.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/06/2023 - 04/22/2023
CaringBridge has been running ebook offers where they prompt the ebook downloader to create an account with their organization. They have previously used the call to action, “Create a site” to communicate the breadth of what CaringBridge offers when you create an account to share yours our your loved ones health journey. Our hypothesis was that the language to “create a site” may have the perception that they’re about to make a large commitment. Creating a site may sound tedious, laborious, or intimidating. We wanted to reduce any anxiety about the process by simply changing the word “site” to “page.” This phrase is still accurate but reduces the perceived investment and commitment they’re about to make.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the call to action from “create a site” to “create a page” had a significant impact in increasing the website creation rate. The treatment group saw a valid 151.4% increase in website creation for all traffic, with a statistical level of confidence of 99.0%.
These results indicate that asking someone to "create a page" reduces any potential anxiety they may have had in taking action. Creating page sounds like it's less time consuming and manageable.
Our proposed next experiment is using the world "journal" instead of "page." We believe that the word "journal" lowers the barrier to entry even further.

experiments.title: How reducing the number of calls to action impacted clicks in a cultivation message
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that presenting a single call to action for cultivation message readers will achieve an increase in engagement because the isolated option will reduce potential friction caused by decision fatigue.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/01/2023
When preparing a cultivation message around a specific topic of interest, we found the organization offered a lot of relevant resources. The control copy gave the reader a full menu to select from. They could listen to a podcast, read a post, or explore stories from people personally impacted by the program. We wondered if having this many choices could result in decision fatigue and cause the reader to not engage with ANY of the options. We developed a treatment to test this theory and isolated the choice to read a single article. We believed the treatment would help the reader have clarity by establishing a clear call to action. We split the audience 50/50 and measured the impact on clicks.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we discovered through this experiment was the treatment resulted in a 95.8% increase in clicks with a 100% statistical level of confidence. The key learning from this experiment is that presenting a single call to action for cultivation message readers can significantly increase engagement by reducing decision fatigue. This means that the control copy that offered a full menu of options may have overwhelmed readers and resulted in lost engagement opportunities. In future experimentation, it is recommended to simplify the call to action and reduce options to reduce the risk of decision fatigue and improve engagement rates.

experiments.title: How a more direct call to action in a Facebook ad impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that concisely communicating the call to action for Facebook ad viewers will achieve an increase in clickthrough rate because it kicks off the cognitive momentum to keep things congruent with the landing page copy.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/02/2023
In a previous test on this offer, we tested short copy vs. longer copy and found that the longer copy version motivated more visitors to click the ad. In this next iteration, we wanted to test back into using shorter copy but installing the learning from the previous experiment which was that visitors needed clarity about the pledge they would be signing before they would commit to taking the next step. So this time, we kept the outline of the four pillars they would be agreeing to in the pledge in the copy, but removed all the extra lead-in copy. The treatment also introduced checkbox emojis to indicate each statement of the pledge they would be agreeing with if they signed.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that concise communication of the call to action in Facebook ads significantly increases clickthrough rates. By removing unnecessary lead-in copy and emphasizing the four pillars of the pledge with checkbox emojis, the treatment achieved a 15.2% increase in clicks for all traffic with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence. This suggests that visitors respond well to clear, concise messaging that ensures congruence with the landing page. In future experiments, it would be beneficial to continue testing variations of concise messaging and strong calls to action that align with the landing page content.

experiments.title: How a second donate button impacted donor conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a second donate button for web visitors will achieve higher donor conversion rate because there is clarity in the ask.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/14/2023 - 03/02/2023
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
In an effort to increase donor conversion from web traffic, we decided to test adding a second donate button that directs to the Avila Army (monthly donation effort) in addition to the regular donate button.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a second button to the Avila Foundation website that directs viewers to the Avila Army (monthly donation effort) can be successful at increasing donor conversion rate. After running an A/B test, we saw dramatic increases in Donations for All Traffic, Revenue, Email Visitors, Desktop Visitors, Returning Visitors, and 55-64 Visitors as a result of using the revised language with two donate buttons. The success of this experiment can be attributed to using clear and direct language which more clearly outlines the ask.
When creating future experiments, we should use this success as proof that implementing two donate buttons with clear and direct language can be effective in increasing donor conversions. Furthermore, we should pay special attention to the messaging associated with each donate button, as this can play a large role in converting visitors into donors.

experiments.title: How adding a privacy statement below the submit button on content offer landing page affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a privacy statement below the submit button for landing page visitors will achieve greater email conversion because it will decrease anxiety about data security and increase the appeal of the offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/01/2022 - 09/30/2022
For a faith-based media organization, we wanted to know how including a privacy statement below the form submission button would affect email acquisition rates. We know that privacy continues to be a growing priority for users online and we wanted to test whether or not anticipating any reluctance to submit payment information online at the point of requesting a devotional eBook would reduce anxiety and encourage more people to complete the form.
The privacy statement below the button on the treatment reads: “*We take data and privacy seriously. Your information will be kept safe and will be used to send you your eBook. “
~50% of traffic received the control and ~50% of traffic received the treatment.  We set this up in Unbounce and, unfortunately, didn’t reset the conversions report so the samples look skewed in the report below as “control” reflects all samples since the launch of the content offer not just the ones during the test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Based on the results from this Unbounce experiment, it's clear that including a privacy statement below the form submission button had a major impact on email acquisition rates.
This shows that anticipating privacy concerns in our customers and adapting our strategies to address those concerns can have a substantive impact on our overall marketing efforts. In the future, it will be valuable to continue testing our privacy statements to determine what language is most effective in communicating security, understanding that our customers expect transparency and taking responsible steps to secure their personal data. We will be testing this approach on donation pages as well.
Unfortunately, we were not able to validate a similar increase in downstream donor conversion from this test.

experiments.title: How the placement of a call to action impacted email acquisitions on the homepage
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that communicating the value prop in a more prominent location for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in program sign ups.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/22/2022 - 12/28/2022
As we began driving paid traffic to the Little Patriots home page from multiple channels, we were noticing both high bounce rates (58%) and low traffic to the registration page (3.28%) which made us wonder if it was clear to new visitors how to sign-up for Little Patriots upon arriving to the website. In reviewing the site, there were multiple calls to action that seemed equally weighted and there was a lot of friction in the way interfering users from clearly understanding how and why to sign-up for the program. We discovered that the MOST important action we wanted users to take was being presented as the LEAST visible element on the page, likely causing visitors to miss it altogether. Since the goal for visitors was to sign up, we were curious if instead of having the sign up info presented at the bottom of the page, if we could increase conversion by putting the sign up info in the top content block on the page to reduce confusion about what the desired call to action was in order to drive higher name acquisition rates.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found after running the experiment for a week, was that the treatment with the value proposition and call to action placed at the top of the page increased email acquisition rate by 154.9% with 99% level of confidence. We believe this is because we moved the content block promoting and communicating the value of the extremely high-value free coursework which serves as the main driver of name acquisition for LP from being buried at the very bottom of the page where visitors had to hunt for the information. We made it easier immediately upon arrival to understand what the desired action was by removing confusion and friction: were visitors supposed to watch a video? explore programs and new episodes? make a gift? These were all options on the page that had varying weights assigned by their placement and level of which they were featured on the site. When we gave visitors a clear entry point at the top of the page and communicated the WHY, we found that more visitors took the intended action to sign up. Going forward, we will roll out the treatment design in order to improve the visibility of Little Patriots coursework while reducing friction and improving UX in an effort to increase name acquisition rates to make better use of the paid advertising dollars being spent to drive traffic to the site.

experiments.title: How Adding a “Start My Gift” Button to the Top of a Year-End Donation Page impacts donor conversion for mobile traffic
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a “start my gift” button at the top of the year-end donation page will increase donor conversion by decreasing donor friction
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/02/2022 - 12/18/2022
FamilyLife is a Christian ministry serving families worldwide. For their calendar year-end campaign, they wanted to test ways of improving donor conversion, particularly for mobile traffic which has typically had a much lower conversion rate on their site. We hypothesized that the length of the copy on the page might be adding friction to motivated traffic and wanted to see if we could reduce that friction by providing a button that would invite visitors to “start my gift” right at the top of the page. Clicking the button would drop the visitor directly to the donation form.
We ran the experiment on their campaign donation page for almost two weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we did not achieve a greater than 95% level of confidence for all traffic, mobile validated a lift and all other primary channels showed a strong positive impact with a directional level of confidence. What was surprising was that this button also increased revenue. We excluded any gifts over $750 that would skew the results and still achieved a 237% increase in revenue from the treatment with a 94% level of confidence. We will need to do additional testing to dig into why this impacted revenue so strongly but we will use this button for the remainder of the campaign.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Direct visitors had a 30.6% increase in donations with a 91% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How does the impact of a CTA that is Value Driven vs Product Driven affect the click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: My hypothesis was more visitors would convert on a CTA that was value driven rather than product driven. So, the value of a daily pro-life devotional that is emailed to you is that it reminds you to pray daily for the unborn.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/01/2021
Every Lent, we offer a 40-day Lenten Devotional Prayers for Life. This is always a popular offer and this year we wanted to have a sticky bar on our blog pages to draw visitors to the offer. We often use the CTA Get Your Free 40 Day Lent Devotional. I wanted to test this against a challenger variant that I have used in the past on Facebook. Get a Daily Reminder to Pray for the Unborn!
experiments.keyLearnings: People were 242% MORE motivated by the promise of a daily reminder to pray than simply by a free Lenten devotional. We will continue to test into ways of getting at the WHY of an offer in the CTA vs just WHAT the offer is.

experiments.title: How reinforcing the value proposition and adding a call-to-action affects donor conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a call-to-action and reinforcing the value proposition improve donor conversion and revenue?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/13/2016 - 06/13/2016
In an effort to optimize the main donation page for The Heritage Foundation, we took a look at the copy that was currently there to find ways to improve and increase the force of the value proposition. While the existing copy was strong and provided solid evidentials, we noticed it was missing an important element in the copy, an ask. Our hypothesis was that we would be able to increase donor conversion and overall revenue by adding copy, to what was already there, that included a call-to-action and reinforcement of the value proposition.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We had arrived at the original copy through a series of prior experiments. As a result, we were relatively confident that it adequately communicated the value proposition of The Heritage Foundation, but by simply adding an additional paragraph that reinforced that message and asking them to make a gift, we were able to not only get more donors to give, but give even more generously. The treatment increased donor conversion by 49%.
Additionally, we saw an increase in average gift by 62.9%. While the average gift lift had not yet statistically validated by the time the test was shut off, the approaching validity seems to indicate that we would be experiencing a combined lift to revenue of 143.5%.
This experiment reinforced the importance of increasing the force of the value proposition by reminding people of what their gift will do and always having a call-to-action in the copy.

experiments.title: How changing the CTA button on a Facebook ad to download now impacted email acquisition rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a download now button for ebook downloaders will achieve an increase in email acquisition rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/01/2022 - 12/31/2022
The Hoover Institution is the public policy think tank for Stanford University. Part of its outreach for the public is making available ebooks on specific topics available for download.
During the holiday season in 2021 we ran a test where we changed the CTA button on a Facebook ad promoting a specific ebook from ‘learn more’ to ‘download now.’ Our hypothesis was since we were asking people to download the ebook on the landing page by providing their email address, using a ‘download now’ CTA button would reduce the friction.
experiments.keyLearnings: By simply changing the CTA button to download now, Hoover had a 48.3% increase in email acquisition rates, with a 99.9% level of confidence. Tying a download of an ebook into the button copy made a significant impact  for Hoover. This is a test we should continue running on multiple offers.

experiments.title: How a softer call to action affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a softer call to action generate more donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/14/2016 - 03/18/2016
The Heritage Foundation was promoting their Membership Card to non-members giving them the opportunity to become a member.
Anticipating that the direct ask to “become a member” would receive fewer (but more motivated) clicks from the email, they wanted to test whether the added step and call-to-action to “confirm the name on your card” would generate more clicks, and result in more donations.
Since they weren’t making a direct ask, they created a different offer flow for this treatment. They created a two-step form for the treatment that started with a simple “confirm your name” step (pre-populated with the visitor’s name), and then took them to complete their donation. Because the visitor was indicating their intent to become a member by completing the first step, they removed much of the sales copy on the donation page in order to remove friction from the process.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The softer call to action generated a 54% increase in clicks, but a 51.6% decrease in donations. This revealed a key insight: all traffic is not the same. Visitors come with different motivations, and the traffic to the treatment flow was not at all "sold".

This prompted a second test to see if reintroducing the value proposition copy on the follow-on donation form would affect the results.

experiments.title: How the call to action of a Facebook ad affects clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: Which Facebook ad call to action will generate more clicks?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/15/2016 - 07/15/2016
NextAfter has a research blog we run Facebook advertising on to reach new audiences with our nonprofit fundraising optimization content. We wondered if simply altering the call to action on one of these ads would affect the click rate.
The control call to action was simply the name of the blog, while the treatment turned the call to action into an actual action saying “Learn How to…”
experiments.keyLearnings: We learned that altering the call to action to be focused on ‘learning’ creates a 74% drop in click rate. It’s possible that the call to action of “Learn How to Humanize Your Email Fundraising” did not provide enough clarity about the nature of the content. In contrast, the control of “5 Ways to Humanize Your Email Appeals” provided a clear description of the type of content, how long it was, and exactly what aspect of fundraising it applied to.

experiments.title: How a text link instead of a button impacted email clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a hyperlinked page URL for prospects will achieve an increase in email clickthrough rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/23/2021 - 02/04/2021
In testing what generated the most clicks when rolling out a new eBook offer to their housefile list, The Fund for American Studies wanted to know whether the approach of using the landing page URL with a hyperlink would definitively be better than using a button in the email promotion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a confidence level of 98.5%, we observed a +17.5% increase in email clickthrough rate.

The reality is that when you write an email from an individual, the reader expects the person that wrote them to not use "marketing techniques" (like in this case, a button).

Therefore, the believability that an actual person on the other side of the email actually wrote them goes up when you treat the clickable link in emails like how a real person would send them — in this case, not as a button, but rather as a page URL that is hyperlinked.

When possible, continue to use clickable URL's instead of fancy marketing elements within emails — like a button.

experiments.title: How a visual redesign of a main donate button on the website impacted clicks

rows.name: New - Donate to TGC Green Button

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that visually emphasizing the donate button for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in clicks to the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/25/2022 - 03/03/2022
The TGC team was inspired by another experiment they saw in a NextAfter Institute webinar to visually modify the main donate button on their website navigation bar. Rather than rolling out the new version based solely on another organization’s results, we decided to test into the optimal design by splitting traffic 50/50 and measuring the results of how many people clicked on each version of the button. The existing button said “Give” and was visually the same as the other calls to action in the navigation header. The treatment was a green button to help it stand out visually from the rest of the page and the text was revised to “Donate to TGC” to add clarity.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for one month, we saw a valid increase in clicks on the new green button that said "Donate to TGC". What was interesting, however, is that donations and revenue recorded on the donation page were actually higher coming from the control version. Because an experiment was simultaneously running on the main donation page that could have been responsible for impacting these results, we will have to take this into account on a future experiment. Overall we learned that the new button design did, in fact, attract significantly more visitors to the main donate page.

experiments.title: How a softer call to action affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a softer call to action generate more donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/14/2016 - 03/18/2016
The Heritage Foundation was promoting their Membership Card to non-members giving them the opportunity to become a member.
Anticipating that the direct ask to “become a member” would receive fewer (but more motivated) clicks from the email, they wanted to test whether the added step and call-to-action to “confirm the name on your card” would generate more clicks, and result in more donations.
Since they weren’t making a direct ask, they created a different offer flow for this treatment. They created a two-step form for the treatment that started with a simple “confirm your name” step (pre-populated with the visitor’s name), and then took them to complete their donation. Because the visitor was indicating their intent to become a member by completing the first step, they removed much of the sales copy on the donation page in order to remove friction from the process.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The softer call to action generated a 54% increase in clicks, but a 51.6% decrease in donations. This revealed a key insight: all traffic is not the same. Visitors come with different motivations, and the traffic to the treatment flow was not at all "sold".

This prompted a second test to see if reintroducing the value proposition copy on the follow-on donation form would affect the results.

experiments.title: How removing the call-to-action at the top of a landing page impacts name conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing the design and navigational elements increase the visitors getting to the assessment?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/24/2017 - 10/30/2017
The treatment decreased the amount of people getting to the assessment by 28.6%! While we hypothesized that the simplified page would help decrease friction and motivate more people to click-through because the focus of the page was on the value proposition, we learned that:
People coming to this page were already highly motivated to take the assessment. The additional value proposition focus wasn’t needed to convert these people.
By removing the call-to-action at the top of the page, we made it harder for these highly motivated people to click-through to the actual assessment.
We recommend building upon these results and learnings and continue testing separate elements on this page to get a better understanding of the motivation of the audience.
experiments.keyLearnings: Focus on the Family has a Marriage Assessment on their website to help couples grow and strengthen their marriage. Before an end-user gets to the actual assessment there is a landing page with value proposition and a call-to-action. In an effort to increase conversion on this page of people getting to the assessment, they developed a treatment. They hypothesized that by simplifying the page – including removing the call-to-action at the top of the page – they might be able to motivate more people to take it by leading with the value proposition of the assessment. The goal of the treatment was to eliminate any elements on the page that would cause potential friction in the conversion process. They split the traffic coming to the assessment page and tested the control against the treatment.

experiments.title: How adding a call-to-action to the top of a simplified landing page impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would adding the button back in to the top of the page increase acquisition on the simplified page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/14/2017 - 11/30/2017
By adding the button back in to the top of the page, we still weren’t able to increase the traffic going to the assessment. We saw a slight decrease in conversion – not as significant as when the button was completely removed – but still a dip.
We hypothesize that this audience is so highly motivated that they don’t actually need this assessment landing page. We propose testing removing this in-between page altogether and sending people straight to the assessment to get more people to take and complete it.
experiments.keyLearnings: Focus on the Family offers a Marriage Assessment on their website. In a previous test on the assessment landing page, we learned that a simplified page where we removed elements of friction actually decreased conversion because we removed a button at the top of the page that would take this highly motivated audience directly to the assessment. To build upon those learnings, we hypothesized that we could increase conversion by keeping and testing the simplified version again but simply just adding back in the button to the assessment at the top of the page. We split the traffic and tested it.

experiments.title: How clarity in the call-to-action affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a softer call-to-action with a compelling video increase course signups?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/25/2017 - 02/19/2017
Dallas Theological Seminary launched a new course called “Can You Trust the Bible?” They had shot a video of their Academic Dean explaining the course, and wanted to see how it performed against a direct invitation from the President to join the course. So they created two entrance paths to the course. The first was their control—a direct ask, via email, from the President of DTS. The second was an invitation from the President with a call-to-action to “watch the video to learn more about the course”. They hypothesized that the softer call-to-action would drive more traffic to the landing page, and the short video with a clear call-to-action would produce more overall conversions.
They split their file and closely monitored the data to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment produced a 32.6% decrease in signups from the control. This shows that the softer ask does not produce more motivation to sign up for the course, even with the added benefit of the video. There could be a few factors at play here. First, all clicks are not created equal—a click to “learn more”, in this case, is far less valuable than a click to “sign up”. Second, video might actually be obscuring the value proposition by not allowing the user to read it in their own time. Many people can read much faster than a video can communicate information, which leads them to be more likely to accept an offer where they control the cadence of the value proposition.
This does not necessarily mean, however, that the video doesn’t have a place in the marketing schedule of a new course—it may just mean that it needs to be placed later in the schedule to motivate users who were not incentivized by the original message.

experiments.title: How adding a “give monthly” button to the main navigation menu affects recurring donation rates.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a give monthly button to a site’s main navigation will achieve higher recurring donation rates .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/23/2022
To increase recurring donation rates for a public policy client, we wanted to know how adding a “give monthly” button to the main navigation menu would impact recurring monthly donation rates. Our hypothesis was that by increasing the visibility of this donation option, more site visitors would decide to give recurring gifts. On desktop this treatment appeared top-of-page, in line with the navigation menu items. On mobile, the give Monthly button was placed at the bottom of a burger menu, directly under the pre-existing donation button.
We ran this experiment for one month, splitting traffic 50/50 between the control and treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment produced a few interesting and unexpected results. First, both the control and treatment (w/ "give monthly" button) failed to produce any recurring gifts, indicating that simply increasing the visibility of the option to make a recurring gift was not enough to motivate prospective donors to do so without a compelling reason why.
Second, while there was not a significant increase in one-time donations for desktop traffic, we did see a valid 144% lift in one-time donations for mobile visitors.
Reviewing these results, we hypothesized that adding a second button increased the visibility of both buttons, not just one. And so we wondered what was causing donors to exclusively choose to give once rather than monthly.
This led us to hypothesize that this increase in one-time giving may have been prompted by the persuasion principle of "reject and retreat". This principle of behavioral psychology states that by "rejecting" a larger ask (a recurring gift), a prospective donor may be more likely to convert when we "retreat" to a smaller commitment (one-time donation).
In this case, by showing two donation options (one representing a larger commitment than the other), donors may have been prompted to reject the larger commitment but then agree to the smaller ask. This may help explain the motivation of choosing one button over the other while so significantly increasing overall giving among people who saw the two-button treatment.
Unfortunately, it is not possible to draw a definitive conclusion one way or the other in this instance, however, this experiment has given us some interesting data points and things to consider about the motivations behind these results.
Since we have discovered that simply adding a monthly donation button provides insufficient motivation for more people to give monthly, we would like to look for future opportunities to increase the visibility of monthly giving, while also conveying why monthly giving is so important and the impact it will have. A sticky bar may be an option, a dear reader, or an exit-intent pop-up. We may also consider drawing attention to the give monthly option + impact on the main donation page. In order to determine the impact of "reject and retreat" on donor motivation, we would need to formulate a more intentional experiment that first asked donors to make a monthly gift, and then followed up with a one-time ask to 50% of traffic. This may be best tested in the form of an appeal that asks specifically for monthly gifts but then follows up with a "retreat" that asks for a one-time gift instead. This might also be tested using a pop-up or some other form of notification. While this may have played a part in the results seen in this experiment, there is simply not enough context to know for sure. But this is an interesting concept that will be worth testing in a different environment.

experiments.title: How emphasizing exclusivity in a Facebook ad graphic impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing appeal by highlighting exclusivity  for Facebook ad viewers will achieve an increase in click rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/19/2022
We were running an eBook cover image for a new offer from The Heritage Foundation and wondered if we could make the offer more appealing by highlighting the exclusivity of the resource. This research report is very unique as it is only available from this organization and no one else, but this was not communicated anywhere in the ad. We created a treatment graphic that placed emphasis on the fact that this offer was exclusive and we reiterated the CTA “Learn More” directly on the graphic to help it stand out in the newsfeed.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found after running both versions of the ad was that the treatment produced a 21.6% lift in click rate with 98.4% level of confidence so the test results are valid. We believe this experiment saw positive results because the enhanced appeal of making the offer more exclusive made it more desirable because ad viewers were now aware they could not get a resource like this from any other organization or get access to this information anywhere else. We will continue testing this concept on other exclusive offers from The Heritage Foundation and see if we can achieve similar results in other acquisition spaces—such as email prospecting—using this learning.

experiments.title: How changing the donation button on website to go directly to a donation form impacted results.

rows.name: Replace The Drop Down with Donation Button

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing friction on a donation button for a web visitor will achieve a higher conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/26/2021 - 01/27/2022
The Hoover Institution is one of the country’s oldest think tanks, and offers a very wide variety of research to the public. The donation button on their homepage traditionally linked to a ‘ways to give’ page, since Hoover has a variety of ways the public can support their research. We wanted to test whether directing that button directly to a donation form would increase conversion rates. We ran this test from the end of October through the end of January, and while we originally started it as a 50/50 traffic test, during their calendar-year-end campaign we pushed 90% of the traffic directly to the donation form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Despite the larger sample size (due to shifting more traffic towards the test variant), our hypothesis held true: an increase of 85% in donor conversion rates with a 99.6% level of confidence.
By reducing the amount of clicks, and thus friction, potential donors had to take to support the work of the Hoover Institution, this experiment showed that when someone clicks on a donate/support button, their intention is to make a donation as fast as possible. Adding in different ways to give only increases the friction points.
Moving forward, we will continue to experiment with this, as well as adding in a monthly donate button with the launch of Hoover's new monthly supporter program in early 2022.

experiments.title: How adding a monthly donate button to the main navigation menu impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a monthly donate button to the main navigation menu will increase donor conversion
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/23/2022 - 06/10/2022
KCBI is a large Christian radio station in the DFW area with a worldwide listening audience. They wanted to see if they could improve donor conversion on their website by adding a monthly donation button to their main navigation next to their one-time donate button. Monthly donors are an important part of their program and they hoped that this additional callout would increase monthly donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Not only did the addition of a monthly donate button increase donor conversion, but it increased revenue as well by 138.3% with a 94% level of confidence. Our hypothesis is that donors appreciated the clarity of knowing the two types of donations they could make at the start of the process while also just simply giving them another visual reminder of how easy it is to donate. While we originally hoped to also validate an increase in monthly donor conversion, the small sample size of monthly donations was not sufficient for a reliable level of confidence.
Caveats. The language in the onetime donation option changed from the control to the treatment. This muddied the results of the test. Also by adding an additional button, we had to take the onetime gift button out of the nav menu with the other menu items and present it on its own.
Future Testing:
We need to ensure that the changes to the nav menu are rolled out to the entire KCBI site to increase sample size and gain more accurate data. With that in place, we can test the following elements from this test to learn what had the greatest  impact:
Test position of the onetime gift buttonTest the copy on the onetime gift button (give vs donate)test adding a monthly donate button again once we have the data from the prior two tests.

experiments.title: How a sticky donate button with a campaign specific CTA effects donor conversion during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a CTA on a sticky donate button related to an ongoing high urgency campaign will result in a higher number of donations, than a generic donate button.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/07/2022 - 06/22/2022
Focus on the Family runs a high urgency campaign every year centered around pro-life issues. This year, they wanted to test if adding a sticky donate button to article pages with a call to action mirroring the ongoing high urgency campaign would result in higher donor conversions than a similar button with a generic call to action. It is worth noting that this experiment was limited to a segmented audience that used user data to identify if a user had prior pro-life interests. For the control, a generic donate button (with a pulsing heart icon) was placed on article pages that matched the data segmentation. For the treatment, a sticky button with the words ” Save a Baby” (with a pulsing baby icon) was placed on article pages that matched the data segmentation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
During this experiment that had 91,000 samples over a two week period, we saw a 55% increase for the treatment with a 95.5% Level of Confidence. In this case, this experiment validated for the hypothesis. Key takeaways from this experiment are that matching the CTA of the button to the segmented audience during a high urgency campaign can result in a greater conversion.
Next steps for the experiment would be to test his same hypothesis using the same data segments but do so during a non-high urgency campaign to see if properly segmented users are still converted at a higher frequency with a specific CTA.
