experiments.title: How Adding Value Proposition Language near the ‘Give’ Button on a Homepage Affects Conversion Rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition language near the give button on a homepage for web traffic will achieve higher conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/27/2021 - 12/29/2021
Although Luther Seminary has a large, bright donation callout on their homepage, we observed that it was not driving the expected level of donations.
Since there were other bright design elements competing for attention around the donation callout, and because Luther Seminary’s homepage is somewhat ‘busy’, with many navigation choices for visitors, we hypothesized that perhaps visitors weren’t being given enough of a reason to choose to donate, with so many other options from which to choose.
Previous experiments have shown that clearly communicating a value proposition motivates donors to give. In light of these past learnings, we decided to test how adding value proposition language near the ‘GIVE’ button on Luther Seminary’s homepage might improve conversion rates without necessitating a redesign of their homepage.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running our experiment for 90 days, we observed a 21% decrease in conversion rate with an 82.5% level of confidence.
While this result was surprising, it does provide context and points the direction for future optimization.
Most interestingly, while returning visitors showed a 65% decrease in conversion rates, the new treatment did result in a 127% increase in donations from new traffic (including an impressive 295% lift in paid traffic donations).
This provides some interesting insights:
first, we can hypothesize that for first time visitors, especially those being acquired via paid traffic, the value proposition was helpful in inspiring them to give
second, there could be a variety of factors leading to the decrease in donations from returning traffic, one of which may be the sheer number of things competing for a visitor's attention on the homepage. A quick heuristic analysis of the homepage indicates that it is quite busy and may cause friction for returning visitors. However, an analysis of website data (such as page path, exit, and bounce rate reports), combined with scroll tracking / heat mapping, should be conducted to see what people are doing when they are landing on the homepage and where they are going when they click through
It is doubtful that the inclusion of helpful value prop language was the key motivating factor in reducing donations from returning visitors, but additional analysis would be required to determine this with a high level of certainty.

experiments.title: How topic-focused versus Persona-focused calls-to-action affect email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that topic-driven CTAs for homepage traffic will achieve more site conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/11/2022 - 06/06/2022
When initially launching NextAfter.com, we had implemented specific calls-to-action on the homepage targeting 3 key personas: nonprofit executives, fundraising directors, and fundraising practitioners.
We wondered if a more topic-based approach could actually lead to more conversions from the homepage. The thinking was that many people are coming to our website looking for specific solutions – and giving clear categories related to common problems would lead to a more relevant  engagement.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The result was that a more topic-driven CTAs on the homepage were leaning towards a 9% decrease in overall conversions - but only at 72% level of confidence. Most interestingly – the topical CTAs led to a valid 25% decrease in conversion for returning visitors, but new site traffic saw a 28% increase in conversion at an 82% level of confidence.
This shows us that the traffic coming to our homepage is not of a singular motivation. There appears to be variance of motivation and intention of new VS returning traffic.
This will require more analysis of new VS visiting behavior to determine how we might customize a website experience for differing motivations.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Returning visitors had a 25.9% decrease in all downloads and registrations with a 100% level of confidence.New visitors had a 28% increase in all downloads and registrations with an 82% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a revised headline and CTA impacted signups for a weekly newsletter
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the value and clarity in an email newsletter signup form for potential newsletter subscribers will achieve an increase in email acquisition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/04/2022 - 06/06/2022
Our third test on this page had a significant variance in the sample size on the control versus the treatment so we wanted to relaunch the test to make sure the results were not muddied. We split traffic between the control page and a treatment page with a headline that had a stronger emphasis on the VALUE provided to the subscriber rather than the newsletter product itself: “Get weekly access to trusted, timely, gospel-centered resources.” We also created a treatment to the button copy to provide additional clarity in the call to action:“Send TGC Weekly to my inbox »”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment ran for one month and we observed a 24.2% decrease in conversion rate with 97.7% level of confidence so the results are valid. This time our sample sizes were accurately proportionate so these results override the increase we observed on the previous test. Our hypothesis is that getting TGC's "best content" was a more appealing description and "trusted, timely, gospel-centered resources" did not provide enough clarity, appeal, or value to convince users to subscribe to the weekly newsletter.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Desktop visitors had a 33.2% decrease in email acquisitions with a 95% level of confidence.
 	Returning visitors had a 40.2% decrease in email acquisitions with a 98% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 28.6% decrease in email acquisitions with a 100% level of confidence.

Another organization we partner with had seen promising results from adding a sticky button to the side of website blog and article pages. We wanted to test this concept with another organization that had regularly high engagement on article and blog pages to see if we could convert engaged website visitors to donors by including a call to action sticky button that followed them down the page as they scrolled during their time on the site and consumed the content. We excluded any URLs that would be only visited by people looking for services from the organization.

experiments.title: How a revised headline and CTA impacted email acquisition for a newsletter sign up (Part III)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the value and clarity in an email newsletter signup form for potential newsletter subscribers will achieve an increase in email acquisition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/12/2022 - 04/29/2022
Our previous two tests on this page failed to get the results we were after, so we gave it a third try. Rather than trying to pack the page full of value proposition, we hypothesized that page visitors were already motivated enough to sign up because of the value prop in the email copy. We still believed we could optimize the signup form page so we developed a treatment headline that had a stronger emphasis on the VALUE provided to the subscriber rather than the newsletter product itself: “Get weekly access to trusted, timely, gospel-centered resources.” We also created a treatment to the button copy to provide additional clarity in the call to action:“Send TGC Weekly to my inbox »”
experiments.keyLearnings: What we saw after about 2 weeks was that the treatment page had a 48.3% increase in conversion rate with 99.9% level of confidence so the results are valid. We believe this is because we increased clarity and made the offer both more appealing and more exclusive in a very succinct manner. Thousands of other newsletters use "Subscribe" as their call to action. We wanted to show exclusivity that this is a specially branded and curated newsletter product just from TGC that is not available anywhere else. We also let people know 1) how often they could expect to receive the newsletter, 2) what they could expect to receive in the contents of the newsletter as well as the value it would provide them, and 3) where they would receive it. Because traffic to this page was limited to the email channel, specifically those in the New Subscriber Welcome Series, we had a fairly low number of total email acquisitions. It would be best to test this concept again but open the filters to include additional traffic sources such as additional emails, organic search, and internal website visitors so we can get a larger sample size.

experiments.title: The Impact on Donor Conversion of a Sticky Donate Button on Family Life’s Article Pages
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a sticky donate button will increase donor conversion on FamilyLife’s article pages.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/16/2022 - 04/24/2022
FamilyLife receives a lot of traffic to their article pages. Prior testing with similar organizations found that by having a donate button that “sticks” to the side of the screen with a small animated element (like a heart) that scrolls with the user, we could increase donor conversion. (Focus on the Family had a 60% increase in this test). We hypothesized that by creating a sticky donate button using their brand colors and a small animated heart, we could increase donor conversion by reducing friction and creating appeal for donor engagement.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The addition of a sticky button to article pages increased donor conversion for all traffic by reducing friction and increasing appeal. However, due to a large donation made on the control version during the test, revenue results were skewed. Even with that large gift, revenue impact was unable to reach a high level of confidence.
Either way, we have shown that a sticky donate button on the article pages drives more traffic and donations for Family Life.

experiments.title: How adding Value Prop language below the donation button impacted donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding in Value Prop language below the donation button for premium buyers will achieve higher revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/11/2022 - 04/19/2022
Pathway to Victory is a premium based and driven organization. The audience comes because of their interest in the book, resource, media set. PTV has not historically focused on value prop language on their website but through the Value Prop Survey we saw that the audience sees PTV as the source of truth and practical biblical teaching. So we tested including that value prop underneath the donation submission button, on all donation pages, to see if in the moment of decision, reinforcing the fact that the donor doesn’t just get a premium but also helps PTV to equip Christians with resources.
experiments.keyLearnings:
NextAfter had seen this test increase revenue with other clients which led us to try it out with PTV. We were very shocked to see such a decrease in donations and revenue on the treatment.
PTV is very premium focused on their website and does not have value prop language or general language that guides a donor to give toward the mission of PTV. The only donation opportunities are tied to premium offers. Moving forward we will test into adding Value Prop language into other places on the website prior to the donation submit button so that the audience is primed and ready to make a donation to the organization through micro yeses.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Donations experienced a 49.2% decrease with a 100% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding a donation ‘button’ on a website impacted donation page views for First Baptist Dallas
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donate button for website visitors will achieve higher views of the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/10/2022 - 04/04/2022
First Baptist Dallas’ website focuses on promoting the work that the church does, from Sunday services to events in the community, to other services around the Dallas-Forth Worth area, as well as virtually through their iCampus platform. While an option to donate to the church is part of the header navigation, it is not prominent. We wanted to test whether creating a ‘button’ for the Give link would drive more traffic to the donation page (we could not track conversions).
experiments.keyLearnings:
Using pageviews as our metric for tracking the results of this experiment, we saw a negligible decrease between the control and treatment 'button.' This is most likely due to the fact that the majority of the visitors to First Dallas' website are not going there with the intent to donate; rather, they are looking for information about First Dallas, events, Sunday services, and other information.

experiments.title: How adding an impact statement under the donate button affects conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an impact statement under the donate button for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/03/2022 - 03/10/2022
In a recent experiment, we hypothesized that adding an impact statement directly under the donate button would decrease abandonment and thus increase conversion rates. While the treatment in that experience did produce an 11.9% lift in conversion rates, our sample size prevented us from validating our experiment.
We decided to run a similar experiment in an effort to validate our hypothesis and to improve flat conversion rates on a donation page where visitors land after signing up for one of Focus on the Family’s video series. As in our previous experiment, we developed a treatment that included an impact statement directly below the donate button. And because we wanted to validate the lift we saw before, we left the messaging untouched. We again hypothesized that this would help decrease abandonment by highlighting the value of their donation. For this experiment, we ran an A/B test with traffic split 50/50.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 64 days, we observed a 24.4% lift in donor conversion rates with a 97% level of confidence, validating our hypothesis that adding an impact statement under the donate button would improve conversion rates.
We believe that this lift resulted in part from using impact language that showed what specifically has been accomplished when donors give. In addition to adding value, this treatment also shifts the perspective from "we" (organizational) language to donor-centric language that puts them at the heart of the solution.
We also believe that placing the impact statement near (in this case directly under) the donation button may have reduced abandonment as it provides one more reminder of the tangible value of the donor's contribution.
Interestingly, returning visitors had an 89.5% increase in donations, indicating that the impact statement may have inspired donors who previously reached the donation page but did not give to donate this time around.
This experiment validated (and even improved upon) the lift of our prior experiment and reinforced the value of showing donors the impact of their donations. Based on these findings, we believe that such an impact statement should be included on all of Focus on the Family's donation pages. It may also be worth testing the impact of moving the impact statement above the donation form so that donors see it earlier in the transaction.
Observations of the impact on visitor segments in this experiment:
Returning visitors had an 89.5% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidenceEmail visitors had a 216.1% increase in donations with an 90% level of confidence

experiments.title: How a visual redesign of a main donate button on the website impacted clicks

rows.name: New - Donate to TGC Green Button

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that visually emphasizing the donate button for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in clicks to the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/25/2022 - 03/03/2022
The TGC team was inspired by another experiment they saw in a NextAfter Institute webinar to visually modify the main donate button on their website navigation bar. Rather than rolling out the new version based solely on another organization’s results, we decided to test into the optimal design by splitting traffic 50/50 and measuring the results of how many people clicked on each version of the button. The existing button said “Give” and was visually the same as the other calls to action in the navigation header. The treatment was a green button to help it stand out visually from the rest of the page and the text was revised to “Donate to TGC” to add clarity.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for one month, we saw a valid increase in clicks on the new green button that said "Donate to TGC". What was interesting, however, is that donations and revenue recorded on the donation page were actually higher coming from the control version. Because an experiment was simultaneously running on the main donation page that could have been responsible for impacting these results, we will have to take this into account on a future experiment. Overall we learned that the new button design did, in fact, attract significantly more visitors to the main donate page.

experiments.title: How promoting a petition in the “Dear Reader” call to action promotion on news articles impacted email signup rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that promoting a relevant petition for article readers will achieve increase email signup rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/12/2022 - 01/24/2022
As a part of experimenting with the promotion of newsroom articles, Americans for Prosperity wanted to test whether promoting a news-relevant petition in their “Dear Reader” promotion (which is a call to action at the end of a news article on their website) would be more productive than promoting their email subscription offer.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With 100% level of confidence, we observed nearly a 3x increase in email signup rates (+196.1%) when promoting the news-relevant petition.
What's also interesting to note is that the treatment petition offer also offers access to the subscription type offered in the control ad, but it's the optional checkbox shown before the button to submit your petition form.
The data shows that even though this box is unchecked by default — meaning that if someone wanted to sign up for that subscription type, they would have to go through an extra step of checking the box before submitting the form — the treatment saw that 90.7% of the people who signed the treatment form also checked that box.
This means that the treatment with its unchecked, optional subscription offer, produced +58.8% more subscriptions to the primary offer made in the control experience (confidence level: 100%).
This means that we should look to promote petitions on the news articles moving forward. The next challenge will be to understand/determine how this offer performs when the news cycle shifts away from the offer in question.

experiments.title: How redesigning the main donate button on the homepage affected traffic at year-end

rows.name: New - Green Donate to TGC Button

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that emphasizing the main donation button for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in traffic to the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/30/2021
Leading up to calendar year-end, we know that visitors to the TGC homepage had a high motivation to give. We wanted to make sure that potentially highly motivated donors could easily find where to make their year-end gift.
We rolled out a new button design on the homepage to see if we could make it easier for potential donors to find the donation page, but we wanted to run this as a test against the old version to capture the impact.
The new button (running as the control for this experiment) read “Donate to TGC” and we made it green to stand out for visual emphasis while the old button (the treatment version) simply said “Give” and visually matched all other menu options in the header.
We split traffic to each version.
experiments.keyLearnings:
What we found was the new button (green button) drove more traffic to the donation page. Of these two buttons, the “Donate to TGC” button provided the additional clarity necessary to increase traffic to the page while the white "Give" button saw a decrease in traffic of 20%.
Because we were running a separate test on the donation page, we did not factor in conversion rate or revenue on the donation page from this experiment. We only wanted to measure the impact on clicks through to the donation page. What we learned is that the more visual button made it easier for potential donors to find the next step we want them to take by adding clarity in the messaging and visually leading them where to go.

experiments.title: Does removing images and buttons and using plain-text on a high urgency campaign increase clicks for Pathway to Victory?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing images for email subscribers will achieve a higher click through rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/31/2021 - 12/31/2021
December 31st is usually the day when digital donors receive the most emails from nonprofits, as that is the day where the majority of them raise the most revenue. In order to cut through the ‘noise’ of December 31 emails, for Pathway to Victory we tested a plain-text email versus their control email which used graphics. The messaging remained the same on both versions; for our test however we made the email look like it came directly from Dr. Robert Jeffress. By removing all images, and removing hyperlinked text, while adding in the full URLs for the landing page, our goal was to increase the number of click throughs.
experiments.keyLearnings: By making the digital donor feel like they were being personally reached out to by Dr. Jeffress, instead of receiving a generic 'marketing' email with graphics, we drove a higher click through rate and engagement with Pathway to Victory. This hypothesis will be useful to continue to test moving forward with not just high-urgency campaigns like a December 31st email, but throughout the year.

experiments.title: How framing the call to action as a recurring donation impacts overall conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that emphasizing the need for ongoing monthly support for survey takers will achieve an increase in long term revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/17/2021 - 11/24/2021
With the ACI education survey, the initial send to the house file found that the donation page had a ~20% instant donor conversion rate. This indicated that this was a highly motivated audience. Since one of the priorities in the coming months is to grow a sustainable recurring donor program for the organization, we decided to test out recurring language for subsequent email sends.
We had previously tested similar language in a texting campaign and found that it was able to increase recurring donor conversion rate but at the sake of slightly less one-time donors. Since that campaign was a direct solicitation, we wanted to test the same concept on a content offer like this survey.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The emphasis on recurring giving reduced overall conversion rate by 33.6%. It did result in one additional recurring gift but that is certainly not worth the significant reduction in overall revenue and donations.
The goal of acquiring recurring donors should still be a focus but the language used in this experiment was not compelling enough to drive the desired increase on ongoing monthly support. We'll need to find new reasons for giving a monthly gift or increase the emphasis on recurring in future experiments.

experiments.title: How the type of ask in an appeal affects click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a softer ask increase click rate in an email appeal?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/20/2017 - 10/24/2017
National Breast Cancer Foundation sent an email appeal for BCAM and wanted to test the click-through rate on a direct ask call-to-action (give your gift here) vs. a soft ask (learn more about how you can help). The rest of the email remained the same on the control/treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings: The softer ask increased the clickthrough rate by 78%. It’s likely that the “learn more” call to action encouraged more people who weren’t initially planning to give to the donation page, while people who were already motivated to give a gift were more likely to click a link from a direct ask. This leads to a higher click-through rate but does not necessarily translate to more gifts.

experiments.title: How call-to-action visibility affects email clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a second button to the top of the email increase click through rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/01/2016 - 05/13/2016
The treatment with the second button increased click through rate 40.4%. The new button at the top of the email received some clicks, but the CTR on the unchanged links lower in the email increased as well (19%, 11%, and 27% respectively). This confirmed our hypothesis that more readers would stay engaged with the email and ultimately click if there was a call to action visible immediately upon opening.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The Heritage Foundation was partnering with Townhall Media to generate email signups through an eBook giveaway. Townhall generated new signups by sending sponsored emails to their list.
Early results showed acceptable open and conversion rates, but lower than expected clickthrough rates.
We hypothesized that the email openers were abandoning the email because the value proposition and call to action were not immediately visible upon opening. By adding a second call to action button at the top of the email we anticipated a higher number of readers would remain engaged with the email and ultimately click through.

experiments.title: How a more prominent callout combined with a more powerful value proposition affects new site signups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the visibility of the CTA and the force of the value proposition for blog post visitors will achieve an increase in new site signups.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/02/2021 - 02/17/2021
CaringBridge had engaged in a campaign to write more blog posts to help people in life situations who might be ideal prospects to start a CaringBridge site. However, on the blog posts themselves, the CTA was a small purple button that was inserted in different areas throughout the posts. The team thought that the button should be fully built out into a detached section that made a focused value proposition for starting a site and had a more powerful CTA.
They created a box that popped out visually from the main container, had a headline that addressed the audience segment directly, and listed a clear, concise value proposition for starting a new site. Finally, they centered the CTA around the user and highlighted that it was free, and used a lighter brand color to call more visual attention to the button.
They hypothesized that this would increase the number of new sites started, a critical metric for CaringBridge.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment produced a 65.5% increase in new site signups, which showed the first step in converting more blog traffic to start new CaringBridge sites. Future tests will include more design differentiation, placement of the box, and augmenting it through sticky bars and popups.

experiments.title: How a text link instead of a button impacted email clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a hyperlinked page URL for prospects will achieve an increase in email clickthrough rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/23/2021 - 02/04/2021
In testing what generated the most clicks when rolling out a new eBook offer to their housefile list, The Fund for American Studies wanted to know whether the approach of using the landing page URL with a hyperlink would definitively be better than using a button in the email promotion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a confidence level of 98.5%, we observed a +17.5% increase in email clickthrough rate.

The reality is that when you write an email from an individual, the reader expects the person that wrote them to not use "marketing techniques" (like in this case, a button).

Therefore, the believability that an actual person on the other side of the email actually wrote them goes up when you treat the clickable link in emails like how a real person would send them — in this case, not as a button, but rather as a page URL that is hyperlinked.

When possible, continue to use clickable URL's instead of fancy marketing elements within emails — like a button.

experiments.title: How showing the impact of a donor’s gift on each array button impacted revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that showing the impact on the gift array buttons for prospective donors will achieve increase revenue.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/22/2021 - 01/28/2021
The Canadian Taxpayer Federation recently launched a petition and in an attempt to improve revenue. The donation page outlined how a gift would be used to promote the petition to others so they could sign the petition, too.
We decided to experiment with showing the number of people their gift could put the petition in front of on the gift array buttons to see if doing the math for the donor would increase their generosity.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The gift array with impact was a significant improvement in revenue, driven by a +22% increase in average gift, along with a +15.3% increase in donor conversion rate (LoC: 70%).

These two metrics compounded together to drive a +40.7% increase in revenue with this approach.

This is a massive win for the organization, and where possible, pairing impact with a gift array is key to driving increased generosity for the donor.

Some other observations made from this experiment are:

experiments.title: How a tabbed form increases giving on a main donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: Presenting the giving options in two clear and distinct formats will increase giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/05/2021 - 04/04/2021
For their February and March campaigns, we tested a tabbed giving form on FamilyLife’s main donation page. The premise behind this was to see if presenting the different giving options, one-time or monthly, in two distinct giving forms would increase donor conversion. We have seen this work well for some organization’s audiences and not for others. So the first step was to ensure that this format did not decrease giving. The second step will be to see how this format affects recurring donation conversion with different calls to action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This tabbed form increased giving by 60.5% and increased revenue by 230%. Our hypothesis is that by making the giving options more clear, it diminished the decision friction, which in turn allows the donor to feel good about their choice to give.
Other notable findings in different segments include that this treatment increased giving on all devices, especially mobile. Both returning and new visitors preferred this format. The only channel that saw a decrease in conversion was paid search, with a decrease of 57%. While both traffic from organic and social saw an increase in donor conversion.
The next step is testing the call to action for the monthly giving option to see if we can increase recurring giving.

experiments.title: How more direct button copy affects checkout rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using direct button copy for prospective sponsors will achieve an increase in completed sponsorships.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/18/2021
Compassion International sent visitors to a “child bio” page as part of their sponsorship process. In previous testing, this additional step helped provide more information and resulted in more completed sponsorships than immediately beginning the checkout process. However, they still had a large amount of people who added a child to their “cart” but did not complete the sponsorship process. The team hypothesized that the button copy might be a good place to start. The current copy said “Select this child”, which was what the organization wanted the visitor to do. However, they hypothesized that this indirect copy might not give the visitor enough information about what was next—the checkout process. They changed the button copy to “Sponsor this child and check out” to give a sense of a “finish line” and launched a test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment produced a 9.7% increase in completed sponsorships, which indicated that this sort of copy gave the user more of a "finish line in sight". This new copy also provided a "micro-commitment" that gave the clicker some sort of obligation to follow through, where the previous copy, "Select this child", left the final step a bit less certain.

Future testing will revolve around increasing the value proposition to sponsor this child. Now that the micro-commitment results in more completed transactions, they need to get more people clicking that button.

experiments.title: How a reintroducing clickable looking text in an ad impacted donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that clickable text in an ad image for prospective donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/01/2021
In a recent study, Americans for Prosperity tested their way into a simplified ad experience with an ad image promoting a petition funnel that used what appeared like buttons (saying “Yes” and “No” in response to a question).
Unfortunately, this ad image was eventually rejected by Facebook due to it not adhering to ad policy guidelines that specifically prohibit images that have “fake buttons” on them to generate high clickthrough rates.
Therefore, it forced us to try another approach — one that looked like a banner, and another that looked like “clickable text” in response to the question the ad posed.
Which approach would produce the best donor conversion rate?
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 99.5% level of confidence, we observed a 238% increase in donor conversion rate when using the "clickable text" approach to the question-based ad promotion.
We also observed the following performance against the banner (control) approach:
+345% in ad clickthrough rate+261% in email signup rates
This ad image approach captured most of the essence of the original "fake button" approach we introduced and tested our way into. So long as your ad image is using what doesn't appear to be a button, it appears that you can meet the standards of the Facebook ad policy, while also maintaining the results that "fake button" approach would produce.

experiments.title: How simplifying your ad impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying the ad experience for prospective donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/17/2021
As a part of an open letter (or petition) promotion campaign, Americans for Prosperity wanted to experiment with simplifying an otherwise verbose and complicated open letter promotion.
They thought that by presenting a simple “Yes” or “No” question to the person the ad was being served to, that it could improve clickthrough and email signup rates, subsequently positively impacting donor conversion and revenue for the same dollar spent to advertise the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 96.2% level of confidence, we observed an increase in donor conversion by +64.4% by simplifying the ad experience in such a way that people would be presented with a simple "Yes" or "No" answer to a question, and would be called to take action today if they aligned with the organization (in this case "No").
In fact, we saw increases at every step of the funnel.
Here is the lift that we achieved across the treatment version of the campaign:
Ad CTR: +268.8% (LoC: 100%)Email Signup: +142.4% (LoC: 100%)Donor Conversion Rate: +64.4% (LoC: 96.2%)Revenue: +52.7% (LoC: 98%)
The results achieved with the simplified approach to the ad experience really started by dramatically decreasing the cost per click — showing a -80% decrease in cost per click while nearly quadrupling the number of clicks we saw against the control experience.
This higher volume of clicks for the same expense delivered similar more cost-effective results all of the way throughout the process, which boosted ROI by 100%, and decreased the cost to acquire an email by -69% and the cost to acquire a donor by more than -40%.
There was one more observation worth mentioning, as well. This approach had such an improvement in post engagement that it contributed to the lowering of costs for the outcomes we received. The question/answer-based approach created shares and comments that put this ad extended the reach of these campaigns. Specifically, we observed an 88% increase in post comments (LoC: 100%) due to this approach.
Where possible, experimenting with a very simple approach to issues related to a reader's beliefs in the form of a "Yes" or "No" question can potentially provide dramatic increases in clicks that the efficiency metrics on cost per outcome drop to levels not seen before.
It should be noted that Facebook has rejected some ad images that (like above) look like they have buttons on them. So, further experimentation may be required to use banners or something similar when/if future ads like this are rejected.

experiments.title: How asking a specific question in an ad impacts clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplified Facebook ads posing a relevant question for potential donors will achieve an increase in traffic to the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/27/2021
As part of a direct ask campaign for monthly donations, the Texas Public Policy Foundation wanted to test if specific policy ads would outperform a general invitation to join a branded recurring donor program. They had seen how other organizations had seen increases in traffic conversion through simplifying ads and wanted to test the concept on their images for the policy-specific ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 100% level of confidence, we observed a 1,223.4% lift in click rate on these Facebook ads that included a banner with the option to select "Yes" or "No" to indicate if they align with the organization's stance on specific policy areas. Once the visitors clicked on the call to take action, we were able to present them with the recurring donation ask to support TPPF's efforts in that specific policy area they just told us they have a shared belief.
What's interesting is that when we looked at our donor conversion rate on the donation page,  we actually saw an 80.5% decrease with 100% Level of Confidence—which means that now that we've determined how to increase the number of clicks in order to drive a substantial amount traffic to the page, we can now shift our focus to running experiments to improve conversion on the donation page.
While the increase in traffic is not necessarily producing visits from highly motivated donors to the page, we still see this as a worthwhile effort because once they visit the donation page we have the ability to set up additional campaigns designed to retarget them and find other opportunities to reach them distinctly with content related to their policy interests.

experiments.title: How raw links instead of buttons impact click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: What is the impact of using raw links in an email CTA?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/08/2021 - 03/19/2021
In an effort to continue to understand all of the tactics that help increase engagement and conversion in an email, we wanted to test using raw links instead of buttons for the CTA. Buttons have historically been used, but as we see the more human-centric approach to email has increased conversion, we thought the raw links would be a significant test to run since all emails have a CTA.
experiments.keyLearnings: The raw links increased click rate by 5%! This reinforces the idea that people are more likely to engage with emails that use human-centric tactics. A person is more likely to copy and paste a URL into an email they are sending you rather than insert a big, pretty button. People want to feel as if a PERSON is communicating with them and not an organization or marketing team.

experiments.title: How does reinforcing value proposition under the donation button affect conversion on a premium acquisition page
experiments.researchQuestion: Adding value proposition in the critical moment of decision will increase donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/09/2021 - 05/02/2021
FamilyLife was offer a premium book on parenting for a gift of any amount. The audience comes because of their interest in the book, but they also learn about how their gift to FamilyLife will help families that are broken or hurting. So we tested including that value prop underneath the donation submission button to see if in the moment of decision, reinforcing the fact that the donor doesn’t just get a book but helps families in critical need. This was run on a Facebook acquisition offer.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Our hypothesis held true that reinforcing the value proposition in the key decision moment did convert more donors, 75% more were converted by the simple message below the button. This held true with all devices, new and returning visitors, and all demographics.
The next step is to roll this out to all premium acquisition pages and test this on donation pages.

experiments.title: How visually changing the “Give Monthly” button impacts traffic to the donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: Would subtle animation on the Give Monthly donate button increase traffic to the monthly giving page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/26/2020 - 06/12/2020
On this organization’s website, they present two options for people to give, “Donate” and “Give Monthly”. We hypothesized that we could increase traffic to the monthly donation page by visually setting off the “Give Monthly” button. To do this, we created a subtle animation on the “Give Monthly” button that used a heart icon that pulsed once and the effect would then ripple across the rest of the button. We split the traffic 50/50 and ran this experiment across the website’s entire navigation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The subtle animation on the "Give Monthly" donate button increase traffic to the monthly giving page by 19%! We weren't able to validate this experiment off of monthly donations because the conversion rate on the page is too low to validate.

The subtle animation on the treatment caught people's eye causing them to click through to the donation page. We hypothesize that this tactic may be a novelty. We will continue to monitor it's effectiveness and retest the approach again in two months to see if the novelty has worn off, or if the subtle animation really is a longer term tactic to increase clicks to the donation page.

rows.name: You helped this single mom choose life

experiments.researchQuestion: Will having the CTA in the subject line increase conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/27/2020
For our bi-monthly email newsletter, I wanted to test including the call to action in the subject line. These newsletters are always sent from our CEO’s email address and I wanted to see if him saying the reader should do something would increase their likelihood of engaging with the content. For both versions, I used the same preview text which remains unchanged from newsletter to newsletter.
experiments.keyLearnings: Having the conversational CTA in the subject line increased not only the open rate, but the click through rate as well. The CTR increased 41%! People were primed from the subject line to click the articles in the Newsletter to read them on the site. This is something we will continue to test in future emails, especially when there is something important we want the reader to do.

experiments.title: How the reader’s motivation behind the call to action impacted the donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How does the “give to others” call to action compare to the “get more” call to action?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/04/2020 - 10/02/2020
As a part of experimenting with motivation on post-eBook signup donation pages for Americans for Prosperity, we decided to experiment with a “give” vs. “get” call to action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 97% level of confidence, we observed a +607% increase in donor conversion rate by setting up the problem and outlining our offer to help the reader solve it.
The difference between the two treatments was so wide and no further promotion of this offer is expected, after having achieved only about 16% of the minimum number of conversions we would normally like to see with an experiment of this type.
Therefore, further experimentation is required. With that said, the learning here might be that focusing on what you offer to the audience as a solution to a common problem they feel they are experiencing instead of getting information to other people might be more motivating moving forward.

Live Action was running a test on their main donate button located on their homepage. They wanted to see if users responded better to a stronger, but still direct ask (Support Live Action), or a value proposition (Defend the Preborn). All other elements of the page remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings: “Support Live Action” produced a 24% lift in donations as opposed to “Defend the Preborn.” This is most likely because a more direct ask produced more highly motivated traffic than a softer ask with a value proposition.

experiments.title: How an “Email Pause Button” affects unsubscribes
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding an option to pause email decrease unsubscribe rates?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/11/2020 - 09/14/2020
We often have people request to take a break from our marketing emails for a time. This is normally because they are in the midst of a busy season or they just accessed a resource that gave them tons of ideas and want to focus on those for a bit.
So we created an “Email Pause Button” to allow users to pause emails for a time instead of unsubscribe.
Version A was a just a normal email. Version B included the pause link near the unsubscribe link.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Interestingly enough, the option to pause email actually increased unsubscribes by 90% – the opposite of what we had intended. It's possible that this is because it drew more attention to the unsubscribe option and asked people to make a decision – where normally they may not have even been considering unsubscribing.

However, I don't think this rules out the pausing emails as an option. It may be more effective to weave it into the unsubscribe process as an alternative option to opting-out.

experiments.title: How visually presenting the donate button differently impacts traffic to the donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase traffic to the donation page by visually changing the look of the donate link in the primary navigation?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/20/2020 - 06/12/2020
On Care Net’s primary navigation, they provide a link that says “Donate”. This link takes people to their donation page where people can go and make a gift to support the organization. We hypothesized that presenting the link as a button and as an animated button might increase visual presence and ultimately increase traffic to the donation page resulting in more gifts and revenue. To test this, we developed two variations. The first variation was simply making the donate link, look like a button. For the second variation, we added subtle animation to the button that had the heart of the Care Net logo pulse once and the effect ripple across the rest of the button.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the experiment for two weeks, we found that by changing the donate link to a button negatively impacted clicks to the donation page. Additionally, the pulsing donate button had neither a positive or negative impact on clicks. We weren't able to validate the experiment off of donor conversion on the donation page, but we did we a directional 17% decrease in conversion from variation one - which is ultimately due to the significant decrease in traffic going to the page.
We hypothesize that the donate button saw such a negative impact in clicks because the use of buttons on their website, and across the majority of websites, is overused. People may actually be turned off by this. We believe this hypothesis to be true because of other testing we have done with the Care Net audience where we would test out of button and instead use hyperlink text. This would reinforce why the control with the underlined word "Donate" performed best in this experiement.

experiments.title: How adding a resource call-to-action button impacts donor conversion on the broadcast page
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase donor conversion by presenting the featured resource on the general broadcast page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/20/2020 - 05/10/2020
Focus on the Family gets significant traffic to their general broadcast page. This page shows people all of the available broadcasts they can hear/watch. Through previous testing, we learned that we could increase the number of people getting to the specific episode pages by adding a clear CTA to “watch this episode” on the general broadcast page. Knowing that every episode has a resource connected to it, we wanted to test the CTA idea we used increase the number of people watching the episodes to the resources as well. We developed a treatment that presented a CTA button on every episode featured on the general broadcast page that said, “Get today’s featured resource”. That button clicked through to the specific episode page and anchored to the resource.
experiments.keyLearnings: Donor conversion increased by 67% on mobile devices when we presented the resourced CTA on the general broadcast page. This tells us that a large volume of people coming to the broadcast page are either specifically looking for those resources and we were able to make the user experience better for them and get them what they were looking for more effectively, or we were able to convert the less motivated traffic into donors by showing them there is a specific resource tied to an episode they may be interested in. We are currently testing this same experience on desktop devices too.

experiments.title: How adding a call-to-action button on a Facebook ad affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will adding Facebook’s default call-to-action button affect conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/05/2016 - 05/19/2016
The Stanford Graduate School of Business was promoting their free eBook, Driving Innovation. A previous test had already shown the how clarity in advertising copy affects conversion rate in their Facebook ads. They wanted to test and quantify the impact of adding Facebook’s call-to-action button to the ad.
This button is a small grey button in the bottom right corner of the ad. Facebook allows the advertiser to select from several prewritten messages. In this case, the team chose to use “Learn More” as the copy, since they hypothesized that the viewer was at the very beginning stages of the conversion funnel.
They launched A/B ad test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The ads with the button showed a slight increase in conversion, though it was statistically valid. This tells us that the Facebook call-to-action button may present an actionable step in the conversion funnel for some users, as it causes them to make a mental “micro-yes” when they click “Learn More”. This doesn’t mean that they will definitely convert, but it does show that it slightly increases the likelihood of conversion.

However, due to a technical setup issue, the mobile version had a different experience. CaringBridge decided to re-test this to determine if the same results held for the mobile experience.
experiments.keyLearnings: On mobile devices, the tribute widget increased conversion 26.2% — which showed that the same "inbound" technique of getting the user to start their donation with a message to a friend was highly effective on mobile devices, where conversion is reduced anyways. This highlights a tactic that could be effective on mobile donation pages, which typically convert much lower than desktop donation pages.

experiments.title: The impact of Aligning Call to Action Text with the Donation Page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will an ad video’s CTA perform better if aligned with the final donation goal of the ad, or with the ad’s headline?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/03/2020 - 02/26/2020
We launched a new pledge to acquire monthly donors on Facebook. For this pledge, we decided to test the text in the ad video. Prior experiments showed us that videos performed better than images in our Facebook campaigns. So we wanted to find out what type of CTA in the video would work best. One that was the same as the headline of the ad, or one that was more aligned with the donation ask that would follow the pledge the ad invited people to sign.
We decided to do a 3-way test between “Sign the Pledge” “You can help save a baby…” and “Do you oppose abortion?”
experiments.keyLearnings: The "You can help save a baby from abortion" version received the most ad clicks. It also converted the most clicks to pledge signers and acquired the most new monthly donors (11 vs 8 on the control and 9 on the other variation). While the ad's headline invited viewers to sign the pledge, the "you can help save a baby" aligned viewers with the ultimate CTA of the donation page as well as appealed to their pro-life sentiments. They wanted to sign the pledge because they opposed the radical new pro-choice laws being passed and wanted to save babies from abortion. We are going to take this learning and see how we can incorporate even greater alignment in the ad copy with the donation page text.

experiments.title: How moving the call to action up on the page affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a slide out offer for the same CTA at the bottom of the screen increase the email acquisition rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/17/2016 - 05/24/2016
As part of the promotion of their Safe Bathroom campaign, Alliance Defending Freedom created a 10 minute informational video and then a pledge for visitors to sign. The primary goal of the page was to collect email addresses and our concern was that people would get distracted watching the video and not sign the pledge.
Based upon this concern, we decided to utilize a tactic that had been previously successful on the blog. We added a slide out version of the pledge that would show after 10 seconds. This way, visitors would see the pledge, even if they were watching the video. We made sure to exclude any visitors coming from DRTV given their differing motivations.
experiments.keyLearnings: It turns out that adding the slide out offer at the top of the page actually decreased email acquisition by 38%. Initially, this was a surprising result since it has seen so much success on the blog. However, after further contemplation, it would be our hypothesis that moving the offer up on the page made the ask before we had adequately explained the reason why. By adding this short cut, we reduced the clarity of the value proposition which led to the decrease in the conversion.

experiments.title: How strengthening clarity for the call to action increased donor conversion from an email

rows.name: From the Desk of Franklin Graham Control
From the Desk of Franklin Graham Treatment

experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase donor conversion with a radical, simplified version of the email?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/29/2019
Billy Graham Evangelical Association send out a monthly email called, “From the Desk of Franklin Graham”. This email serves two purposes – one, to ask people to pray, and two, to ask people to make a gift. When we analyzed what was currently being sent out, we hypothesized that a more simplified approach to the email may increase conversion. We hypothesized that making the email look and feel like it was actually from Franklin Graham may increase engagement and ultimately conversion. To do this, we removed the template of the email and made it more plain text, removed the headline, image, CTA’s buttons, and social sharing options. We then moved the premium off up into the main part of the body copy to connect the donation ask to the offer. No kept the same body copy of the email and didn’t add or change any of it. This approach was tested on 10% of the email file.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment version increased donor conversion by 75%! By simplifying the email and not making it feel like a templated, marketing email, people were more highly motivated to give. It removed all of the elements that distracted the reader from the message itself and clearly communicated what it was that we wanted them to do.

experiments.title: How adding clarity to a donate button affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will additional language clarify the recipient of the donor and reduce refund rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/17/2015 - 03/25/2015
CaringBridge launched a two-week fundraising campaign in March 2015, and was experiencing a higher-than-average refund rate for donations given during this time. From user feedback, it was apparent that donors weren’t sure where their money was going — to the individual or to the organization. The CaringBridge team decided to add a line of text under the donate button that reminded donors that their gift was going to power the organization’s platform, hypothesizing that added clarity would reduce the refund rate.
The following text was added underneath the donation button:
Your tax-deductible donation to CaringBridge powers our non-profit service of providing safe, protected websites for no charge. Every 7 minutes, a new CaringBridge site is created – along with a new compassionate community to surround that individual on their health journey.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment with the additional copy below the donate button did not effectively reduce refund rate — but it delivered a 31% lift in conversion! This showed that clarifying the recipient of the gift increased the clarity of the value proposition.  We’ve often heard that “clarity trumps persuasion” — and it proved to be true.
Upon further analysis, it was revealed that a large number of the refunds had come from a single user who had incorrectly promoted the page as a crowdfunding site, which helped to explain the increase in refund rate.

experiments.title: How a Clear Call to Action Impacted Monthly Donor Conversion on a Donation Page

rows.name: Clear 365 Days a Year Value Proposition

experiments.researchQuestion: Will stripped down value proposition with a clear impact CTA increase the number of monthly donors acquired on our donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/16/2019 - 08/13/2019
For several years, Care Net has used the same value propositional language for our monthly donor acquisition page.  Every January, we would send out a series of appeals seeking to raise new monthly donors, donors we called “Caring Partners.” As we prepared for the January 2019 campaign, we revisited the donation page and realized that the copy was full of organizational language that may add friction. We wondered if anyone really wanted to become a “caring partner” or even knew what that phrase meant. We also saw that we had copy dedicated to selling a list of benefits to becoming a monthly donor and wondered if those benefits really mattered. Did people become monthly donors because they wanted a “dedicated Care Net team member to serve them?” We stripped out these sections of the page and radically redesigned the copy. We boiled down the value proposition into one key statement: Save babies 365 days a year by becoming a monthly donor. We kept the copy focused on the donor and the impact of their gift of $1/day. We linked it to a concrete number of lives saved by monthly donors since 2008. We broke out the impact of the monthly gift using bullet points. We did not set up a straight AB test, however we had historic data from the page we used the prior campaign that we could use to compare to the new redesigned page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new page performed far and above the old one. It was so successful, that we kept using it in a wide variety of campaign offers which is why the traffic was so much higher than the control page. It is important to note that the variation had a conversion rate around 15% during the January campaign, the lower conversion rate reflects the average of all visitors to the page between Jan-August.
Few people are excited about becoming a monthly donor because of "benefits" Care Net provides. They want to save babies 365 days a year. This new value proposition was clearer and more appealing.

				× 51.0% increase in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: The impact of an image of a newborn baby on a homepage pledge CTA
experiments.researchQuestion: Would adding an image aligned with the content of a pledge offer increase conversions on our homepage?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/23/2019 - 05/31/2019
We’ve had great success with a featured pledge form and CTA on our homepage. Currently, we’ve been running a pledge against abortion and infanticide. We wondered if adding an image of a newborn baby inline with the text would increase conversions and/or donations to the landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings: People were far more likely to donate to save babies from infanticide and abortion when presented with an image of a newborn infant. This informs our decisions on upcoming offers on the homepage and the testing of images.

experiments.title: How emphasizing the donate button affects traffic to a donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: How will visually emphasizing the donate button affect traffic to the donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/27/2019 - 05/11/2019
Hillsdale College redesigned their site to a more visually-engaging website with moving video as a background image. However, in the redesign, the donate button had been given the same visual treatment—or equal weighting—as the rest of the navigation items. Their team hypothesized that calling the button out by giving it a white background would increase the traffic to the donation page and might result in additional revenue from people who previously couldn’t find it. They launched an A/B test with this simple addition to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The new donate button attracted 15% more traffic to the donation page. It is of note that conversion rate on the donation page did not increase—motivation stayed the same. However, there was a significant increase in revenue, though not statistically valid, due to more people finding the page.

experiments.title: How the navigational buttons impacts traffic driven to the donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will including donation links in the main navigation drive additional traffic to the donation page and, if so, how will it convert?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/26/2016 - 10/20/2016
We were able to statistically increase the volume of traffic sent to the donation page by 141% by adding a button to the main navigation. The button added to the secondary navigation did not statistically validate but seemed to indicate an increased volume too (it did not appear on mobile which limited its impact with the visitors).
However, while we were able to drive additional traffic, we were not able to significantly boost the number of donations made on that page. Since a navigation change like this would have proven a challenge to implement, it was decided that we would not implement the winner for such a minor lift to donations.
The biggest takeaway from this whole experiment was that simply presenting the donation opportunity was not enough with this audience. We will need to test more radical approaches to motivate them to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The Daily Signal website is a news site created by The Heritage Foundation as a purely educational outlet. There were email acquisition opportunities found throughout but donor conversion was not a central focus of the site. The only way to make a donation was by clicking on a simple Donate link found buried at the bottom of the site among 30 other links.

Recently, the development department was given the opportunity to introduce donor conversion opportunities to the audience. With the high volume of traffic to the site, we wanted to test the impact of simply adding a donation button to the main navigation. To ensure we adhered to branding guidelines, we developed two different treatments of the donation button; one in the main navigation and then the secondary navigation.

experiments.title: How the call to action of a Facebook ad affects clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: Which Facebook ad call to action will generate more clicks?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/15/2016 - 07/15/2016
NextAfter has a research blog we run Facebook advertising on to reach new audiences with our nonprofit fundraising optimization content. We wondered if simply altering the call to action on one of these ads would affect the click rate.
The control call to action was simply the name of the blog, while the treatment turned the call to action into an actual action saying “Learn How to…”
experiments.keyLearnings: We learned that altering the call to action to be focused on ‘learning’ creates a 74% drop in click rate. It’s possible that the call to action of “Learn How to Humanize Your Email Fundraising” did not provide enough clarity about the nature of the content. In contrast, the control of “5 Ways to Humanize Your Email Appeals” provided a clear description of the type of content, how long it was, and exactly what aspect of fundraising it applied to.

experiments.summary:

On the thank you page for a petition, we were making the primary call to action by a social share. We decided to test whether acknowledging their previous action and limiting the number of social networks would help increase the likelihood to share.
experiments.keyLearnings: In this instance, limiting their sharing options actually decreased their likelihood to share by 32%. While it is statistically valid, we would want to provide a larger audience and a longer period of time for the test to run before pulling any significant learnings from this test. Unfortunately, the promotion of this campaign ended and we were not able to do that.

experiments.title: How the absence of radio buttons on a Straw Poll affects name acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Would the absence of radio buttons improve conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/04/2016 - 03/08/2016
With the 2016 Presidential Election heating up, SBA List wanted to better understand where their audience stood with the Republican candidates. They put together a Straw Poll and invited their audience to cast their vote for who they wanted as the Republican nominee. Traffic was pushed to the Straw Poll by an email. After someone took the Straw Poll, there was a confirmation page with a donation ask.
The Straw Poll featured the candidates pictures with radio buttons next to them.  They tested this against a treatment with no radio buttons and just the candidates pictures. They A/B split the audience to determine which would perform better.
experiments.keyLearnings: While research has shown that radio buttons can create friction, they actually improved name acquisition for the Straw Poll. This suggests that the radio buttons presented a clear way for someone to vote. The Straw Poll had a very high conversion rate, but the absence of the radio buttons on the treatment may have created friction by confusing someone with how they were supposed to make their selection and vote.

experiments.title: How emphasizing a primary call to action in the navigation can affect donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Which website header design will lead to the most donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/08/2015 - 09/17/2015
Previous experiments with Jews for Jesus had proven that the audience making it to their primary donation form were highly motivated. They were achieving a ~70% conversion rate for those making it to the page which led us to the conclusion that we were losing some low hanging fruit along the path to a donation.
Based upon these conclusions, we decided to run some experiments to find ways to increase the amount of traffic making it to the donation page. Our first attempt was focused solely on the home page where we added some additional donation buttons but did not see any statistical change to the amount of traffic being directed.  From this, we learned we needed to broaden our focus beyond just the homepage.
For this experiment, we rearranged the header of the website to add an additional donate button with two different calls to action. We expanded the targeting of the test so that it included the newsletter and RealTime sections of their website which are frequented by the target audience for donor acquisition.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The “Make my Gift” button added to the header increased the traffic to the donation page by 39%. This increase in traffic resulted in a 74% increase in total donations since there was an increased number of visitors and they were more highly qualified based upon the button they had clicked.
What is interesting is that the “Donate Today!” button ended up decreasing the amount of traffic being driven to the donation page by 9.6%. We learned that by putting the call to action in the context of the donor “Make my Gift” instead of a command “Donate Today!,” the donors were able to align better action and were more likely to click.
experiments.fluxMetrics:
The Flux Metrics analyze the three primary metrics that affect revenue (traffic, conversion rate, and average
				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:
0% increase in traffic
				× 74.0% increase in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How the kind of buttons used for gift amounts affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: What kind of donation amount buttons would increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/27/2016 - 11/03/2016
Jews for Jesus has done a lot of testing on their primary donation page to see if they can increase their conversion rate. Because the conversion rate on this page is already so high, they have been testing radical treatments. In an effort to continue to optimize the page but better understand specifically what motivates their donors, Jews for Jesus decided to test only one variable on the page at a time. Their first test using this approach was the buttons used to select a donation amount. The control used radio buttons and the treatment used larger buttons with the giving amount on them. All other elements and copy remained the same. They split the traffic between the two versions and tested it.
experiments.keyLearnings: The larger buttons with the donation amount on them had a 22.9% increase in donor conversion! While we have seen these buttons work well with donors on eAppeal landing pages, we weren’t sure how they would perform with Jews for Jesus’ highly motivated donors that come to their primary donation page. The radio buttons on the control creates friction to the giving process because of how small they are to select. By making the clickable buttons larger and putting the gift amount on them, we were able to reduce this friction on the page and convert more donors.

Live Action was running a test on their main donate button located on their homepage. They wanted to see if users responded better to a stronger, but still direct ask (Support Live Action), or a value proposition (Defend the Preborn). All other elements of the page remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings: “Support Live Action” produced a 24% lift in donations as opposed to “Defend the Preborn.” This is most likely because a more direct ask produced more highly motivated traffic than a softer ask with a value proposition.

experiments.title: How a button’s value proposition affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Which language on the donate button most clearly communicates the unique value proposition of Online for Life?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/10/2014 - 07/22/2014
The general donation button on Online for Life’s website had always communicated a value proposition beyond simply giving a gift. The buttons message of “Save a Baby” communicated what their gift accomplished. However, we wanted to test whether this button copy was the best way to communicate both value and the purpose of the subsequent page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The “Give” button resulted in 41% less conversions and a 30.5% decrease in revenue. Removing the value proposition from the button ended up significantly reducing the visitors likelihood to give.
On the other hand, expanding the audience beyond just “Save a Baby” to “Save a Child” increased revenue by 62.2%. This simple change reminded donors of the long term impact of their gift. We save want to save a baby from abortion because of who they will become over time.

experiments.title: How using a “learn more” call to action in the Dear Reader ads decreased conversions and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Will softening the ask within the Dear Reader ads from “Activate your membership today” to “Learn more now »” improve overall donations or revenue, knowing that we will be sending far more traffic, albeit with lower motivation to activate a membership.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/17/2019 - 01/18/2019
In an attempt to drive a higher volume of traffic from The Daily Signal to the donation page from the “Dear Reader” ads, we wanted to experiment with softening the ask in the ad to entice higher click volumes.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 96.1% confidence score, we validated that the "Learn More Now »" call to action and softened ask from the Dear Reader ad decreased the donor conversion rate by 78%.
The experiment was turned off quickly because we also noticed that the revenue/visitor metric decreased by 86.6% (from $7.59 down to $1.01).
It is worth noting that we did see a dramatic improvement in click activity, where the following happened:
Control (Activate your membership): 32 / 9,850 = 0.32% ad clickthrough rateExperiment (Learn More): 147 / 10,249 = 1.42% ad clickthrough rate
That equates to a lift in ad clickthrough rate of 447%, but the decreases in donor conversion rates and revenue/visitor superseded the value of increased visitors to the donation page.
Perhaps experimenting with increasing the relevance of the message/ask on the instant donation page alongside the "Learn More Now »" message will improve donor conversion rates while keeping traffic volumes higher.

World Concern offers a Global Gift Guide that offers people the opportunity to give practical gifts like goats, water, medicine, etc. to people living in poverty. On the Global Gift Guide homepage, they give people the option to give a gift. We hypothesized that expanding the value proposition of the ask might motivate more people to want to give and increase conversion. We added value proposition to the headline and the body copy to test the impact that value proposition would have on conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: By adding value proposition on the homepage feature we were able to increase donor conversion by 35%! The reinforces the idea that the people coming to this homepage needed more clarity and understanding of what their gift would do before they actually made the decision to give.

experiments.title: The Impact of A Direct CTA in the first line of ad copy

rows.name: Do you want to be more like Jesus?
Get Your Free 40 Day pro-life devotional!

experiments.researchQuestion: What is the impact of having a CTA in the first sentence of ad copy on a Facebook content offer?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/06/2018 - 12/11/2018
We recently tested language in our Sanctity of Human Life Sunday Facebook ad. We personalized the copy of the ad and copied the CTA from the headline, to the first sentence of the ad copy. Our hypothesis was that people would see the first sentence of copy, before they saw the headline. We already knew from testing that having the CTA in the headline increased conversion, so we wanted to see if it would increase it here as well. The results were great. However, as we had also changed the copy, we did not know what impact was from the CTA being in the first sentence and what impact came from personalized copy. So, we decided to run the test again on a different content offer.
experiments.keyLearnings: Once again, we had a fantastic lift from adding the CTA to the first line of the ad copy. We did also change the button on the ad from "learn more" to "download." We will test that element alone in future tests to see how it performs. I will add that testing this on some offers has not shown as clear of an impact on conversion, but most have. The results here are from our Prayers for Life devotional ad test.
