experiments.title: The Impact of Making Two CTAs in one Appeal Pt. 2
experiments.researchQuestion: Would cutting down the copy on the Cut the Cord donation page, increase donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/28/2018 - 12/05/2018
We had already tried our new “Cut the Cord” appeal back in August to see if it would increase the number of monthly donors, Caring Partners, we acquired. It converted 60% less donors, but had a higher CTR than our control version. We wondered if the higher CTR indicated that leads were prepared to give, but there was too much copy on the donation page, causing them to drop off. We wanted to launch the test again, this time with less donation page copy to see if it increased the number of donors.
This time, the test would be run during one of our CYE appeals when we find donor response to be relatively high.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Once again, the CTR on the cut the cord email was higher than that of the control email (this time by 38%). Yet, even with shorter copy on the donation page, there were no gifts on the variation. Our control meanwhile brought in six donations. 

The fact that cut the cord was unable to get a single donation during our CYE campaign, while getting more clicks, indicates that the introduction of a second CTA (cutting spending on entertainment) introduced too much friction for the donor. We hypothesize that the increased CTR stems from the uniqueness causing people to click through to learn more about it.

experiments.title: How using value-focused language on a broadcast call-to-action button impacts clicks.

rows.name: For a Gift of Any Amount, Get Your Resource Here
Get Your Resource For a Gift of Any Amount Here

experiments.researchQuestion: Which button would increase traffic to the donation page and increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/28/2018 - 09/04/2018
On Focus on the Family’s broadcast pages, we found that we could increase traffic to the donation page by using value focused copy on the call-to-action buttons for the broadcast resource. That said, we also found that even though we were able to increase traffic to the page, we actually saw a decrease in donor conversion. We hypothesized that the original experiment didn’t provide enough clarity to the end reader which is why we saw the decrease in donor conversion. To build upon these learnings, we wanted to test adding more clarity to the buttons by telling them “for a gift of any amount” they could/would get the resource. We tested two versions of this approach, “For a Gift of Any Amount, Get Your Resource Here” and “Get Your Resource For a Gift of Any Amount Here”. These two buttons were tested against the current “Give Now” button.
experiments.keyLearnings: We increased traffic to the donation page with both treatments so we know that we can increase donation page traffic by continuing to use value focused copy. We saw a more significant increase in traffic (49%) when we led with the offer rather than leading with the donation copy (31%). While increased traffic is great, we ultimately want to increase donor conversion. Tracking for this experiment wasn't reporting accurately. We have since corrected this tracking and relaunched the test.

experiments.title: How removing friction and putting a donate button inline of site on a homepage can impact conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would moving the donate button increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/21/2018 - 04/26/2018
On the Boys Town homepage, we hypothesized we might be able to increase the number of people getting to the donation page by increasing the visibility of the donate button, removing other links that potentially created friction. We developed a treatment that moved the donate button from the very top of the page to close to the primary navigation, removed social sharing links, and added an additional opportunity for people to support Boys Town through their Family Home program.
experiments.keyLearnings: We were able to increase the number of people getting to the donation page by 36%. This increase in conversion didn't have a direct impact on donor conversion or revenue yet. But now that we know we can increase conversion to the donation page by reducing friction on the homepage and putting the donate button more inline with a person's sight, we will do testing on the donation page to help increase conversion of the additional people who are on that page.

experiments.title: How using value focused copy on a donation button can impact conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: Which button call-to-action would increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/05/2018 - 04/23/2018
Focus on the Family gets significant traffic to their broadcast pages on their website. On each episode page, they offer a featured resource that people can get when they make a donation to Focus on the Family. To get the featured resource, the button currently says, “Give Now”. We hypothesized that having a call to action that was focused more on what a person gets than what they have to do to get the offer might increase conversion. We developed a treatment button that said, “Get Your CD Here”.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment increased clicks to the donation page by 64.7%. While this is a great metric to look at, it doesn't tell the full story. We ultimately want to increase donations and revenue, not just clicks. When we analyzed the test results even more, we found that while the treatment significantly increased the number of people getting to the donation page, it decreased donor conversion by 4%. 

The increase in clicks tells us that people were more highly motivated to "get" the resource than to give a donation.

The decrease in donations hypothetically tells us a couple of things:
1. The initial messaging on the resource feature told people they could get the resource when they gave a gift could be strengthened. The donation part of that messaging may have gotten lost with the strong appeal of the button CTA
2. The messaging on the donation page could more clearly communicate how people can get the free resource by adding copy to this page and strengthening it's value proposition.

The approach that was taken with the treatment button copy on this broadcast page can potentially have significant impact on donations and revenue by testing out some of the new hypothesis that came from the results of this experiment.

experiments.title: How different button types in a giving array affect donation conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the colorful buttons for a giving array increase the conversion rate of the page over radio buttons?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/31/2013 - 11/06/2013
The Heritage Foundation utilizes a giving system that allows for the creation of a prepopulated gift array. We can make suggested gift options to the donors as a way to increase the average gift. Their giving system’s default display for giving arrays utilize radio buttons.  This feature is helpful but the method of display can present a challenge for some visitors. It was our hypothesis that radio buttons might be difficult to click on a mobile phone and might not be as visible as other elements on the screen.
We wanted to find keep the impact that suggested gift arrays provide while creating a better user experience for the donor. As are result, we created a new treatment in one of their fundraising campaigns that utilized colorful buttons in place of the radio buttons.
experiments.keyLearnings: The buttons were able to increase the conversion rate by 46.3%. While these buttons are a relatively small part of the donor experience, the selection of their gift is critical in the giving funnel. By making this easier to accomplish and more visually impactful, we were able to increase the number of donors that made a gift

To get more people to the donation page, they wanted to run an exit intent popup on the website. The control had “donate now” as the call to action, while the treatment directed people to “learn more.” The rest of the popup was the same.
experiments.keyLearnings: The Learn More CTA increased clicks by 223%. People are more likely to click through with a softer CTA.

experiments.title: How removing donation language from the CTA of an email appeal impacts click-through and donations.
experiments.researchQuestion: Which call-to-action would get more people to click through to the donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/18/2017 - 01/23/2017
Harvest Ministries monthly appeal message is always focused on the content of the premium they are giving that month. The premium is a gift to the subscriber if they give a donation. Because the message of the email is centered around the content of the premium, they wondered if they would be able to get more clicks and ultimately more donations if they made this the primary CTA as well. The control said, “Cold Case Christianity is our gift to you in thanks for a donation to Harvest Ministries today. You can get yours by going to this page” The treatment said, “You can get a copy of Cold Case Christianity by going to this page”. They A/B split their email file and ran the test.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment CTA had a 62.6% increase in clicks compared to the control. It is really encouraging to see such a significant increase in clicks; however, the ultimate goal is to get more donations. While we weren’t able to validate the donations, the initial data tells us that even though we had  more people clicking through to the donation page, these people didn’t make a donation. The treatment had a decrease in donations by 9.3% (80% valid).  The treatment CTA did reinforce the value proposition (which is why we had an increase in clicks), but it didn’t present clarity. When people clicked through from the email, they were probably expecting to just get the book/premium for free because there was no mention that they would get the premium when they gave a donation to Harvest Ministries. This shows the importance of being very clear in our CTA so that we don’t mislead people and they know what to expect during the next step in the giving process.

experiments.title: How adding urgency to a repositioned call to action affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will highlighting the urgency of a deadline increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/08/2015 - 12/09/2015
Harvest Ministries prepared an appeal for their year end campaign that offered a book, “The Biggest Story”, as a premium. They had prepared an email that told a personal story, made a financial ask with a call-to-action button, and then followed up with some information about the premium. On the landing page, there was a callout box that indicated that time was running out to order the premium and receive it before Christmas.
Their team formed a hypothesis that if they moved this callout earlier in the donation funnel, that they would incentivize more readers of the email to click through to the page. They developed a treatment that added urgent copy that emphasized the impending deadline, and moved the call-to-action button after this copy.  They then ran an A/B split test to see the results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This tells us that highlighting urgency on the landing page isn’t enough — we must move it earlier in the decision process if we want to motivate and incentivize the maximum amount of prospects. Because email performance rates decline naturally at each step (from delivery to open rate to clickthrough rate), we can reach more prospects with this message as we move it further down the inverted funnel. However, that doesn’t mean that we should move it to the subject line — when improperly placed, urgency can deceive and disincentivize prospects.

This also gives a valuable lesson about call-to-action placement. In the first email, the button was placed before the premium, which meant that many prospects made the decision before they were fully informed and incentivized. By moving the button to the end of the email (and strengthening the incentive that comes before it), Harvest was able to nearly double clickthrough rate.

experiments.title: How a direct call to action affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a more direct call-to-action increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/09/2016 - 03/21/2016
Harvest Ministries was testing the button text on the premium block on their homepage.  This block was used to ask for a donation in exchange for a premium offer, in this case, a DVD.
Since the offer was centered around a premium, the button text had been “get your copy”. However, the copy was clear that the DVD was given in exchange for financial support, so the Harvest team decided to test “donate” on the button text. If the motivation of their site visitors was aligned with charitable giving, they hypothesized that more people would click through, as opposed to the more eCommerce-focused call-to-action of “get your copy”.
experiments.keyLearnings: This reveals an interesting and powerful learning about Harvest site visitors: they come to the site with some sort of motivation to give. If Harvest site visitors had a lower motivation to give, they would most likely respond to some other copy like “learn more”, since they wouldn’t be prepared to give. However, the response to the “donate” call-to-action reveals that they are not repelled by a direct ask — in fact, they are more likely to take action.

On The Daily Signal, we had found that the “Dear Reader” style of donation ask provided the greatest volume of donations and revenue from TDS website. There was a concern that this style may begin to lose its impact over time so we wanted to see if we could get the same results (or better) by only showing it on the first article a visitor saw in a day and by making it appear on the page in a more dynamic fashion. The “more dynamic fashion” would be a slide down reveal once a visitor made it 20% down in the article.
experiments.keyLearnings: It turns out that reducing the frequency of the call to action decreased the donor conversion by 36%. While a drop in effectiveness is still a potential concern, it is something that will need to be monitored over time and addressed once we see that impact occurring.

rows.name: Show me how my friends score

experiments.researchQuestion: Does clarifying the intent of the social share button by indicating the outcome increase social sharing (and in doing so, increase name acquisition)?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/29/2014 - 11/11/2014
Heritage Action For America launched a name acquisition campaign through an online mini-game in 2012 called “How Conservative Are You?”. The game let people answer 10 questions about legislation, then compared them to their Congressman. The game also had a social element too — if shared on Facebook, the user’s friends could take the quiz and then compare scores. Heritage Action wanted to promote social sharing because of the multiplier effect it had on name acquisition.
We launched a test on the button that asked the user to share. The control asked them a question — “How conservative are your friends?” While that might pique the interest of the user, it’s unclear what the button does. We launched a treatment that teased the outcome of the social share — “Show me how my friends score”. We also clarified copy around the button to indicate what was happening.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment delivered a 214.7% lift in social sharing — which meant that not only did more people share it, more of their friends saw it. This brought a tremendous lift in traffic to the site, and more names were acquired as a result. It served as a great learning and reminder that at the process level of value proposition, using “outcome” language to earn the click can increase overall CTR.

experiments.title: How a button’s value proposition affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Which language on the donate button most clearly communicates the unique value proposition of Online for Life?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/10/2014 - 07/22/2014
The general donation button on Online for Life’s website had always communicated a value proposition beyond simply giving a gift. The buttons message of “Save a Baby” communicated what their gift accomplished. However, we wanted to test whether this button copy was the best way to communicate both value and the purpose of the subsequent page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The “Give” button resulted in 41% less conversions and a 30.5% decrease in revenue. Removing the value proposition from the button ended up significantly reducing the visitors likelihood to give.
On the other hand, expanding the audience beyond just “Save a Baby” to “Save a Child” increased revenue by 62.2%. This simple change reminded donors of the long term impact of their gift. We save want to save a baby from abortion because of who they will become over time.

experiments.title: How a softer call to action affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a softer call to action generate more donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/14/2016 - 03/18/2016
The Heritage Foundation was promoting their Membership Card to non-members giving them the opportunity to become a member.
Anticipating that the direct ask to “become a member” would receive fewer (but more motivated) clicks from the email, they wanted to test whether the added step and call-to-action to “confirm the name on your card” would generate more clicks, and result in more donations.
Since they weren’t making a direct ask, they created a different offer flow for this treatment. They created a two-step form for the treatment that started with a simple “confirm your name” step (pre-populated with the visitor’s name), and then took them to complete their donation. Because the visitor was indicating their intent to become a member by completing the first step, they removed much of the sales copy on the donation page in order to remove friction from the process.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The softer call to action generated a 54% increase in clicks, but a 51.6% decrease in donations. This revealed a key insight: all traffic is not the same. Visitors come with different motivations, and the traffic to the treatment flow was not at all “sold”.
This prompted a second test to see if reintroducing the value proposition copy on the follow-on donation form would affect the results.

experiments.title: How a softer call to action affects name acquisition rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a softer call to action like “Learn More” result in more course signups than an “activation” ask like “Get Free Access Now”?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/12/2015 - 11/16/2015
Hillsdale College had launched a new online course about the life and writings of acclaimed author and apologist C.S. Lewis. They wanted to promote these on Facebook, as they had done very successfully with other courses. But they wanted to better understand which calls to action drove the greatest amount of traffic to the page. Previously, they had used “activation” language like “Start Your Free Course Now” or “Get Instant Access Now”. They decided to test whether their audiences were actually motivated at that point in the funnel to “activate” — or if a softer call to action like “Learn More” would drive more traffic and conversions. So they set up two ads and set out to gather enough data to determine a result.
experiments.keyLearnings: We learned in a related test that the softer language drove more traffic. But what we learned here was that the softer language also drove a higher conversion rate. By giving people the correct “micro-yes” with a “Learn More” CTA, we actually can pique their curiosity and enroll more students from the ads.

experiments.title: General vs Specific: Does a Tangible Ask Increase Sticky Button Conversions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the sticky button ask from “Donate” to “Give Shoes” for website visitors will achieve more gifts and revenue because a specific, tangible ask makes the impact clearer and more motivating than a generic donation ask.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/10/2026 - 03/11/2026
Buckner is a humanitarian ministry organization with many varying initiatives and campaigns. We’ve recently been evaluating how to convert visitors to their websites into donors, and decided on a sticky donate button design. However, we wanted to test if a specific campaign call-to-action would outperform a general donation ask in driving donation conversions through the sticky button.
We tested two versions of a persistent sticky button:
Control: A heart icon with the text: “Donate”, leading to a general donation page supporting children and families
Treatment: A shoe icon with the text: “Give Shoes”, leading to the Shoes for Orphan Souls donation page (one of Buckner’s most recognizable giving opportunities)
Shoes for Orphan Souls is an evergreen campaign that is also currently promoted with Meta Advertising. It has a strong correlation to Buckner’s brand and a clear cost-per-impact, at $15 for a pair of shoes for one child.
Our hypothesis centered on whether specificity, especially tied to this well-known and emotionally resonant program, would make the giving opportunity feel more compelling.
This test ran for 30 days with a 50/50 traffic split on their general homepage and blog pages, excluding any sensitive blog topics or resources that usually avoid donation asks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We ran this test for 1 month, but the site traffic and sticky button conversion rates were too low for us to acquire enough samples for a valid result. The data we do have shows the specific treatment ask to Give Shoes was likely preferred over the control ask to Donate, but further testing is needed to see if this result is truly valid.
Key Learnings:
1.) Tangible framing may also influence gift amount - The treatment saw a higher average gift ($70 vs $50), suggesting that the specific impact framing anchored donors to give more generously, not just convert at higher rates. This suggests that reducing ambiguity at the first touchpoint  may increase both conversion and gift size.
2.) Familiar, campaign based asks can outperform generic ones - Shoes for Orphan Souls is a well-known and emotionally resonant program. This test suggests that leading with a recognizable and mission-specific initiative can outperform general appeals, especially on high traffic entry points like a homepage, where they may be motivated to give to that campaign in the first place.
3.) The Sample size limits confidence, but the direction is strong enough to warrant future testing - With only 8 donations, this test lacks the volume needed for a firm conclusion. However, the consistency of the lift indicates that this is a promising direction worth retesting or scaling in a new way.
In future testing, we would need to increase the sample size by running the element on more pages, and also track clicks on each CTA button to see if the landing pages had any effect on the conversion rate.
We should also test alternate elements such as a slide-up element to see if that would increase visitor engagement and get more samples for a test like this.

experiments.title: How adding clarity of the gift type to the submit button affects transactions (ICE Survey)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity for the gift type on the submit button for donors will achieve an increase in transactions because the donor more clearly understands the commitment of the gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/08/2025 - 09/04/2025
This experiment tested whether clarifying the gift type on the ICE Survey donation form’s submit button would increase completed transactions. The submit button previously did not specify whether the donor was making a one-time or recurring gift, which could introduce uncertainty at the point of decision. To address this, we ran an A/B test for just under 30 days, introducing a variation that clearly stated the gift type on the button (for example, “Make My Monthly Gift” or “Make My One-Time Gift”) to reduce friction and give donors more confidence in their commitment.
The goal was to see if clearer messaging on the submit button would lead to more completed transactions. This aligns with behavioral research showing that reducing uncertainty at critical decision points can increase conversion rates. The test measured the impact on completed transactions as the primary success metric, with button click-throughs as a secondary metric to understand engagement before completion.
If the treatment variation outperformed the control, it would demonstrate the value of transparent communication at the point of conversion. Insights from this test could inform updates to other donation forms and campaigns, ultimately helping drive overall donation growth. By clarifying donor expectations, we aim to create a smoother giving experience and encourage more supporters to complete their gifts.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment variation that added clarity about the gift type to the submit button resulted in a -63.7% relative difference in transactions compared to the control, indicating a substantial decrease in performance. The experiment reached a 93.1% confidence level, providing strong statistical evidence that the treatment negatively impacted conversions.

This suggests that adding gift type language to the submit button created unintended friction or confusion for donors, reducing their likelihood of completing the transaction. 

Future tests should focus on maintaining simplicity and momentum at the conversion point, while addressing commitment clarity at earlier, lower-friction stages of the donor journey.

experiments.title: How adding clarity of the gift type to the submit button affects transactions (Membercard)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity for the gift type on the submit button for donors will achieve an increase in transactions because the donor more clearly understands the commitment of the gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/20/2025 - 09/04/2025
The Heritage Foundation is urging its donors to refresh their membership cards as part of an effort to improve transaction completion.This experiment tested the hypothesis that adding clarity about the selected gift type (one-time or monthly) to the submit button on a donation page would increase completed transactions. The goal was to reduce any hesitation or uncertainty at the final step of the giving process by confirming to donors exactly what type of gift they were about to make.
We conducted an A/B test on the donation page from August 20, 2025 to September 4, 2025. The control version displayed the existing generic submit button “Make My Gift”, while the treatment version dynamically updated the button text to reflect the donor’s selected gift type (e.g. “Make My Monthly Gift” or “Make My One-Time Gift”). All other elements of the page remained the same. The primary success metric was completed transactions, with secondary metrics including form starts and abandonment rate.
This two-week A/B test provided insight into how small, high-visibility text changes at the point of conversion can influence donor behavior.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment were that adding gift type clarity to the submit button did not increase transactions as hypothesized; instead, it resulted in a -30.3% relative decrease in conversion rate with an 81.1% confidence level, indicating a directional but not statistically significant negative effect. 

Donors may have perceived the added detail as complexity or an unexpected change in their intended gift, creating last-minute doubt or second-guessing. Instead of offering reassurance, the extra wording may have disrupted their momentum toward completing the donation.

Future testing could explore lighter, more subtle ways to reinforce gift type clarity earlier in the flow rather than at the final submit button. This could preserve donor confidence without disrupting the decision moment at the point of conversion.

experiments.title: How changing the call-to-action on the homepage affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the CTA on the homepage banner for website visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because having the CTA be donation focused would give clarity and encourage users to give a donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/29/2025 - 05/02/2025
Every month, Time of Grace has a banner on their homepage of the premium running that month, with the CTA “Get the Book”. For this experiment, we wanted to change the CTA to be more focused on donating rather than it being focused on the book.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the CTA on the homepage banner to focus on donations instead of a book led to a significant increase in donor conversion rate. The treatment showed a 102.3% increase in Transactions for All Traffic with an 89.4% statistical level of confidence, indicating that the change was effective in encouraging users to donate.

In future experimentation, we should continue to explore the impact of donation-focused CTAs on user behavior and conversion rates. It would be beneficial to conduct additional tests with a larger sample size and over a longer period to validate the results and ensure consistency. Additionally, we can consider testing different variations of donation-focused CTAs to optimize and further enhance the impact on donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: Will adding value proposition to the donation button increase conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition for the homepage donate button will achieve more transactions because the added value proposition will reinforce the ‘why’.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/25/2025
For this test, we wanted to see if we could optimize the ‘Donate Now’ button that is on their homepage. To do so, we added some value prop language inside the button itself that will help reinforce the the ‘why’ to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a value proposition to the homepage donate button actually led to a decrease in donations, contrary to the initial hypothesis. However, the results are not statistically significant with a confidence level of 80.3%. Therefore, these results should not be used for future decision making.

Moving forward, it is important to conduct further experiments with a larger sample size or make adjustments to the value proposition language before drawing any conclusions. It is also crucial to ensure that future experiments reach a statistically significant level of confidence (at least 95%) in order to make informed decisions based on the results.

experiments.title: Will adding a button on mobile donation pages impact gifts?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that optimizing the donation page for mobile users will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the button will allow users to jump straight to the donation form instead of having to scroll.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/11/2025
Moody is running their Spring Share campaign which generates a ton of traffic to their donation pages from mobile.
For this test, we wanted to see if we could make the donation page be mobile first by adding a ‘start my gift button’ at the top of the page. When clicked the button will anchor a user down to the form.
Below is what we found
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that optimizing the donation page for mobile users with a 'start my gift button' did not achieve the desired outcome of increasing donor conversions. In fact, the treatment resulted in a decrease of 18.1% in donations for all traffic compared to the control.

Based on these results, it is clear that the 'start my gift button' did not have the intended effect on donor behavior. It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was below 95%, indicating that further testing would be necessary to validate these results.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing different strategies to optimize the donation page for mobile users. This could include exploring other design changes, such as simplifying the donation process, improving mobile responsiveness, or testing different call-to-action buttons. Additionally, conducting more rigorous testing with a larger sample size and longer testing period can help ensure more accurate and reliable results.

experiments.title: Is a graphic CTA better than a dear reader for non campaign season asks?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that replacing the graphic cta with a dear reader for the content pages cta will achieve more transactions because the dear reader is clearer to understand what the cta is and offers additional value proposition that can not be replaced by a graphic.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/24/2025 - 02/18/2025
For campaign seasons we’ve found that including a dear reader on the content pages significantly increases transactions and revenue. We wondered if we could use this same tactic during noncampaign seasons.
For this test, we replaced a content offer graphic with a dear reader to see if we could increase overall transactions and revenue. Below is what we found
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that replacing a graphic call-to-action (CTA) with a dear reader on content pages resulted in a 21.1% increase in Transactions for All Traffic, with an 88.3% level of statistical confidence. This indicates that the dear reader CTA was clearer and offered additional value proposition that resonated with users.

Moreover, there was a significant 319% increase in Transactions - One-Time, with a high level of confidence at 96%. This suggests that the dear reader CTA was particularly effective in driving one-time transactions.

Moving forward, it is important to note that while the results are promising, further testing is needed to validate the findings. The learnings from this experiment can be used in future experimentation by continuing to test the effectiveness of different CTAs, including dear reader messages, to optimize conversion rates and revenue. By iterating on these tests and analyzing the results, we can continue to refine our strategies and improve overall performance.

experiments.title: How including the benefits a person will receive to a landing page’s call-to-action affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe including the benefits a person receives to the call to action on an eBook landing page will increase the conversion rate because people will understand the value they receive.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/02/2024 - 08/06/2024
The Heritage Foundation was running an eBook offer using Facebook ads and prospecting emails to send traffic to a landing page where people could request their free copy of their 2024 Index of U.S. Military Strength eBook. In an attempt to increase email conversion rate on this page, they previously tested adding a curiosity-creating headline, as well as a preview of the content available in the eBook. Both of these treatments, however, resulted in a decrease in email acquisition rate.
As a follow up, they wanted to test if adding the benefits a person receives in the page’s call-to-action (CTA) would lead to an increase in email conversion rate.
The control’s CTA read: Get instant access to your FREE copy of the 2024 Index of U.S. Military of Strength by filling out the form below!
The treatment’s CTA read: Get instant access to your FREE copy of the 2024 Index of U.S. Military of Strength to discover the state of the U.S. military and what it means for your future.
The control and treatment each received 50% of traffic, and the experiment ran for one month.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding the benefits a person receives in the call-to-action on an eBook landing page actually led to a decrease in email conversion rate. The treatment group, which included the benefits in the CTA, saw a 10.4% decrease in Email Acquired for All Traffic compared to the control group. The statistical level of confidence was 87.3%, indicating that the results are not yet fully valid but are approaching validity.

Moving forward, it is important to acknowledge that in this specific case, highlighting the benefits did not have the intended effect of increasing conversion rates. This information should be used to guide future experimentation by considering alternative strategies for optimizing the landing page, such as testing different messaging, design elements, or incentives to encourage email sign-ups. It is also crucial to continue testing and iterating on different approaches to find the most effective way to drive conversions for the eBook offer.

experiments.title: Will aligning the value proposition from the donate button and the episodic giving page increase transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that aligning the value propostion from the donate button to the doantion page for main episodic donation page will achieve a higher number of transactions because the congruency between the button and the donation page will decrease friction and reinforce motivation.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/04/2024 - 11/01/2024
In this series of testing we have found that using Biblical Resources on the main donate button increases the number of clicks to the donation page.
We saw that aligning the value proposition with the main donation button on the monthly page increased transactions by 154%.
We wondered if we could duplicate those results for the episodic donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that aligning the value proposition from the donate button to the donation page for the main episodic donation page led to a 13.7% increase in transactions for all traffic. This indicates that having congruency between the button and the donation page can potentially decrease friction and reinforce motivation for users to make donations.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue exploring the impact of aligning value propositions on donation pages to see if this trend holds true across different scenarios. Conducting additional tests with larger sample sizes and varied approaches to alignment could help validate these findings further and provide more conclusive evidence. It is important to note that while the results of this experiment are encouraging, they do not meet the standard threshold of 95% statistical confidence, so further testing would be necessary to confirm the effectiveness of this strategy.

experiments.title: How reordering a headline’s CTAs impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that rewording the headline to make the primary focus the campaign for October Scripture Memory landing page visitors will achieve an increase in sign ups  because making the campaign the focus relieves the privacy concerns and concerns of spam that are often associated with texts from an organization.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/30/2024 - 10/11/2024
Every month, The Navigators have been running a Scripture Memory Text Campaign to help their audience memorize more scriptures.
However, there has still been some confusion that this is a text campaign – a series of 7-8 texts, 1 per day. Therefore, their team decided to change the headline to read: “Sign up for Text Messages and join us for a Scripture Memory Challenge!” in an effort to emphasize participants would receive daily tests.
We hypothesized that this change would result in a lower number of sign ups due to the perceived level of commitment, privacy concerns, and spam all associated with texts.
So, we decided to test it against a new headline that focused on the Scripture Memory campaign first, rather than just signing up for texts.
Our goal was to get people bought into the campaign first then agree to receiving text messages.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the headline to focus on the campaign itself rather than the text messages resulted in a slight decrease in sign-ups, indicating that visitors were not impacted, neither positively or negatively, by the headline asking them to sign up for text messages first. While these results did not validate, it is a helpful learning that The Navigators audience did not have a heightened or lessened motivation to sign up by the commitment to texts.
In the future, it may be beneficial to strike a balance between promoting the campaign and addressing potential privacy concerns. A revised headline that emphasizes the benefits of the Scripture Memory Challenge while also addressing privacy and spam concerns could potentially yield better results. Additionally, it's important to ensure that any changes made are tested with a higher level of statistical confidence before implementing them on a larger scale.

experiments.title: How a skip the donate page button impacts donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a “skip to my course” option for instant donation page viewers will achieve the same amount of donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/13/2024 - 09/10/2024
DTS offers free online courses as content offers. When somebody signs up for a free course, they then go to a instant donation page. This has causes some course enrollees to get confused and email DTS asking why they need to pay for a free course. We decided to do an A/B/C test and test putting “You can access your course immediately by clicking here.” in the copy and adding a button to the bottom of the page that says “No thanks, take me to my course now.” We wanted to see if adding clarity would impact donations and impact the amount of people emailing DTS.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a "skip to my course" option on the instant donation page did not lead to the same amount of donations as the control group. In fact, the treatment group saw a significant decrease in transactions for all traffic. While the results are not statistically significant at the 95% level, they are approaching validity at 86.9%. This suggests that further testing would be necessary to confirm the impact on donations.
In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the messaging and design of the instant donation page. While clarity is crucial in guiding users to their desired action, such as accessing their course immediately, it is also important to balance this with the donation call-to-action. It may be beneficial to test different variations of the messaging and design to find the optimal combination that drives both course access and donations.
Overall, the results from this experiment provide valuable insights for future testing and optimization of the instant donation page to improve user experience and donation conversion rates.

experiments.title: How Does adding Consistent Value Language on the CTA Button Affect Conversions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that aligning the call-to-action value claims for the main navigation donate buttons will achieve a higher conversion rate because there is less friction for the donor. They will no longer have to choose which initiative they would want to support.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/03/2024 - 07/30/2024
Our first test in this series was seeing if aligning the CTA on the duel donation buttons would make an impact. You can read that test writeup here: https://app.winstonknows.com/experiment/8277.
For this test, we wanted to see if aligning the value proposition on the buttons could increase transactions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment conducted on the FOTF homepage showed that aligning the call-to-action value claims for the main navigation donate buttons resulted in a 61.3% increase in Revenue for All Traffic. While this result is statistically significant, the confidence level of 84.0% falls slightly below the threshold of 95% for validity.
The key learning from this experiment is that aligning the call-to-action value claims can potentially reduce friction for donors and lead to an increase in conversions and revenue. With the directional lift in transactions and revenue by using Biblical resources this variant will become the control for the next iteration of testing.

experiments.title: How adding a flag icon to a donation form gift array button affects donation rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a small American flag in a donation form’s gift array and submit button to build affinity for direct appeal prospects will achieve an increase in conversion rate because the flag image will help align their motivation with the intended action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/30/2024 - 05/30/2024
The Heritage Foundation had previously run a test on their membership renewal page where they added a small flag image to a tabbed donation form’s monthly gift tab in order to appeal to the patriotism of their donors, which led to an increase in donations.
The Heritage team wondered whether this flag image would appeal to potential donors on another direct appeal donation page. They believed testing this image in the donation form’s $100 gift array button and in the form submit button would lead to an increase in donations, due to both the image’s appeal to the donors’ patriotism and the suggestion of $100 as the “preselected” array option.
They launched an A/B test where the control had no flag icon anywhere on the form, and the treatment had the flag icon only in the $100 gift array button.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a small American flag image to a donation form's gift array button may not always lead to an increase in conversion rate as expected. In this case, the treatment actually resulted in a decrease in Transactions for All Traffic by 42.6% with an 88.3% statistical level of confidence, indicating that the control performed better.
This result may indicate that the flag image was a distraction from the task at hand for this direct appeal. The image on the gift array button specifically may have confused or dissuaded some potential donors who planned on giving an amount other than the preselected amount.
In future experimentation, it would be important to further test the impact of adding the flag image in different elements of the donation form, such as the monthly tab as well as the submit button, or even testing different patriotic symbols or messaging to see if that resonates better with potential donors.

experiments.title: How using donor autonomy in a CTA impacted clicks on a survey
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that giving survey takers s a choice rather than a command for housefile email recipients will achieve an increase in clicks because there is an improved sense of autonomy in the process, giving the potential donors ownership.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/18/2024 - 05/20/2024
The concept behind donor autonomy is that people like to make choices for themselves, rather than being told what to do. When applying this concept to online behavior, we wondered if using language in a CTA that gave the email recipients the chance to indicate their willingness to participate, rather than instructing them with a command would have any influence on the click rate to complete a survey. We split traffic 50/50 between each version.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment indicate that giving survey takers a choice, rather than instructing them with a command, resulted in a 5.9% increase in clicks for all traffic. This suggests that there is a positive impact on engagement when potential donors feel a sense of autonomy and ownership in the process. However, the statistical level of confidence is below 95%, indicating that further testing would be necessary to validate these results.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing variations of language that offer choices to recipients rather than giving commands. In our next test, we will build on the concept of donor autonomy by including the option to say "no" rather than only including one place to click and say "yes" to the request, which may lead to an even greater sense of agency for the email recipient and potentially increased engagement.

experiments.title: How using donor autonomy in a CTA impacted clicks on a survey

rows.name: Click here to begin your survey now
I'm willing to give my feedback

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that giving survey takers s a choice rather than a command for housefile email recipients will achieve an increase in clicks because there is an improved sense of autonomy in the process, giving the potential donors ownership.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/18/2024 - 05/20/2024
The concept behind donor autonomy is that people like to make choices for themselves, rather than being told what to do. When applying this concept to online behavior, we wondered if using language in a CTA that gave the email recipients the chance to indicate their willingness to participate, rather than instructing them with a command would have any influence on the click rate to complete a survey. We split traffic 50/50 between each version.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment indicate that giving survey takers a choice, rather than instructing them with a command, resulted in a 5.9% increase in clicks for all traffic. This suggests that there is a positive impact on engagement when potential donors feel a sense of autonomy and ownership in the process. However, the statistical level of confidence is below 95%, indicating that further testing would be necessary to validate these results.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing variations of language that offer choices to recipients rather than giving commands. In our next test, we will build on the concept of donor autonomy by including the option to say "no" rather than only including one place to click and say "yes" to the request, which may lead to an even greater sense of agency for the email recipient and potentially increased engagement.

experiments.title: How changing the color of form donate buttons impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the color of the donate button for donation forms will achieve a decrease in donor conversion because the color is darker than the previous and is more difficult to read.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/19/2024 - 02/21/2024
Dallas Theological Seminary is celebrating their centennial this year and for it, they had an update to their brand.
For this test, we wanted to see if the updated colors would impact overall donor conversion due to the new yellow/gold being darker than the previous.
We ran this test across all donation pages, including acquisition pages and the main donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:

1. The updated color of the donate button for donation forms resulted in a 9.9% increase in One-Time Donations for All Traffic.

2. Although the increase in One-Time Donations was statistically significant at 81.4% confidence level, it did not meet the standard threshold of 95% confidence for valid results.

3. While the darker color of the donate button may have initially been thought to be more difficult to read and potentially decrease donor conversion, the results showed a positive impact on conversions and revenue.

In future experimentation, it is essential to continue testing variations in colors, designs, and elements on donation forms to understand their impact on donor conversion and revenue. Additionally, it is crucial to ensure that experiments are run for a sufficient duration to gather reliable data and reach a higher level of statistical confidence before making conclusive decisions based on the results.

experiments.title: How a high contrast donate button in the main navigation of a website affects conversion and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a high contrast donate button to the main navigation for heritage.org visitors will achieve a lift in transactions and revenue because it helps website visitors visual interpret the webpages hiearchy of purpose and breaks down their decision process to decide to give a gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/05/2024 - 03/07/2024
After successfully testing the addition of a red donate button in the main navigation on the homepage of heritage.org we decided to run the test side wide to Heritage.org visitors in a 50/50 split. The test excluded the homepage from results. This experiment validated several times and ran for over a month, but for logistical reasons it was kept running. While there was lift among all website visitor types, (Email, Social, Search, Returning, and NEw Visitors, etc), the largest lift came from organic traffic to the home page, which created a 55% lift in transactions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. Adding a high contrast donate button to the main navigation for heritage.org visitors resulted in a significant increase in revenue for all traffic, specifically a 24.9% increase. This indicates that the addition of the donate button had a positive impact on user engagement and conversion rates.

2. The treatment also led to a 55% lift in transactions from organic traffic to the home page, showing that the donate button was particularly effective in capturing the attention of visitors coming through this channel.

3. Although the results showed a high level of statistical confidence (85.2%), further testing is recommended to ensure the validity of the findings. The statistical confidence level of 95% was not reached in this experiment.

4. It is important to note that the treatment resulted in a 4% increase in bounce rate. While this may be a concern, it could also indicate that the donate button led to more engaged visitors who explored the website further before exiting.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to conduct additional tests to validate the impact of the donate button on revenue and transaction rates. It may also be worth exploring different variations of the donate button, such as varying the placement or color, to determine the most effective design for encouraging donations.

Overall, the results of this experiment suggest that the addition of a prominent donate button to the main navigation can potentially improve user engagement and increase revenue for Heritage Foundation.

experiments.title: How adding a donate button to the header and navigation menu affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donate button to the header and navigation menu for home page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because more people will click to view the donation page. because more people will click to view the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/15/2023 - 12/11/2023
The Daily Signal is The Heritage Foundation’s multimedia news platform. On their website, the only call-to-action to donate and support the Daily Signal’s work is located at the very bottom in the footer of each page and doesn’t stand out.
In an attempt to increase the amount of traffic to the donation page from the home page, we wanted to test if adding a more prominently displayed donate button to the top of the home page led to an increase in donor conversion rate.
The donate button appeared in both the home page’s header and dropdown navigation menu (in both the desktop and mobile experiences) and was blue in color in order to stand out from the surrounding buttons. 50% of home page traffic saw the control and 50% saw the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for 27 days, in which time the donate button variant saw a 42.8% increase in donations for all traffic compared to the control. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this increase is 53.2%, meaning that the results cannot be considered valid or reliable for future decision making.

In terms of revenue, the treatment saw a decrease of 0.11% in average gift, but at a 6.52% level of confidence these results cannot be relied upon to predict a valid outcome.

Reasons for this may include the donate button appearing less hierarchically prominent than the email "Sign Up" button above it, or home page visitors not yet having experienced the value in the Daily Signal's articles before seeing a donate button.

Based on the results, it is clear that additional testing is needed to understand what motivates The Daily Signal visitors to donate to the organization. This could include adding value proposition copy next to the button, or testing different locations on the home page. It may also be worth testing an unobtrusive sticky donate button that follows the visitor through their journey sitewide.

experiments.title: How making a statement call to action with lapsed members in a fundraising email affects the click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making the call to action a statement instead of a question for lapsed members will achieve a higher click-through rate because the desired action is cleaner.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/22/2024
This email experiment was conducted for The Heritage Foundation’s Founder’s Day campaign.  The email was a personal communication informing lapsed members about the challenges America faces today and how The Heritage Foundation is solving those issues. At the end of the email, they ask lapsed members to “reactivate” their membership.
We wanted to know if using a statement call to action would encourage lapsed members to click instead of a call to action that asked a question.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that making the call to action a statement instead of a question for lapsed donors resulted in a 28.0% increase in the click-through rate. While this result is promising, the statistical level of confidence is below 85%, which means the results cannot be considered valid.
Note: While the click-through rate increased the conversion rate decreased by 50%. The sample size was not large enough to attain the required level of confidence of 95% or above.
In the future, it is essential to conduct further experiments with a larger sample size to obtain more reliable results. Additionally, it is crucial to continue testing different variations of the call to action to determine the most effective approach for encouraging lapsed donors to re-engage. By using what was learned from this experiment, we can refine our strategies and optimize our communication to better connect with our audience and achieve our goals.

experiments.title: How adding an anchor button on an experience page impacted conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding an anchor button at the top of the page for web traffic will achieve a higher conversion rate because it makes the offer more easily accessible.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/30/2023 - 11/22/2023
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
For November 2023, PTV released a brand-new children’s book by Julia Jeffress Sadler. To promote this page, PTV built an experience page with tons of graphics, testimonials from readers and images of the interior of the book. At the bottom of the page is where the web visitor can select the offer bundle they would like to receive with their generous donation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment was a decrease of 11.6% with a 92% level of confidence in transactions.
We believe that the version with the button actually caused web visitors to miss all value proposition for the offer and made it more transactional in nature.

experiments.title: How aligning call-to-action verbs in a tribute widget impacts giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that aligning all the call-to-action words in the triibue widget for site visitors will achieve an increase in donations because there will be additional clarity and affirmation in the conversion process.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/07/2023 - 11/10/2023
CaringBridge’s users heavily rely on the tribute widget in order to make a gift in honor of their loved one. In order to increase conversion rate, we sought to add clarity to the pre-existing copy by using consistent CTA’s and driving the desired action: give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that aligning all the call-to-action words in the tribute widget did not result in an increase in donations. In fact, there was a decrease of 9.3% in donations for all traffic. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence is below 85%, which means these results cannot be considered valid.

Additionally, we saw the results vacillating between flat and a slight decrease throughout the experiment. This suggests that creating congruency in the call-to-action words for the tribute widget is not a significant motivating factor for journal page viewers. They likely already have enough clarity from the existing copy about what the tribute widget is asking of them

It's suggested we test other concepts in the copy such as an emotional appeal to see if that will drive an increase in giving.

experiments.title: How a premium offer dear reader CTA on podcast pages impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a dear reader premium offer on podcast pages will increase donor conversion
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/17/2023 - 08/14/2023
FamilyLife has a number of popular podcasts with thousands of visitors a month coming to their site to tune in. In reviewing these podcast pages, we hypothesized that we could provide further value for visitors and increase donor conversion by presenting a premium offer in a “dear listener” message on the page.
In our prior testing, we found that an end-of-article dear reader would increase donor conversion on article pages by reducing the friction of making a gift and increasing the appeal of the donation CTA through more space for value-driven copy. We believed that a dear listener message would achieve the same result by providing a way for listeners to engage in a deeper way with what they were hearing and receive a valuable resource in exchange for their gift.
We selected their most popular premium offer, Seeing Beautiful Again and created a dear-listener message on the podcast pages to promote it. We split traffic evenly and ran the experiment for a full month.
experiments.keyLearnings: The dear listener message improved donor conversion significantly. While we weren't able to validate this increase to a 95% or greater level of confidence, the lack of a negative impact from the treatment makes us confident that featuring a dear-listener premium offer is more likely than not to improve donor conversion. For our next experiment we will test this premium offer against a different one to see which sort of offer best aligns with the visitors to these podcasts. We will also continue to test the placement of the message on the page to see how that impacts donor conversion and user friction.

experiments.title: How does reinforcing the security of donation information with an impact statement beneath the donation button affect donation rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a more prevalent security-themed statement below the donate button will increase donations by anticipating security concerns and overcoming hesitancy at the point of completing the transaction.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/16/2023 - 04/01/2023
Care Net is an organization that provides support and resources to women and men considering abortion.
They have been acquiring donors online for many years now but have seen the number of donations/donor conversion rate drop over the last several months.
Many donors feel hesitant or spend the majority of time in the “consideration” stage of a transaction at the button. This is where the momentum most often stops. How would adding a short statement that reinforces the security of the donation affect donation rates?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. The inclusion of a security-themed statement below the donate button had a negative impact on donation rates, with a significant decrease in donations for the treatment.

2. Although the results of this experiment are not statistically valid due to a level of confidence below 85%, the consistently negative trends observed across multiple metrics and segments indicate that caution should be taken when considering the implementation of a security-themed statement in future experimentation.

Based on these learnings, it is recommended to further investigate and address the underlying drivers of hesitancy or concerns among potential donors. Future experimentation should focus on optimizing the donation process, simplifying the user experience, and building trust through other means such as social proof, testimonials, or transparency in how donations are used.

Additionally, it may be valuable to conduct user research or surveys to better understand the reasons for hesitancy and gather feedback from potential donors. This qualitative data can provide insights into the specific concerns and help inform future experimentation and optimization efforts.

experiments.title: How reducing the perceived investment of a call to action impacts account creation rate (Pt.3)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that prompting people to create a “page” or “journal” instead of a “site” will increase site creation rate because there will be a reduced perception of work involved which will reduce anxiety as well.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/14/2023 - 07/02/2023
CaringBridge has been running ebook offers where they prompt the ebook downloader to create an account with their organization. They have previously used the call to action, “Create a site” to communicate the breadth of what CaringBridge offers when you create an account to share yours our your loved ones health journey. Our hypothesis was that the language to “create a site” may have the perception that they’re about to make a large commitment. Creating a site may sound tedious, laborious, or intimidating. We wanted to reduce any anxiety about the process by simply changing the word “site” to “page.” This phrase is still accurate but reduces the perceived investment and commitment they’re about to make.
When we ran the first iteration of this test we ran the experiment on one offer. We saw a valid lift when using “page.” Before rolling it out to all offers, we decided to test it among all offers and add another variant using “journal” to see if that would also reduce anxiety. In the second experiment, we saw a decrease in conversion rate when using “page.”
In this particular experiment we tested this concept on a separate offer that used slightly different verbiage in the button and started with the language “page” under the first assumption that people responded more to the use of “page”. Therefore, the control is “page,” treatment 1 is “site,” and treatment 2 is “journal.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment affirms what was uncovered in experiment #2 - "page" (the control in this experiment) is not universally beneficial. In fact the usage of "site" resulted in a 93% increase in site creation with a level of confidence of 80%. While the use of "journal" resulted in a 138% increase in site creation with a level of confidence of 91%.
Based on this, it would be beneficial to continue testing the use of "journal" as a potential alternative to "site" or "page".
Overall, these findings suggest that there is value in exploring alternative language options to reduce perceived commitment or anxiety when prompting users to create a site on CaringBridge.

experiments.title: How a pulsing heart sticky donate button affected donations on homepage
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a side donate button for homepage visitors will achieve higher conversion in donors because the side button has a pulsing sacred heart and is eye catching.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/13/2023 - 07/03/2023
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
In an effort to increase donations from the Spiritual Directions Homepage we decided to test adding a side sticky donate button with a pulsing heart.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
1. The treatment version with the side donate button featuring a pulsing sacred heart significantly increased donations, with a 193.6% increase for all traffic.
2. The treatment version had a higher level of confidence (91.2%) compared to the control version, indicating that the results are approaching validity.
3. When looking at specific visitor segments, the treatment version showed a significant increase in donations for desktop visitors (5903% increase with 97% confidence level) and email visitors (9% increase with 96% confidence level).
4. The changes made in the treatment version, including the addition of the side donate button with a pulsing sacred heart, seem to have a positive impact on donor conversion.
To use these learnings in future experimentation, it would be beneficial to conduct further tests to validate the findings and increase the level of confidence. This could involve testing different versions of the side donate button, such as variations in size, color, or animation. Additionally, exploring other elements on the homepage that could be modified, such as placement or wording of the donate button, could provide further insights into increasing donations. It's also important to consider the specific characteristics of different visitor segments and tailor the donation strategy accordingly, as seen with the significant increase in donations for desktop and email visitors in this experiment.
When results of the blog page experiment is combined with the blog pages results of the same test (https://app.winstonknows.com/experiment/6142) we find a 66% increase in donations with a 94% level of confidence

experiments.title: How a Wiki-Style Dear Reader impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a Wiki-Style Dear Reader on Hour of Power‘s daily devotional pages will achieve an increase in donor conversion because it is more visually arresting and transforms into a sticky bar if the user scrolls past it.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/24/2023 - 04/30/2023
Hour of Power is a Christian broadcast ministry that shares inspiring sermons, ebooks, and daily devotionals through their media presence and website. They have seen success in increasing email acquisition on their website’s daily devotional pages by adding Dear Readers below the devotion, and for their spring high-urgency campaign we decided to see how their traditional Dear Reader asking readers to become sustaining monthly donors would perform against a Wiki-Style Dear Reader.
The traditional Dear Reader appears on devotional pages underneath the devotion. The Wiki-Style Dear Reader appears closer to the top of the page and is above the devotional content. If a user scrolls down the page and goes past it, the Wiki-Style Dear Reader will disappear and a smaller version of it will appear at the bottom of the screen like a sticky bar.
The traditional Dear Reader only has one CTA button which brings the user directly to the campaign’s donation page, but the Wiki-Style version has two CTA buttons. The first button also leads the user directly to the donation page, but the second button that says “Give a Different Amount” opens up a small gift array within the Wiki-Style Dear Reader. The user can then select or enter the amount they would like to give and then they will be redirected to the donation page with their selection automatically chosen.
Our hypothesis was that new placement and style of the Wiki-Style Dear Reader would be more arresting and eye-catching for users on the page and would thus increase donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment did not fully validate, but we saw a decrease in donor conversion on the Wiki-Style Dear Reader with a 90% LOC. What this reveals about Hour of Power's audience is that they do not respond as well to arresting popups/sticky bars that use high-urgency language. Instead, it shows us that their audience is more responsive to CTAs that take a conversational approach.

In future testing we can try using the same kind of conversational language in the Wiki-Style that we use in the Dear Reader to clarify if their audience wasn't responding because of the language used in the Wiki-Style or if it was due the placement/presentation of the Wiki-Style.

experiments.title: How a sticky donate button on article pages with a slide-out donor appeal impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a sticky donate button with a slide-out CTA featuring additional value proposition copy will increase donor conversion by strengthening the value proposition of the sticky donate button.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/23/2023 - 03/31/2023
FamilyLife is a Christian ministry serving families worldwide. They had previously tested a sticky donate button on article pages that floated on the right-hand side of the page with a pulsing heart and linked to the main donation page. This sticky button improved donor conversion. They wanted to test a new version of that button with an expanded value proposition that would appear in a slide-out when the button was clicked. This they posited would enable visitors to receive a reason to donate before visiting the donation page itself. Visitors could then choose to give or dismiss the message altogether.
For the experiment, they wanted to test two different value propositions. One would focus on FamilyLife’s standard appeal message about making a gift to support families in crisis. The other would focus on the perceived value the visitor received from FamilyLife this year and ask them to make a gift to keep FamilyLife here for them and their family.
Originally these two variations were going to be tested against the prior winning sticky donate button that lacked a slide-out CTA message. Due to a setup error, the control experience lacked a sticky donate button altogether, so it became an experiment between these two competing value proposition slide-outs and no sticky donate button.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We found that inviting visitors to reflect on the value FamilyLife provided them and then asking them to donate to keep FamilyLife there for them and their family was far more motivational than asking them to make a gift to help other families. We hypothesize this is because it is harder for a potential donor to think about the needs of unidentified "families in critical need" than it is to remember their needs and how FamilyLife has been their for them.
We will continue testing this value proposition to further refine it and identify key motivational elements for donor conversion. Our next test will be this slide-out CTA sticky button against the former control experience of just a sticky donate button without a slide-out message.

experiments.title: How updating the call-to-action button on a Facebook ad impacts click-throughs
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the call to action button for potential course takes will achieve a change in course sign up rates because the button relates to the action we are asking them to take. .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/01/2023 - 03/03/2023
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
For the launch of a new course led by Dan Burke, the founder of Avila, we wanted to test whether a change in the call-to-action on a Facebook ad would make an impact on click-throughs.
Our standard control for most of Avila’s ads has been to use a ‘learn more’ call to action; in this experiment, since we were asking people to sign up to take the course, we tested learn more versus a ‘sign up’ call to action button.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the call-to-action button from 'Learn More' to 'Sign Up' did not lead to an increase in click-throughs for Avila Foundation's new course. The treatment resulted in a 6.7% decrease in clicks, which was statistically significant with a 94.9% level of confidence.
However, when viewing this experiment through the lens of how many people actually signed up for the course, the results are different. While there was a higher number of clicks on the 'learn more' control version, it actually had a 6.3% less sign-up rate once those people got to the landing page. Thus, we can see a correlation between asking people to sign up as the call-to-action, and them actually going through the process. While this only had a 61% level of confidence, it does show a directional lift in response.
Continued testing for this kind of experiment is needed. Future variations could include making the copy of the ad itself correlate more to the call to action, and/or testing within the ad headlines as well so they correlate to the call-to-action button.

experiments.title: How adding a sticky “my cart” button when a user begins a donation affects conversion rates

rows.name: Cart button at top of page

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a sticky cart button for donors who add an item to their cart (scroll persistent), will achieve a greater number of donor conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/22/2021 - 09/27/2022
Added a sticky cart button that appears when users add an item to their cart (scroll persistent).
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations
• Mobile donor conversion was higher for the sticky cart (42%) at a statistically valid LoC (95%).
• Mobile revenue per visitor was higher for the sticky cart (68%) at a statistically valid LoC (98%).
• Recommendation is sticky cart addition to all gift catalog-related pages.
• Projected yearly incremental lift of $200,000.

experiments.title: How revamping email subscription button copy affects email acquisition rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the homepage email button copy to emphasize urgency for potential donors will achieve a greater email conversion rate because it will create a more immediate response.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/25/2022 - 03/04/2022
If we change the homepage email button copy to emphasize urgency, email conversion will increase because users need to act immediately. We tested four different variants against our control language “join us” for this experiment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations:
• Overall email conversion was directionally higher for "Sign Up" and "Join Now."
• Mobile email conversion was directionally higher for "Join Now."
