• Desktop email conversion was directionally higher for all variants.
• Of the five, "sign up" (13.7%) and "join now" (24.1%) had higher email capture rates.
• Mobile email conversion was higher for "join now" (19%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (64%).
• All four variants' desktop conversion was higher at a non-statistically valid confidence levels.
• Recommendation is not changing the HP email copy due to lack of statistical significance.
• We could potentially test "sign up" vs. "join now" button language in the future, to see if there is a more significant result with fewer variants since both had a more significant email conversion rate.

experiments.title: How Moving the Global Navigation Donate Button Onto Its Own Menu Line impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that moving the “give now” button onto its own global navigation menu line will increase donor conversion
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/05/2022 - 07/24/2022
KCBI is a large Christian radio station in the DFW area with a worldwide listening audience. In a prior test, they’d found that the addition of a “monthly” donate button to their global site navigation menu increased donor conversion. However, there were some issues with that test that made the results unclear. We had several factors that changed in the experiment (the placement of the “give now button,” the wording on the buttons, and the addition of a monthly donate button) that clouded the results. In order to understand WHICH factor led to the increase in donor conversion, we decided to test simply moving the “give now” button out of the menu line with the other options and onto its own line. We believed this would give it prominence as it would stand on its own in the navigation bar.
Since this would only impact Desktop traffic, that is the only source of donors we examined.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we didn't wait to reach a 95% or greater level of confidence, there was no period during the test or segment of donors where we were seeing a positive impact and it was clear that moving the button was having a directional decrease on conversion, so we ended the test.
This showed us that donors were not more likely to click the "give now" button when it was in its own menu. Next, we will test the language of the donate button, "give now" vs "donate now" to see which is the most converting.

experiments.title: The Impact of Aligning a Landing Page’s headline with the CTA and adding Social Proof to the page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by aligning a landing page’s headline with the CTA and adding social proof, we will improve email acquisition conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/07/2022 - 04/22/2022
Focus on the Family uses free video series offers on Facebook to acquire new email addresses and donors. In a prior test, they found that adding social proof language to a landing page improved email signups by 4% with an LOC of 89%. They decided to test this approach on their Praying the Scripture Over Your Children video series offer to see if it would improve conversion. For the treatment, they added “Join over 1 million people who have been reached with encouraging, faith-building content across all our free video series like this one!” under the image of the offer.
While preparing for the test, they also decided to align the headline of the page with the CTA. So, they changed the headline from Learn How to Be a Prayer Warrior for your Kids to Learn How to Be a Prayer Warrior for your Kids With This Free Video Series.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Even though the addition of social proof improved conversion on their other offer, for this video series, it did not. That said, we don't know if the result would have been different had this test not included the additional copy on the headline. Because we changed two elements of the page at the same time, it's impossible to know if both elements decreased conversion or if they worked to cancel each other out.
For future tests we should test one element at a time to ensure a clean result. Also, even in the prior test when the social proof increased conversion, it only had a 4.3% impact on conversion. We will prioritize future tests on Focus offers that are likely to result in more significant impacts.

experiments.title: How tightening copy and replacing a button with a hyperlink affected click rates on an auto-responder
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that tightening the copy and replacing a button with a hyperlink for email recipients will achieve a higher click rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/15/2022
The Gospel Coalition developed a content offer which provided a free e-book download when people signed up. To further cultivate this audience and provide even greater value to their experience, a discussion guide was sent the next day to anyone who downloaded the ebook. We wanted to know how different tones and link presentations might affect click rates and developed a treatment which would be run through an A/B test with traffic split 50/50.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment produced a 9.2% directional lift in click rates with an 87.2% level of confidence indicating that the shorter, more direct copy of our treatment (compared to the more conversational, interrogative copy of the control) did have a measurable positive effect on click rates.
More subtly, the treatment copy was structured to provide the "why" (value proposition) of downloading the question guide at the beginning of the email, directly after the salutation. This is based on the theory of influence that states that providing a clear 'why' within a request is more likely to trigger a response of compliance—in this case by clicking to download the question guide "to help you get the most from this insightful resource."
This value proposition is later reinforced near the CTA (call to action) by illustrating that the question guide will a) help readers apply the text's lessons to their own life and b) provide a framework for discussing the text with others.
The treatment also hypothesized that a simple hyperlink may also help improve click rates over a button which, while very visible, may create additional friction at the CTA.
These results show that providing a "why" and streamlining the language within an email promotion can trigger a more favorable response from recipients by creating a slippery slide which carries them effortlessly from salutation to CTA while providing enough benefit for recipients to click through.
In the future, we will test these elements individually to help isolate the variable that produced the lift.

experiments.title: How adding a donate ‘button’ impacts donor conversion rates for Pathway to Victory
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donate button for web visitors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/10/2022 - 04/16/2022
Pathway to Victory is a ministry that has a fundraising program that is built around premium offers. The main navigation on their homepage does not lend itself easily to a donor finding a way to make a donation; in this test we created a donate ‘button’ and tested it against the regular ‘donate’ link in the header navigation to see whether it would drive a higher conversion rate. Both of the donate links went to a landing page where the donor could choose which premium the wanted to receive as thanks for their donation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
There was a slight increase in conversion rates for users seeing the donate button, with a 3.6% lift in conversion rates and an 81% level of confidence. Interestingly, there was a larger increase in conversion rates when looking at returning visitor traffic, with an increase of 6.7% in conversion rates and an 87% level of confidence.
This is most likely due to the nature of web traffic to the Pathway to Victory website, and the path people take to get there. Generally, people get to the website by heading about Pathway to Victory on TV, the radio, or seeing a digital ad for a specific premium offer. They have no real context about what Pathway to Victory does, so they are less likely to make a donation and are more likely to look for the specific premium they wanted to purchase. However, returning visitors already have some context about the work Pathway does, and are more likely to make a donation to support the organization.

experiments.title: How adding a donate button increases donor conversion rates for Oblates
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donate button for website visitors will achieve increased donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/31/2022 - 03/04/2022
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate had a website where the donate call to action from the homepage as part of the header navigation menu and not a button as most non-profit websites have. As they are in the process of getting a new redesign, we wanted to test whether creating a donation button would increase donor conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Adding in a donate button increased donor conversion rates by 15.5% overall, while the increase for new visitors was over 27%.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Organic Search visitors had a 31.9% increase in donations with an 86% level of confidence.
 	New visitors had a 27.2% increase in donations with a 94% level of confidence.
 	35-44 visitors had a 105.5% increase in donations with an 89% level of confidence.
 	18-24 visitors had a 168.4% increase in donations with a 92% level of confidence.

experiments.title: Will offering a free membership trial leads to more membership activations?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering a free 1-month trial for landing page visitors will achieve more membership activations.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/01/2021
As we reconfigured what membership looked like, we were trying to figure out a way to get more people to activate a membership to the NextAfter Institute. So, we thought if we offered folks a free 1-month trial, it would allow them the chance to learn what membership is about and be able to access our resources right away. We ran this experiment on our membership landing page with a 50/50 split in traffic between signing up for the typical $49/month membership vs. the 1-month free trial.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Something important to note is that metric tracking between all systems in this experiment was messy. We did our best to isolate variables, but they are not perfect.

Although the experiment did not validate, results point in the direction that the free trial is most effective in achieving more membership activations. However, something worth considering is if those folks will be retained as well as those who pay for the membership right away—more testing and analysis needs to be done to figure that out.

experiments.title: How simplifying “Dear Reader” ads on news articles impacted donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying the “Dear Reader” ad experience for newsroom visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/24/2021 - 04/02/2021
As a part of on-site promotions of a recent open letter (petition), Americans for Prosperity wanted to experiment with simplifying their “Dear Reader” ads — which are ads that promote new offers and acquisition funnels at the end of news articles — by replicating the “simplified ad experience” that was so effective within their Facebook advertising campaign.
Would this simplified ad experience also improve results for their on-site visitors to their newsroom pages?
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a nearly valid 89% level of confidence, we observed an increase in donor conversion rate of +390%. Because Americans for Prosperity was looking to maximize results on the limited time they had available to promote this offer, we decided to roll this out pretty quickly.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 832.5% increase with a 97% level of confidence.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Email visitors had a 390.8% increase in donations with an 90% level of confidence.Returning  visitors had a 1.0% increase in donations with a 92% level of confidence.
It would appear that using the simplified ad experience (presenting a question, with a "Yes" or "No" button alongside it as a call to action is also as effective in on-site promotions through the "Dear Reader" ads as it was on the Facebook advertising campaign.

experiments.title: How a YOU-centered call to action impacted donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How will framing the ask in the context of what you—the letter signer—just did, believe, and are looking to accomplish by taking this action impact donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/09/2020 - 10/23/2020
As a part of the SCOTUS 2020 confirmation hearings for (now) Justice Amy Coney Barrett, Americans for Prosperity had designed a digital activism opportunity for citizens to sign a letter and send it to their senators to tell them that they approved this nomination and were urging their elected officials to represent them by voting to confirm the nominee when it came time to officially vote.
The letter-signing page, once completed, would redirect signers to a donation page where they would be asked to make a financial gift to support the continued efforts to confirm the nominee.
In reading the “control” message, we felt that it was very organizationally-centered, and thought: What if we provided a more you-centered call to action, but because there was so much traffic available, we also decided to test a treatment that outlined the services that are available to donors to the organization, as well.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 92% level of confidence, we were able to achieve a +50.9% increase in donor conversion rate for the 2018 variant (which was YOU-centered in it’s appeal to the letter-signer/prospective donor).

Although this didn’t fully reach the target 95% level of confidence, we decided to turn off the study and roll this treatment out to 100% of the traffic, considering there was only a week or so left in the confirmation hearing process, and therefore the promotional campaign.

The key learning here is that we should always focus on what action someone just took, and think about the reasons WHY they just took that action. If we channel this line of thinking, we can present to them a reasonable and compelling reason as to why they would make a donation after having completed that action.

experiments.title: How the Freedom Quiz button text on the button promotion impacted petition click rates

rows.name: yes, i’ll sign the petition now
read the petition now - email capture last

experiments.researchQuestion: Which button text at the end of the quiz will generate the highest click rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/03/2019 - 04/05/2019
Americans for Prosperity launched a new quiz, which led to a petition signature page. At the end of the quiz, we wanted to test which button text would generate the most signatures.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 88.5% level of confidence, the treatment text on the button increased petition button click rate by 77.9% over the control.
With our goal being to get as many people from the quiz to the petition (and subsequently the donation page), our learning here is that we should err on the side of “soft asks” on button text. The control message forces the quiz-taker to decide whether or not they want to sign the petition before they’ve ever seen it.
Because the email capture rate is so low across both segments, we wanted to end this experiment quickly to launch an email capture at the front of the quiz experience (instead of at the end of the quiz) to increase overall email capture rates.

experiments.title: How call to action and value proposition affect email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: Will changing the headline and intro copy strengthen the value proposition and increase email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/01/2016 - 06/16/2016
We were hosting a live broadcast to unpack the first article of the Fundraiser’s Creed: People Give to People. We had set up a landing page to allow people to register for the broadcast. Upon peer review, we thought that a few small tweaks could increase the force of the value proposition on the page.
The first hypothesis had to do with the text above the headline. On the control, this copy said “Upcoming Live Internet Broadcast”. This was correct, but it gave the user no motivation or orientation in regards to what we wanted them to do. For the treatment, we changed this copy to “Register now for the live broadcast”.
The second hypothesis centered around the first paragraph. It was intended to address the reader and frame the problem, but we hypothesized that it might bury the actual value proposition for the broadcast, without adding much value. In the treatment, this paragraph was removed.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment produced a 24% lift in email acquisition. This shows the importance that each piece of copy has — copy can either orient and direct the visitor, or distract them and weaken the value proposition.

experiments.title: How active language in the call to action increases donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does active language on a donate button increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/26/2017 - 06/14/2017
Live Action wanted to increase donations on their main donation page. They tested the copy used on the “Donate” button in the navigation of liveaction.org. After finding that the copy “Defend the Preborn” had a 60% increase in clicks over the original “Donate” copy and the other treatment, “Support Live Action”, they tested which copy converted more donors.
experiments.keyLearnings: The control was “Defend the Preborn” and treatment #1 was “Support Live Action”. “Support Live Action” had a 46.5% increase in donations over “Defend the Preborn.” So while “Defend the Preborn” was able to get more people to the donation page initially, more people gave a donation using the text “Support Live Action”. What this tells us is that even though people were more motivated to click on “Defend the Preborn”, the lack of clarity in that copy to where they would be taken resulted in less people converting to what the primary objective was on the page – to give a gift. By using more active language with greater clarity, we were able to increase traffic to the donation page and increase donations and revenue.

experiments.title: The impact of adding a second CTA as a P.S. in email
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a second signup CTA as a P.S. lead to a higher click-through rate?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/25/2018
We decided to market our free online course to subscribers who had not yet enrolled. In crafting our emails, we wanted to know if adding a second signup CTA as a P.S. would lead to more people clicking through to the landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings: While our results were just below a 95% level of confidence, we saw a clear directional decrease in conversion with the addition of the P.S. Our hypothesis is that the P.S. did not feel natural to the flow and thus made the email seem less personal to the recipients.

experiments.title: How a direct call to action influences clicks on a sticky-bar
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a softer call-to-action drive more traffic to the landing page?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/11/2018
In this experiment, we were launching a t-shirt design contest for the 2018 NIO Summit. We placed a sticky bar on the homepage of www.niosummit.com to let visitors know about the contest. We tested two different calls to actions: Enter Your Design vs Learn More
experiments.keyLearnings: The “Learn More” call-to-action drove 53.4% more traffic to the landing page, although the experiment did not reach the 95% level of confidence we would have liked. Nevertheless, this is a strong indicator that a softer call-to-action for general traffic on the homepage is more effective at increasing clicks.

experiments.title: How reinforcing the value proposition in the body copy and CTA affects donor conversion.
experiments.researchQuestion: Which landing page would increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/15/2016 - 08/26/2016
For Harvest Ministries mid-month appeal they wanted to see if they could improve donor conversion on the landing page. The control and the treatment had the same value proposition, but the treatment had subtle additions to the copy of the headline, body and CTA to stregthen the value proposition of the page. They ran a split test to see which landing page would increase donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment landing page had an increase in donations by 22.8%. The reinforcement of the value proposition throughout the treatment landing page strengthened the message and ask, resulting in more people giving a donation.

experiments.title: How increasing the visibility of the donation button impacts giving
experiments.researchQuestion: Will making the donation button easier to find increase the overall number of gifts received?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/05/2017 - 07/21/2017
This test, after running for nearly two months, we did not reach a 95% level of statistical validity. While the big red button seemed to increase giving, there was no significant change to the number of people that were clicking on the button. With this being the case, it does not seem reasonable to say that the bigger button motivated more people to give.
experiments.keyLearnings: Based upon donor feedback, The Heritage Foundation wanted to find a way to make the donation button on the Heritage.org website easier to find. In doing this, we wanted to measure what kind of impact it would have on donor giving.

experiments.title: Can a “Start My Gift” Button on the Main Donation Page Reduce Friction for Year-End Donors?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a “Start My Gift” button near the top of the main donation page for visitors during the year-end campaign will increase donor conversion by reducing friction for highly motivated donors and allowing them to quickly begin the giving process.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/19/2025 - 12/31/2025
During the year-end campaign, many donors arrive on the main donation page after clicking through from ads, emails, and other campaign touchpoints. While the page includes a detailed value proposition explaining the impact of giving, the team was concerned that the longer messaging could introduce friction for highly motivated donors who were already ready to give.
Specifically, visitors had to scroll past the page’s text before reaching the donation form, which could delay donors who simply wanted to give after responding to an appeal.
However, the team also feared that shortening the page would be counterproductive, as many visitors arrive still unconvinced to donate.
To address these concerns, we decided to test a “Start My Gift” button near the top of the donation page. The test variants were:
Control Version: visitors saw the standard donation page experience, where they would scroll down the page to reach the giving form
Treatment Version: a “Start My Gift” button was placed near the top of the page. When clicked, the button automatically scrolled the visitor directly to the donation form, allowing donors to skip the body copy and begin the giving process immediately
The goal of this test was to determine whether reducing friction for highly motivated donors would increase the donation page conversion rate during the year-end campaign. The test ran from December 19 – December 31.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Overall Conversion
Conversion rates were virtually identical between the two versions — 12.9% for the control and 12.8% for the treatment. Adding the "Start My Gift" button didn't hurt overall conversion, suggesting that a faster path to the form didn't undermine the page's persuasive messaging.
Revenue Per Visitor
There was no significant difference in overall one-time revenue per visitor. However, a meaningful shift emerged among larger donors: for gifts of $1,000 or more, the treatment increased revenue per visitor by 34.9% at 93.5% confidence. This suggests the button reduced friction for highly motivated mid-level and major donors who arrived ready to give and simply wanted a faster way to do it.
Monthly Sustainer Gifts
The clearest tradeoff was in recurring giving. The control generated 50 sustainer gifts compared to 31 for the treatment — a 36.8% decline at 95.8% confidence. A likely explanation: the button allowed donors to skip past the page's value proposition, reducing exposure to messaging that might have inspired a monthly commitment.
Mobile vs. Desktop
No meaningful differences emerged between devices. The button performed similarly across mobile and desktop.
Key Takeaway
Reducing friction helps unlock larger one-time gifts without hurting overall conversion — but it may come at the cost of recurring giving. Further testing should focus on whether a fast-access path can be designed in a way that preserves exposure to the monthly giving ask.

experiments.title: How changing copy on a button for a landing page impacted conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing button copy to be clearer for potential monthly donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/22/2025 - 04/29/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of changing the CTA copy on the buttons. We hypothesized that the word ‘JOIN’ was confusing for the user and thus was decreasing conversion. We changed the copy to ‘DONATE TODAY’ to clearly communicate the action and improve conversion rates. The results showed that the CTA change improved user clarity and increased conversion rates. The results showed whether this change improved conversion rates, though specific outcome data is not provided here.
experiments.keyLearnings:
It is important to note that the donation sample size (17 total) was very small as we were aiming for monthly donors. This test aimed to assess the impact of changing the CTA copy on the buttons. We hypothesized that the word ‘JOIN’ was confusing for the user and thus was decreasing conversion. We changed the copy to ‘DONATE TODAY’ to set appropriate expectations for the user.

Key Results:

-       +76% non-statistically significant lift (75%) in overall conversion rate.

-       +99% non-statistically significant lift (89%) in overall RPV.

Recommendation/Implementation:

We have changed the CTA copy on the landing page to DONATE TODAY.

 

Desktop performance:

Control: 2.3% conversion rate and $0.76 RPV.

Treatment: 3.9% conversion rate, which is a 69% increase with a 44% level of confidence. RPV increased by 29% with a 60% level of confidence.

 

Mobile performance:

Control: 1.5% conversion rate and $0.28 RPV.

Treatment: 2.8% conversion rate, which was an 81% increase with a 69% level of confidence. RPV also increased by 167% with a 91% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How changing the donate button link to non-tribute affects donor conversion rate on site profiles
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the donate button links to be the non-tribute page for site profile visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because  the donate buttons leading to the tribute page without the context of the tribute concept, would lead to potential confusion and friction about the message. Therefore, leading the users to the non-tribute page that had more missional language, would reduce that confusion and friction, encouraging users to donate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/22/2026 - 01/27/2026
On site profiles, all donate buttons usually lead to the tribute donation page for CaringBridge.
For this test, we wanted to change the donate buttons in the header, middle of the page and the footer to lead to the non-tribute donation page instead of the tribute page. The tribute widget would still lead to the tribute donation page, maintaining that continuity.
This was tested on site profiles and was tested on both mobile and desktop. This was tested when there were no campaigns or matches going on for CaringBridge.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
Changing the donate button links to the non-tribute page for site profile visitors led to a 7.8% increase in Transactions for All Traffic. This indicates that the change had a positive impact on donor conversion rate, potentially reducing confusion and friction for users.
In future experimentation, it is important to note that while the results of this experiment suggest a positive impact on donor conversion rate, the statistical level of confidence was below the recommended 95%. Therefore, these results should be interpreted with caution and not used for making definitive decisions.
However, the insights gained from this experiment can guide future testing strategies. It is worth exploring further variations in the donation button links to see if similar positive results can be achieved with a higher level of statistical confidence. Additionally, considering the significant increases in both donations and revenue from the non-tribute donation page, incorporating more missional language and context into donation pages could be a valuable strategy to enhance donor engagement and conversion rates.

experiments.title: How changing the donate button link to non-tribute affects donor conversion rate on site profiles

Therefore, leading users to the non-tribute page with more missional language would reduce that confusion and friction, encouraging users to donate. Donation buttons leading to the tribute page without the context of the tribute concept could lead to potential confusion and friction with the message. Therefore, leading users to the non-tribute page with more missional language would reduce that confusion and friction, encouraging users to donate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/09/2026
On site profiles, all donate buttons usually lead to the tribute donation page for CaringBridge.
For this test, we wanted to change the donate buttons in the header, middle of the page, and the footer to lead to the non-tribute donation page instead of the tribute page. The tribute widget would still lead to the tribute donation page, maintaining that continuity.
This was tested on site profiles and on both mobile and desktop. This was tested when there were no campaigns or matches going on for CaringBridge.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that changing the donation buttons on site profiles from leading to the tribute donation page to the non-tribute donation page resulted in an 8.4% decrease in donations across all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 53.9%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. experiments.title: How a dynamic impact statement below the donate button affects transactions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a dynamic impact statement  for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in transactions because the statement would act as social proof and show the visitor how many other people support this mission, encouraging the visitor to make a donation and join in this mission. because  the statement would act as social proof and show the visitor how many other people support this mission, encouraging the visitor to make a donation and join in this mission.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/02/2025 - 12/02/2025
During Giving Tuesday, we wanted to test adding a dynamic impact statement below the donate button for CaringBridge on their donation pages. The statement pulled the number of donors on the donation page during the day of Giving Tuesday. The statement read as follows –
Your gift’s impact will combine with the X other donors who have also stepped forward to DOUBLE their Giving Tuesday gift alongside the $50,000 match!
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a dynamic impact statement on the donation page did not have a significant positive impact on transactions during Giving Tuesday. In fact, the treatment group saw a 3.9% decrease in transactions compared to the control group, with a statistical confidence level of 31.1%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%.
This suggests that the dynamic impact statement did not effectively encourage visitors to make a donation based on the number of donors who had already contributed. In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the messaging and placement of social proof elements on donation pages to ensure that they resonate with visitors and positively impact their decision to donate.
It is also essential to conduct experiments for a longer duration to gather sufficient data and increase the confidence level of the results before drawing any conclusions or making decisions based on the findings. Additionally, considering different types of social proof elements or adjusting the messaging based on visitor behavior and preferences could be explored in future experiments to optimize donation page performance.

experiments.title: How adding clarity of the gift type to the submit button affects transactions (Guide to the Constitution)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity for the gift type on the submit button for donors will achieve an increase in transactions because the donor more clearly understands the commitment of the gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/20/2025 - 09/04/2025
This experiment aimed to increase completed transactions on the Guide to the Constitution donation form by adding clarity about the selected gift type (one-time vs. monthly) to the submit button. The existing version of the form used a generic “Make My Gift” button, which provided no clear indication of the commitment donors were making when completing their gift. We hypothesized that this lack of clarity could create hesitation or uncertainty at the final step of the giving process.
To address this, we created an A/B test with two versions of the submit button. The control version used the existing generic button, while the treatment version explicitly included the gift type within the button text, such as “Make My Monthly Gift” or “Make My One-Time Gift” depending on the donor’s selection. This approach was designed to reinforce what the donor had chosen earlier in the process and reduce any last-minute confusion before completing the transaction.
The test ran for a little over two weeks, with traffic randomly and evenly allocated between the two page versions to ensure comparable audiences and sessions.The primary metric for the test was completed transactions, with secondary metrics including transactions monthly, one-time and overall conversion rate from page views to completed gifts. By clarifying the commitment on the button itself, we expected to see an increase in donor confidence and a corresponding lift in transactions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key findings of this experiment are the treatment that added gift type clarity to the submit button resulted in a -28.4% decrease in transactions compared to the control, with a confidence level of only 48%. This is well below the typical threshold for statistical significance, indicating that the observed difference is likely due to random variation rather than a true effect. Rather than improving clarity and increasing conversions, the added text on the button may have created friction or uncertainty for donors at the final step.

These results suggest that clarifying gift type at the point of submission is not an effective strategy for improving conversions and may even discourage donors from completing their gift. Future efforts to improve clarity should focus on earlier points in the giving flow—such as during gift selection or form completion—rather than on the submit button itself.

experiments.title: How including a button in an email appeal affects click-through rate for recurring donors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a button to an email for recurring donors will achieve an increase in click-through rate because it provides a clear, visually distinct call-to-action that reduces friction, draws the reader’s eye, and makes it easier for people to take the intended next step.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/26/2025
The Heritage Foundation is running its annual Leader’s Club campaign. The second email of the campaign offered a free ebook called “Flags of Our Fathers.” Within this email, they wanted to test how changing the raw URL to a button would affect the click-through rate to the landing page. This experiment ran on one email, and this segment included donors to The Heritage Foundation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a button to an email for recurring donors did not lead to an increase in click-through rate as hypothesized. In fact, the treatment group saw a 13.6% decrease in clicks compared to the control group. The statistical analysis showed a 63.4% level of confidence, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to provide reliable results and that any conclusions drawn are statistically significant. Additionally, it is crucial to carefully consider the design and implementation of changes such as adding a button, as they may not always have the desired effect. It is also recommended to conduct further testing and analysis to understand the reasons behind the unexpected outcome and to inform future decision-making. Experimentation is a valuable tool for learning and refining strategies, and it is important to approach it with a curious and open mindset to adapt and improve based on the findings.

experiments.title: How adding a sticky stacked navigation bar with a “Donate Monthly” button affects recurring donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a “donate monthly” button on a stacked navigation bar for mobile users will achieve an increase in recurring donations because having a separate button would draw more attention to it, resulting in users clicking on the button and making a donation.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/08/2025
For this experiment, we added a sticky stacked navigation bar for mobile, but we also added a “Donate Monthly” button to see how that would affect transactions. We had previously tested this with just a donate button, and we had seen that there was an increase in clicks to the donate button when in a stacked navigation bar. This experiment was run for mobile users.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a "donate monthly" button to a stacked navigation bar for mobile users can potentially increase donations. The treatment group saw a significant increase in donations, indicating that having a separate button for monthly donations could draw more attention and encourage users to donate regularly.

However, the statistical level of confidence is below the recommended threshold of 95%, which means that these results should not be considered reliable for future decision-making.

We did see an increase in clicks to "donate monthly button". We saw a valid increase in clicks.

But ultimately, we saw a decrease in overall transactions, therefore indicating that this had a negative effect on transactions. We saw a 6.3% decrease in transactions. This was not a valid decrease in transactions.

For future experimentation, it is essential to run further tests with a larger sample size or different variations to validate the impact of adding a "donate monthly" button. It is also important to consider other factors that may influence donation behavior on mobile devices, such as the placement of the button, messaging, and design. Additionally, conducting usability testing and gathering user feedback can provide valuable insights for optimizing the donation process on mobile platforms.

experiments.title: How including a button in an appeal email affects donor conversion rates for non-members
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a visually prominent, action-oriented button that says “Double My Gift” will increase donor conversion rate because it draws more attention than a plain-text hyperlink, creates a clearer and more compelling call to action, and reduces friction—especially for mobile users—by making the next step more obvious and easier to complete.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/31/2025
The Heritage Foundation conducted this experiment as part of their President’s Challenge matching gift campaign. This campaign was designed to drive donor engagement by offering a limited-time opportunity to double the impact of a donor’s gift through a matching challenge. The email in question was the first send in the campaign sequence, making it a high-leverage moment for influencing donor behavior and maximizing initial response rates.
Given the high stakes of the campaign’s early momentum and the importance of clearly communicating the match opportunity, the Heritage Foundation sought to test whether different call-to-action (CTA) formats would affect click-through and conversion rates.
The test was deployed via email, targeting the Heritage Foundation’s non-member list.
Both versions of the email—control and treatment—were linked to the same donation landing page but differed in their CTA presentation.
The experiment was driven by a hypothesis rooted in behavioral and conversion rate optimization principles: a visually prominent, action-oriented button might outperform a plain-text hyperlink in driving user action.
Key motivations included:
Improving clarity and visibility of the CTA: While the raw URL in the control version is technically functional, it lacks the immediate visual weight and action language of a button. The team suspected that this might cause the CTA to be overlooked or feel less urgent.
Reducing cognitive friction: A clear, concise button like “Double My Gift” directly mirrors the donor’s intent and may simplify decision-making.
Encouraging mobile-friendly design: Buttons often perform better on mobile devices where touch interaction is key, and plain-text links can be harder to tap.
This test is a good example of using micro-optimizations in email fundraising design to explore how seemingly small changes can have an outsized effect on user behavior and conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that using a visually prominent, action-oriented button with a clear call to action like “Double My Gift” can potentially increase donor conversion rates compared to a plain-text hyperlink. While the treatment did show a 0.6% increase in donations, the statistical level of confidence was only 1.3%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85% for significance.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the potential impact of small design changes such as CTA formats on user behavior and conversion rates. However, it is crucial to ensure that the statistical significance of the results is high enough to draw valid conclusions. Therefore, when conducting similar tests in the future, it is recommended to continue testing different CTA formats while ensuring a larger sample size to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence before making any definitive decisions based on the results. Additionally, considering other factors such as audience segmentation, messaging variation, and testing on different platforms can further enhance the reliability and applicability of the experiment's findings.

experiments.title: How does adding a button to the May Challenge Grant banner impact donations?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a clickable button with a clear call-to-action beneath the May Challenge Grant banner for users on the homepage will achieve an increase in donations because it eliminates any confusion around the banner being clickable, while also giving a directive to the user.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/06/2025 - 05/13/2025
During Time of Grace’s May Challenge Grant, the primary banner lacked a button and call-to-action directly beneath it. We hypothesized that, despite the banner itself being clickable, it may not be immediately obvious to users on the homepage—especially those on mobile who would not have the ability to “hover” over the banner and see it darken as clickable. We believe adding a clear button and direct call-to-action will increase donations
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a clickable button with a clear call-to-action beneath the May Challenge Grant banner did not result in an increase in donations. The treatment group saw a 12.3% decrease in transactions for all traffic compared to the control group, however, the statistical confidence level was only 40.4%.

These results indicate that there may not be confusion or friction for users on the homepage, knowing they can click the banner itself. After this test, we instead turned our attention to the ask contained in this banner itself.

experiments.title: How increasing specificity and personalization on the Tribute donate button affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that treatment copy for the call-to-action on the Tribute donate button will achieve an increase in conversions because the two options leverage either greater specificity around the kind of a gift a user is making, or personalizes with who they’re making the gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/14/2025 - 07/17/2025
On CaringBridge’s Tribute donation page, the Donate button’s CTA was simply: Submit Secure Donation. We developed two treatments to improve the conversion rate on this page that targeted this final call-to-action. The first, hypothesized that greater specificity: Submit My Tribute Gift would increase donor conversion—better reflecting the kind of gift the user was making. The second iteration, tested personalization of the CTA button. A tribute gift is a donation to CaringBridge made in honor of a particular person. We believe that displaying that person’s name in the donate button itself would increase conversion rate—reiterating who the tribute was being made to.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

The treatment, which aimed to increase conversions by either providing greater specificity in the call-to-action or personalizing the donation button, did not result in a statistically significant increase in donations. In fact, there was a 1.4% decrease in Tribute Donations with a 19.6% level of confidence. This means that the control was more effective in this case. Additionally, it's worth noting that both treatments resulted in a decrease (with statistical validity) in the average gift versus the control donate button.

experiments.title: How changing the color of the call to action button on tribute widgets affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the tribute widget call to action color for profile visitors will achieve an increase in tribute donor conversion rate because changing the color would provide more visibility to the button and provide more clarity as to what the button is for.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/02/2025 - 07/07/2025
For CaringBridge, their website primarily contains their purple brand color. That is the case for the call to action buttons for the tribute widgets as well. That can potentially cause the button to be blended in with the other elements on the website and not be entirely visible for users. For this test, we wanted to test how changing the button to blue would affect tribute donations. This was tested on all of site tribute widgets.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
Changing the tribute widget call to action color from purple to blue resulted in a 2.8% increase in Donations - Tribute for All Traffic, but with a low statistical confidence level of 38.3%. This indicates that the change may have had a positive impact on donor conversion, but the results are  inconclusive.
 	However, Revenue - Tribute experienced a significant 19% decrease with a high level of confidence (100%). This indicates that while the number of donations may have increased, the total revenue generated from tributes decreased.
The impact of changes on multiple metrics: While an increase in donations is positive, it is essential to also consider the impact on overall revenue. It's important to strike a balance between increasing donations and maintaining or increasing revenue.
 	Statistical confidence level: Results with a low statistical confidence level like the one in this experiment should not be relied upon for decision-making. It's crucial to ensure that any conclusions drawn are statistically significant to make informed decisions.
 	Further testing: To validate the impact of changing the tribute widget call to action color, additional testing with a larger sample size and longer duration may be necessary. This will help in drawing more reliable and conclusive results.

experiments.title: How a gift array vs a single button call-to-action in a high urgency campaign footer bar affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the gift array version of the footer for Moody Radio website traffic will achieve an increase in engagement and donor conversion metrics because it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page. because it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/02/2025 - 06/30/2025
During Moody Radio’s Fiscal Year End campaign, we tested into a gift array within the lower third footer vs the standard one-button call-to-action.
While the gift array version had been used in previous campaigns, there had not been a direct test none on the gift array portion alone to understand the motivation of donors to Moody Radio.
This test ran for almost a full month, with equal traffic seeing the control and the treatment.
We believe that using a gift array on a sticky bar for website visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment testing a gift array in the lower third footer of Moody Radio's website are as follows: The one-button version resulted in a 4.6% increase in Transactions for All Traffic compared to the standard one-button call-to-action. The statistical level of confidence for this increase was only 13.3%, which is below the standard threshold of 95% for validity.

While we were unable to reach validity on this test due to the limited time frame, the results do tell us that Moody Radio's audience may be more motivated to give when there is only a single call-to-action button. On the other hand, the results suggest that the gift array version of the footer bar led to a higher average gift.

*Development team added parameters to custom transaction and revenue metrics based on the "src" associated with each variant.

experiments.title: How a gift array vs a single button call-to-action in a high urgency campaign footer bar affects clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the gift array version of the footer for Moody Radio website traffic will achieve an increase in clicks because it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/02/2025 - 06/30/2025
During Moody Radio’s Fiscal Year End campaign, we tested into a gift array within the lower third footer vs the standard one-button call-to-action.
While the gift array version had been used in previous campaigns, there had not been a direct test none on the gift array portion alone to understand the motivation of donors to Moody Radio.
This test ran for almost a full month, with equal traffic seeing the control and the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key takeaway from this experiment is that the gift array version of the footer for Moody Radio did not lead to an increase in clicks, as initially hypothesized. In fact, we observed a 4.2% decrease in clicks across all traffic, with a statistical confidence of 36.3%. This is well below the accepted 95% threshold for validity.

Additionally, downstream metrics showed a 4.4% decrease in transactions, with a confidence level of only 13.3%.

These findings suggest that the gift array version of the footer did not effectively drive more users to the donation page and may have resulted in less motivated traffic. While these metrics did not meet the validity criteria, they point to the possibility that there may be more effective ways to encourage donations from Moody Radio listeners beyond the gift array format.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider that what may seem like a logical approach to increasing user engagement, such as allowing users to select a gift amount before reaching the donation page, may not always lead to the desired outcome. It is essential to test all changes thoroughly before implementing them on a larger scale. Additionally, it is crucial to ensure that experiments are statistically sound and reach a high level of confidence before drawing conclusions.

experiments.title: How a gift array vs a single button call-to-action in a high urgency campaign footer bar affects clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that he gift array version of the footer  for Moody Bible website traffic will achieve an increase in clicks because it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/02/2025 - 06/30/2025
During Moody Bible’s Fiscal Year End campaign, we tested into a gift array within the lower third footer vs the standard one-button call-to-action.
While the gift array version had been used in previous campaigns, there had not been a direct test none on the gift array portion alone to understand the motivation of donors to Moody Bible.
This text ran for almost a full month, with equal traffic seeing the control and the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the gift array version of the footer for the Moody Bible website traffic showed a 4.3% increase in clicks compared to the standard one-button call-to-action. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 21.1%, which is below the 95% threshold needed to consider the results valid.
Furthermore, the treatment saw a 26.1% lift in transactions at a 43.7% LOC, meaning that the treatment led to more people engaging with the footer bar as well as led to more motivated traffic to make a gift.
However, the treatment actually resulted in a 20.7% decrease in revenue at a 56.7% level of confidence (pulled out of the number of transactions)
Control: 11 gifts, $3747
Treatment: 14 gifts, $3780
These results indicate that the treatment was more effective at getting people to engage and make a gift, but ultimately, the control produced a higher average of revenue per donor.
In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that a statistically significant level of confidence is achieved before making decisions based on the results. This means conducting the experiment for a longer duration, increasing the sample size, or making adjustments to the experiment design to improve the reliability of the results.

experiments.title: How adding gift equivalencies to each individual ask array button impacts conversion rates and average gift.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding gift equivalencies for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates and average gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/04/2025 - 06/12/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of adding equivalencies to the ask array section, where each gift amount would have a related ‘item’ that it could fund.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
Adding gift equivalencies to the ask array section resulted in a 4.6% decrease in donations for all traffic.
 	The statistical level of confidence was only 40.2%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for valid results.
 	Therefore, the results of this experiment cannot be considered reliable or used for future decision making.
 	It is essential to ensure a sufficient level of statistical confidence before drawing conclusions from A/B experiments.
 	In future experimentation, it is important to consider factors that may have influenced the results, such as the presentation of gift equivalencies and donor preferences.
 	Further testing with a larger sample size and adjustments to the gift equivalencies may be needed to draw more accurate conclusions about their impact on conversion rates and average gift.

experiments.title: How a 3 button gift array instead of a 4 button affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a 3 button gift array for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in transactions and average gift because having a 3 button gift array with the weighted option in the middle would reduce friction and the donor would more likely make a gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/02/2025 - 06/16/2025
Time of Grace usually has 4 gift array buttons on their donation page. For this test, we wanted to test reducing the gift array to 3 buttons and see how that would affect donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that reducing the gift array to 3 buttons on the donation page led to an 8.8% increase in transactions for all traffic. This suggests that the hypothesis that a 3 button gift array would reduce friction and increase donor conversions may have some merit. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this result was only 46.3%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for validity.

Therefore, while the results of this experiment are promising, they should not be used for future decision making as they are not considered statistically significant. In future experimentation, it would be valuable to continue testing the impact of a 3 button gift array on donor conversion rates, but with a larger sample size or different experimental design to ensure more reliable and conclusive results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to consider other factors such as donor preferences, messaging, and timing when designing future experiments to further optimize donation page performance.

experiments.title: How adding animation to a donation button in the navigation impacts donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding animation to the donate button for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/08/2024 - 02/15/2024
This test explored the impact of adding an animation to the donate button in the main navigation. The goal was to increase conversion by drawing the user’s eye to the donate button. Our hypothesis was that by adding an animation to the donate button in the navigation, it will increase conversions.
In this experiment we tested 2 different variants of animation– a ‘wiggle’ and a ‘bounce.’
experiments.keyLearnings:
There was an overall increase in engagement with the donate button, but this experiment did not validate either on conversion rate or revenue per donor.

Desktop:

Control: 3.8% click through rate, 3.1% conversion rate.

Treatment B (wiggle): 4.6% click through rate, 3.7% conversion rate (20% increase at 76% level of confidence), 4% increase in revenue per visitor (55% level of confidence).

Treatment C (bounce): 4.3% click through rate, 4% conversion rate (27% increase at an 88% level of confidence), 12% decrease in revenue per visitor (67% level of confidence)

 

Mobile:

Control: 1% click through rate, 3.2% donor conversion rate.

Treatment B (wiggle): 1.6% click through rate, 3.1% conversion rate (3% decrease at 14% level of confidence), 19% decrease in revenue per visitor (79% level of confidence)

Treatment C (bounce): 2% click through rate, 2.7% conversion rate (14% decrease at a 66% level of confidence), 20% decrease in revenue per visitor (79% level of confidence)

While it's important to note that this test did not cause an increase in transactions, it did cause a significant increase in revenue. We're digging in with the moody team to see if there are any outlier gifts that would have made this increase.

experiments.title: Will adding value proposition to the homepage donate button increase conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value propostion to the main donate button for cye 2024 campaign will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because the added value propostion will reinforce the ‘why’ . because the added value propostion will reinforce the ‘why’ .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/18/2025
For CYE 2024 campaign we wanted to see if we could increase donations by adding more value proposition under the homepage’s main donate button.
For this test, we only ran it while there was no other elements on the homepage. Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment show that adding a value proposition to the main donate button for the CYE 2024 campaign did not result in a higher donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in donations compared to the control group. However, it's important to note that the statistical level of confidence is only 62.6%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95%. This means that the results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to always ensure that the statistical significance level is met before drawing any conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to further analyze why the value proposition did not resonate with donors in this particular experiment. This could involve gathering feedback from donors, conducting qualitative research, or testing different value propositions to see which resonates the most. By continuing to test and learn from different strategies, we can ultimately uncover what drives donor conversions and improve our fundraising efforts in the future.

experiments.title: How removing a CTA link at the beginning of an email increase clicks and donations on a donor audience
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by removing a a link at the beginning of an email for Hoover’s donor audience will achieve an increase in clicks, emails, and donations because it shifts the focus toward the content and value of the eBook before presenting the CTA.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/06/2025 - 02/14/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank dedicated to promoting ideas for a free society. Hoover recirculates their content offers by sending out an email to their Donors and Nondonors every Thursday offering a free eBook or course. On this Thursday, Hoover sent out the offer, “The Ten Causes of the Reagan Boom”. This specific email was to their donor audience.
Our hypothesis was that if we remove one of the links in the email it would increase clicks and email acquisition and donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that by removing a link at the beginning of an email for Hoover's donor audience, there was a 7.4% increase in clicks for all traffic. This indicates that shifting the focus towards the content and value of the eBook before presenting the call-to-action may have a positive impact on engagement.
Another interesting thing to note is that the control version received 16 gifts, while the treatment received 12 gifts.
Here are some potential reasonings behind the increase in clicks:
1. Reduced Link Fatigue & Increased Focus
In the control email, the reader sees two instances of the eBook link. When people see the same link twice, they might mentally skip over it, assuming they've already processed the message.In the treatment, there’s only one link, making it stand out more and increasing the likelihood that the reader clicks it.
2. Building Anticipation
The control email presents the link early on, potentially causing some readers to click right away and miss the rest of the message. Those who don't click immediately might not feel the need to continue reading.The treatment removes the early link, keeping readers engaged until the end, where they see the CTA more prominently and with more context.
3. More Natural Flow & Call-to-Action Strength
The treatment version subtly shifts the focus toward the content and value of the eBook before presenting the CTA. This can make the final CTA feel more compelling, rather than transactional.The single call to action at the end could create a sense of exclusivity or urgency, reinforcing the importance of clicking.
4. Less Distraction, More Commitment
The control version interrupts the email with a link early on, which might cause some readers to skim or disengage.The treatment keeps the reader focused on the narrative before directing them toward a single action.
5. Perceived Value & Scarcity Effect
By not giving immediate access to the link, the treatment might make the reader feel like they need to read carefully to find out where to get the eBook. This small increase in effort can create a greater sense of investment, making them more likely to follow through.
6. Psychological Consistency (Zeigarnik Effect)
The Zeigarnik Effect suggests people remember unfinished tasks better than completed ones. The treatment email builds curiosity without immediately satisfying it, keeping the reader engaged until the final CTA.

experiments.title: How removing a CTA link at the beginning of an email increase clicks and donations on a nondonor audience
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing a CTA link at the beginning of an email for Hoover’s nondonor audience will achieve an increase in clicks, email acquistion, and donations because it increases engagement and reminds them of the CTA.

4. Less Distraction, More Commitment

5. Perceived Value & Scarcity Effect

The Zeigarnik Effect suggests people remember unfinished tasks better than completed ones. The treatment email builds curiosity without immediately satisfying it, keeping the reader engaged until the final CTA.
However, because of the low level of statistical confidence this will need to be tested further!

experiments.title: How a consistent CTA throughout the donor funnel impacts conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a consistent CTA for the housefile during a high-urgency appeal will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it helps reinforce one clear CTA with consistency, rather than mixing calls to action. because it helps reinforce one clear CTA with consistency, rather than mixing calls to action.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/21/2025
During CaringBridge’s holiday appeal, we hypothesized that a consistent CTA from the email to donation page copy and final “donate/give” button would help reinforce the desired action and increased donor conversion rate. In the control, the ask was presented with various ways of phrasing the call to action: “make a gift” “donate” “give” “match your donation” etc. In the treatment, we used “donate” consistently as the CTA in email copy and donation page copy while also changing the give button to “Donate.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that having a consistent call-to-action (CTA) does not always lead to an increase in donor conversion rates. In this case, using "donate" consistently as the CTA in both the email and donation page did not result in a higher number of donations compared to using various CTAs like "make a gift" or "match your donation."

It is important to note that the results of this experiment are not statistically significant, as the confidence level is below 95%. Therefore, these results should not be used for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it would be worthwhile to test these in other funnels – besides just email (consistent CTA from a Sticky Bar or Facebook ad for example), in addition to swapping the consistency for a different CTA (using Give throughout rather than Donate throughout)

experiments.title: How adding a CTA to a Facebook Ad impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a CTA to the ad creative for Avila’s audience will achieve an increase in donations and click through rate because the purpose of the ad is clearer and therefore more motivated to download.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/31/2025
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
One of Avila’s offers is Rule of Life a special guide to use as you chart out your spiritual “growth plan.”
We wanted to test adding the CTA on the ad creative as well as adding a colored background to see if this impacted the donation rate, click through rate, and email acquisition. By encouraging people at the beginning of the funnel to download the ebook – our hypothesis was that more people would click on the ad and therefore donate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a call-to-action (CTA) to the ad creative can significantly increase donations and click-through rates for Avila Foundation. The treatment group, which had the CTA added to the ad creative, saw an impressive 81.4% increase in donations compared to the control group.
However, the statistical level of confidence was at 79.3%, which is below the commonly accepted threshold of 85%. This means that while the results show a positive impact from adding the CTA, they cannot be considered statistically valid and should not be used for making future decisions.
It also had an impact on how many downloaded the offer:
There was a 13.7% increase in signups on the treatment; with a 76.5% level of confidence.
Additionally, it could be beneficial to explore other elements such as different CTA language, colors, or placements to further optimize donation rates and click-through rates.

experiments.title: How an identity call to action in email, emphasizing “become a donor,” impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an identity CTA for non-donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it helps emphasize the user becoming a donor, speaking to their identity/what they’re joining, versus the action CTA of “make a gift”.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/21/2025
During CaringBridge’s holiday appeal, we hypothesized that using an identity CTA for non-donors, emphasizing “become a donor” rather than just “make a gift” will increase donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:

1. The hypothesis that using an identity CTA for non-donors emphasizing "become a donor" would increase donor conversion rate was not supported by the results.

2. The treatment group, which used the identity CTA, actually saw a decrease in donations compared to the control group.

3. The statistical confidence level of 66.4% is below the standard threshold of 95%, meaning that the results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

It is possible that for non-donors, the identity CTA framed the ask in a way that felt too big. As someone whose never given, "become a donor" may seem more intimidating than "make a gift" having a reverse effect given our hypothesis. It would be worthwhile to re-test this experience for higher validity during a period outside of the increased urgency.

experiments.title: How adding the gift amount in a donation form’s submit button affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding the donor’s gift amount to the submit button for Heritage membership campaign donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the increase in clarity on the donation amount will lead more people to donate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/17/2024 - 11/12/2024
The Heritage Foundation has a page where people can make a gift in order to become a member of Heritage. In an attempt to increase donor conversion rate on the campaign’s donation page, they wanted to test if adding the donor’s selected gift amount to the donation form’s submit button would increase clarity and lead more people to feel secure in their donation.
The control’s submit button read: “MAKE MY GIFT”
The treatment’s submit button read: “MAKE MY GIFT OF $[amount]”
The control and treatment each received 50% of traffic from on-site buttons and popups.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding the donor's gift amount to the submit button for Heritage membership campaign donors did not lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment version with the gift amount displayed saw a 0.8% decrease in transactions compared to the control version, but at a 3.92% level of confidence, these results cannot be relied upon as valid.

In future experimentation, it would be valuable to continue testing different variations of the submit button language and design to see what impact they have on donor conversion rates. Additionally, it may be beneficial to run experiments for a longer duration and with more traffic to ensure reliable and conclusive results.

experiments.title: How does an impact call out versus a sticky bar impact donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the impact feature on instant donation page for paid media acquisition will achieve an increase in donations because it is a visual representation of their matched impact.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/07/2024 - 12/30/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
Last year for calendar year end we tested a sticky bar on instant donation pages that are within the funnel for new name acquisition content offers: https://app.winstonknows.com/experiment/6937
This year we decided to test the sticky bar vs the Anedot donation platform feature of an impact call out. This feature shows the donor the “impact” of their gift, this case the double impact due to the year end match.
While the sticky bar explains the match and impact of the donation, our hypothesis is that the impact features visual will increase donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The treatment, which featured an impact call-out visual representation, resulted in a 2.5% decrease in Transactions for All Traffic compared to the control.

2. The statistical level of confidence for this result was 8.2%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85% for results to be considered valid.

3. Due to the low statistical confidence level, the results of this experiment should not be used for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is essential to ensure that the statistical confidence level is sufficiently high to draw valid conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to explore different variations of the impact call-out feature or other visual representations to determine their effectiveness in increasing donations. A/B testing can continue to be a valuable tool for Pathway to Victory in evaluating different strategies for optimizing donation pages and improving the overall fundraising process.

experiments.title: How adding the gift amount in a donation form’s submit button affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding the donor’s gift amount to the submit button for high urgency campaign donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the increase in clarity on the donation amount will lead more people to donate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/19/2024 - 08/05/2024
The Heritage Foundation was running their yearly high-urgency President’s Challenge campaign. In an attempt to increase donor conversion rate on the campaign’s donation page, they wanted to test if adding the donor’s selected gift amount to the donation form’s submit button would increase clarity and lead more people to feel secure in their donation.
The control’s submit button read: “MAKE MY GIFT”
The treatment’s submit button read: “MAKE MY GIFT OF $[amount]”
The control and treatment each received 50% of traffic from Facebook ads, prospecting and housefile emails, and on-site popups.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment was ultimately inconclusive. We learned that adding the donor's selected gift amount to the donation form's submit button may have a positive impact on donor conversion rate for high urgency campaigns. The treatment version, which included the gift amount in the submit button, saw a 1.2% increase in transactions for all traffic.

However, the statistical level of confidence for this result was only 12.5%, which is below the threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid. This means that we cannot confidently say that the increase in transactions was solely due to the change in the submit button language.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to yield statistically significant results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test the impact of including the gift amount in the submit button across multiple campaigns and audience segments to see if the results are consistent.

Overall, while the initial findings are promising, further testing is needed to confirm the impact of this change on donor conversion rates.

experiments.title: How adding the gift amount in a donation form’s submit button affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding the donor’s gift amount to the submit button for eBook campaign donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the increase in clarity on the donation amount will lead more people to donate. because  the increase in clarity on the donation amount will lead more people to donate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/11/2024 - 11/08/2024
The Heritage Foundation was running ads for their Election Fraud eBook offer, where visitors fill out a form to receive a copy of the eBook and then are redirected to a donation page. In an attempt to increase donor conversion rate on the campaign’s donation page, they wanted to test if adding the donor’s selected gift amount to the donation form’s submit button would increase clarity and lead more people to feel secure in their donation.
