The control’s submit button read: “MAKE MY GIFT”
The treatment’s submit button read: “MAKE MY GIFT OF $[amount]”
The control and treatment each received 50% of traffic from Facebook ads, prospecting and housefile emails, and on-site popups.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding the donor's gift amount to the submit button for the eBook campaign did not lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in Transactions for All Traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 7.4%, which is below the standard threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid.
Therefore, we should not make any conclusive decisions based on the results of this experiment. It is important to recognize that sometimes variations in performance may occur due to random chance rather than a true impact of the changes made. In future experimentation, it is crucial to ensure that a sufficient sample size is used and that statistical significance is reached before drawing conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test other variations or factors that could potentially impact donor conversion rates to gain a better understanding of what drives donor behavior.

experiments.title: Will adding in the value proposition copy from the donate button to the monthly donation page increase transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding biblical resources for the monthly donation page will achieve will increase transactions because their is congruency between the donate button and the copy on the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/04/2024 - 10/29/2024
During our testing travels with the duel donation buttons in the main navigation bar we found that the value proposition of providing biblical resources led to an increase in clicks to the donation page.
We wanted to see what would happen if we adding that same value proposition to the donation page copy.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding the value proposition of providing biblical resources to the donation page copy significantly increased revenue for all traffic. The treatment group, which included the added biblical resources copy, saw a 157.6% increase in revenue with a 97.6% level of confidence, indicating that this change had a substantial impact.

This indicates that there is a strong congruency between the messaging on the donation button and the copy on the donation page, which likely contributed to the increase in transactions. In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue exploring ways to align the messaging throughout the donation process to maintain this congruency.

Additionally, it would be worthwhile to test other potential value propositions or messaging strategies on donation pages to see if they also lead to an increase in donations. By continuing to test and optimize the messaging and value propositions on donation pages, and can further enhance its donation performance and maximize its impact.

experiments.title: Rad: Does a raw link or a button create more clicks?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a raw link for any email links will achieve a higher number of clicks because having a raw links makes the email feel more personal and not like a marketing email.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/04/2024
Moody Radio runs a Fall Share each year that includes a full email campaign.
For this test we wanted to see if the moody radio subscribers prefer a button in an email or a raw link.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that including a raw link in an email campaign resulted in a 9.0% increase in donations compared to using a button. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 45.7%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95%.
Therefore, the results of this experiment cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making. In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a higher level of statistical significance. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test different elements of the email campaign to determine what resonates best with Moody Radio subscribers.

experiments.title: How a premium offer chat CTA on podcast pages impacts donor conversion during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that utilizing a premium offer chat widget with a conversational tone and engaging design will increase donor conversion rates during the high urgency campaign by enhancing the visibility of the call-to-action and creating a more personal appeal.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/01/2024 - 08/22/2024
FamilyLife is a nonprofit organization committed to strengthening families through various resources, including their engaging podcast series. As part of their Fiscal Year End (FYE) campaign, we are testing two different messaging approaches on the podcast pages to encourage donations.
Historically, the “Dear Listener” message has proven successful in driving donations. However, we want to explore whether a more dynamic approach, utilizing a chat widget, might enhance engagement and boost donor conversion rates. This experiment follows a successful test conducted in July with the “Seeing Beautiful Again” campaign, which achieved a 187.9% increase in donor conversion. We aim to see if a similar impact can be replicated with a different premium during the FYE campaign.
We believe that the conversational and eye-catching design of the chat message widget will increase donor conversion during the FYE campaign by improving the visibility of the call-to-action (CTA) and humanizing the appeal.
The control version of the podcast page featured the traditional “Dear Listener” premium message, which included an image of the premium offer, Unoffendable. The treatment version showcased the premium message chat widget, featuring an image of a FamilyLife member. Most of the language remained consistent between the two versions, and traffic was split 50/50 to measure differences in donation conversion rates accurately.
We also tested this on their Article Pages.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment results indicated that the treatment group featuring the premium offer chat widget achieved a conversion rate of 0.39%, compared to the control group’s rate of 0.32%. This represents a relative difference of 24%. However, with a level of confidence of only 43.6%, these results were not statistically significant.

Despite the positive trend in donor conversion rates observed with the chat widget, the lack of robust statistical significance suggests that more data is needed to draw reliable conclusions. The test ran for 21 days, which may have limited the ability to fully assess the widget's effectiveness.

Next Steps:
Extended Testing Duration: Run the experiment for a longer period to gather more data, especially during different times of the month or year to account for seasonal variations in donor behavior.
 	A/B Test Variations of the Chat Widget: Experiment with different designs and messaging variations for the chat widget to identify elements that resonate better with the audience. Consider testing language, tone, and imagery to enhance engagement.
 	Analyze User Interaction: Utilize analytics tools to monitor how users interact with the chat widget (e.g., clicks, time spent, engagement) to identify any drop-off points or issues affecting conversion rates.

experiments.title: How an in-line, clickable “Create a Post” button impacts new journal entries
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a clickable, in-line “Create a Post” prompt for authors will achieve an increase in journal entries because it prioritizes it as a primary call-to-action by making it more visible for the author..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/14/2024 - 09/24/2024
For CaringBridge Authors, posting a new journal entry is one of the primary calls-to-action. However, currently it is given a similar level of priority as the other CTA’s on the left sidebar: Share, Add a Co-Author, Ways to Help, etc. We hypothesized that by placing this prompt in-line with the feed (where their entry would ultimately go) and it clickable would improve new journal entries — addressing a potential point of friction that authors aren’t sure where or how to post.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. The in-line "Create a Post" prompt led to a 3.3% increase in New Journal Entries for All Traffic. This indicates that making the journal entry prompt more visible and clickable can encourage authors to post more frequently.

2. However, the statistical confidence level of 79.6% is below the standard threshold of 95%, making the results inconclusive and not completely reliable for future decision making.

We believe that these soft results indicate that the true point of friction hasn't been addressed by this treatment. A similar level of clicks between the cards and a similar conversion rate of journal entires may indicate that "where" to post or "how" is not what's preventing authors from doing so. Rather, it's likely a question of "what" to post. How can we make it easier for authors to share what's on their mind, even if initially they may believe they don't have anything to say.

experiments.title: How the placement of the call to action impacts email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adjusting the CTA placement for landing page viewers will achieve more email acquisitions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/25/2024 - 09/10/2024
DTS runs courses for email acquisition. For these courses, they use a course platform that has a template for what the landing page looks like. The template has the headline underneath the CTA. The user also has to scroll to see it. We wanted to test putting the headline above the CTA to see how it impacted email acquisitions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adjusting the CTA placement for landing page viewers resulted in a 11.2% decrease in Email Acquisitions for All Traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 42.9%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95%. This means that the results are not valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to yield statistically significant results. Additionally, it may be helpful to run the experiment for a longer period of time to account for any fluctuations in traffic or other external factors that could impact the results. Furthermore, it would be beneficial to consider other factors that could potentially influence email acquisitions, such as the design of the CTA button itself or the messaging around it.

experiments.title: How a dynamic homepage donate button on mobile impacts clicks and downstream donor metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the dynamic text on a donate sticky footer for IMB homepage visitors will achieve an increase in button clicks and downstream revenue metrics because the dynamic, typing text is more eye catching to the user .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/10/2024
In an effort to push more of IMB’s homepage traffic to their main donation page, we decided to run a test of adding an additional “give now” button to the homepage.
Control: No changes
Treatment 1: Additional Give Now button sticky footer (“Give Now”)
Treatment 2: Additional Give Now button sticky footer (“Give Now”) + Dynamic Text (Types out “Send the Gospel”)
Our hypothesis was that the dynamic text would increase the number of clicks to the donation page because it was more eye catching than Treatment 1.
This test only ran on mobile devices.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a donate sticky footer on the IMB homepage resulted in a 42.3% increase in donations for all traffic. Whereas, the dynamic donate sticky footer actually resulted in a 13.5% decrease in donations at 34% LOC.

While neither of these metrics validated, it does suggest that the static donate sticky footer was helpful in pushing more of IMB's homepage traffic to the donation page.

We can also look in clicks based on each treatment.

Sticky Footer: 104 Button Clicks

Sticky Footer + Dynamic Text: 88 Button Clicks

This data suggests that the dynamic, typing text was not as beneficial as the static text in directing visitors to the donation page.

Due to these results, we have chosen to roll out the static button to all mobile visitors.

experiments.title: How reinforcing the call to action below a button impacts clicks to activate a free membership trial
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding copy below each button on the landing page for site visitors will achieve an increase in clicks through to the membership activation page because it reinforces the call to action that they will get a free 30 days of membership.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/27/2024
We noticed a decline in clicks through to activate a free NextAfter Institute membership trial, so we looked back at earlier, better-performing versions of our landing page and noticed there used to be copy reinforcing the CTA below each button on the page. So, we tested adding the copy back to see how that would impact clicks through to the activation page.
experiments.keyLearnings: There was no distinct difference in the results between the control and the treatment. The test did not validate. We will test this again during a time where we are pushing more traffic to this page to see if we can validate a result.

experiments.title: Does Aligning the CTA and the Value Propostion Make a Difference in Conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that aligning the cta and the value prop for the main donate buttons will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because there will be less decision friction in the process.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/22/2024
This is the third test in the series of the dual donate button test for FOTF.
The first test we wanted to see if aligning the CTA would make an impact. The second test we wanted to see if aligning the value proposition would make an impact (this test did!)
For the third iteration, we wanted to see if we could test “Give” vs. “Donate” as the CTA to see which language aligns better with the audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using "Donate" as the call-to-action (CTA) for the main donate buttons resulted in a 11.9% decrease in donations compared to using "Give". However, the statistical level of confidence was only 73.9%, which is below the 85% threshold for validity.
The key finding during this testing series is that the main CTA does nto make much of an impact, but using as much specificity as possible when describing the impact of a donor's gift - even at the button level - does.

experiments.title: How pulling the home donate button out of the hamburger menu on mobile impacts clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that pulling the mobile donate button out of the hamburger menu for mobile homepage visitors will achieve an increase in clicks on the donate button because the button will be more visible and accessible therefore reducing friction for mobile users. .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/17/2024 - 08/01/2024
We have seen a positive impact with other clients pulling their home donate button on mobile out of the hamburger menu to make it more visible to the visitor, so we wanted to test this on IMB’s homepage.
This test ran only on mobile with traffic equally split between the control and the treatment.
The treatment had a “Give Now” button pulled out of the hamburger menu, but we also left it within the hamburger menu to view clicks on both.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that pulling the mobile donate button out of the hamburger menu for the IMB homepage led to a 32.2% increase in clicks just on the donate button within the hamburger menu.

As well as, the new mobile donate button resulted in a total of 78 clicks. While the control button within the hamburger menu only had 22.

While this test is difficult to validate, it does not suggest that having the donate button more visible to the user on mobile devices is helpful in getting more users to the main donation page.

**Note: the 'Give Now - Mobile Header' button is tracking clicks on the NEW donate button that appeared outside of the hamburger menu. The 'Give Now - Mobile Menu' button is tracking clicks for both the control and treatment of the original donate button within in the hamburger menu.

experiments.title: How adding sticky donate button impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a step between clicking a button and giving for the sticky donate button will achieve a higher average gift or conversions because the prospective donor would be primed more to make a donation.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/05/2024
For this experiment, we wanted to see if adding a step between clicking the sticky donate button and going to the donation page could increase the number of transactions or the amount of revenue that a donor would give.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that adding a step between clicking the sticky donate button and going to the donation page actually resulted in a decrease in donations as well as revenue. The yes/no question could have caused more friction in the process instead of being a micro yes.

experiments.title: Will a “Give” button have a higher donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing donate to give for the sticky button will achieve a higher donor conversion because give could be more cohesive with the messaging during year end.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/04/2024
For this experiment we wanted to see if changing the Donate sticky button on the side of the web pages to say “Give” would increase donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that changing the sticky button from "Donate" to "Give" did not achieve a higher donor conversion. In fact, it resulted in a decrease in donations for all traffic.

experiments.title: How does calling out the CTA at the top of an email impact revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by providing the CTA at the very top of an email for email subscribers will achieve a higher gifts and revenue because it highlights what is most important without much reading.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/22/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas
Each month, PTV promotes a different book in an effort to encourage donors to give as an incentive
For July, we released a brand new kids book by Dr. Jeffress.
We received an email from another company that used a text box at the top of the email that plainly and briefly gave the CTA in case the reader did not have time to read the entire email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that providing the call-to-action (CTA) at the very top of an email did not yield the desired results. In fact, the treatment group, which received the CTA at the top of the email, saw a 24.8% decrease in revenue compared to the control group.

It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was below the 85% threshold, which means the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making. This indicates that the decrease in revenue may not be solely attributed to the placement of the CTA at the top of the email.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing different strategies to optimize the placement of the CTA in emails. It may be beneficial to test variations in the messaging, design, or timing of the CTA to determine what resonates best with donors and drives the desired actions. Additionally, considering other factors that could have impacted the results, such as the content of the email or the timing of the campaign, could provide further insights for future experiments.

experiments.title: How a higher contrast donation button impacts clicks and overall giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a higher contrast button on the donate button for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in clicks and overall giving because the higher contrast button would attract the visitors attention quicker therefore reducing friction on the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/03/2024 - 05/29/2024
In an effort to push more traffic to IMB’s Home Donation Page, we tested using a higher contrast button to attract visitor’s attention.
This type of test has resulted in a lift in donor conversion metrics for other clients, so we were excited to see how it performed.
Our goal was that by using a higher contrast button color, more visitors would click the button to be directed to the donation page, therefore, increasing giving.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a higher contrast button on the donate button for homepage visitors resulted in a 4.3% increase in transactions for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 27.3%, which means that these results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence. Additionally, it may be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer period of time to gather more data and potentially increase the validity of the results.

Overall, while the initial results show promise, it is essential to rely on statistically significant data to make informed decisions about implementing changes to the website.

experiments.title: How adding the gift amount to a main donation page form submit button affects donation rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding the donor’s gift amount to the submit button for main donation page donors will achieve an increase in donation rate because increased clarity and donor autonomy will persuade donors to follow through with making their gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/01/2024 - 06/25/2024
In an effort to increase donation rate on their main donation page, The Heritage Foundation wondered whether adding the donor’s gift amount in the donation form submit button would increase clarity and donor autonomy and lead to this increase.
The control form submit button read “MAKE MY GIFT” while the treatment button read “MAKE MY GIFT OF $(amount)”.
Each variant in this A/B experiment received 50% of traffic which ran for 24 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding the donor's gift amount to the submit button on the main donation page did not lead to an increase in donation rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in transactions compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 3.0%, which is below the commonly accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results are not valid and should not be used for future decision making.

Future experimentation should include running this test on a donation page that receives more traffic, such as a content offer.

Additionally, it may be beneficial to test different variations of the submit button text or other elements on the donation page to identify what truly resonates with donors and leads to an increase in donations.

experiments.title: How a higher contrast donation button impacts clicks and overall giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a higher contrast button on the donate button for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in clicks and overall giving because the higher contrast button would attract the visitors attention quicker therefore reducing friction on the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/03/2024 - 05/29/2024
In an effort to push more traffic to IMB’s Home Donation Page, we tested using a higher contrast button to attract visitor’s attention.
This type of test has resulted in a lift in donor conversion metrics for other clients, so we were excited to see how it performed.
Our goal was that by using a higher contrast button color, more visitors would click the button to be directed to the donation page, therefore, increasing giving.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the higher contrast button did not have a positive impact on clicks or overall giving. In fact, all tracked metrics resulted in invalid decreases.

"Give Now" Button Clicks:
Control - 95 clicks
 	Treatment - 93 clicks
 	3.2% decrease in clicks, 17.6% LOC
Control - 362
 	Treatment - 335
 	8.5% decrease in clicks, 76.4% LOC

experiments.title: How a sticky donate button affects donor conversion rates on main pages on a website
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an additional opportunity to give for site visitors  will achieve an increase in donations because they are being prompted to give and reminded how The Navigators has helped them by using value proposition language. because  they are being prompted to give and reminded how The Navigators has helped them by using value proposition language.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/21/2023
The Navigators wanted to take advantage of the traffic generated through their resource and blog pages. As people are being equipped/informed, we wanted to run a test where people would see a sticky donate button on the side that would prompt people to give. The treatment would slide out when someone scrolled halfway through the page. If they click “yes” they would be taken to a donation page to make their gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding an additional opportunity for site visitors to donate can potentially lead to an increase in donations. The treatment version, which had a sticky donate button that prompted visitors to give and reminded them of how The Navigators help others, saw a 28.0% increase in donations compared to the control version.
However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this result is only 70.2%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85%.
It would be beneficial to test different versions of the treatment to further optimize the effectiveness of the sticky donate button. By testing different variations and monitoring the impact on donations, the organization can continue to refine and improve their approach to increasing donations.
Overall, the key learning is that adding an additional opportunity for site visitors to donate can potentially have a positive impact on donations. However, further testing and optimization are needed to determine the most effective approach.

experiments.title: How reducing the size of the Express Checkout button on donation pages impacted revenue and donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the size of the Express Checkout button for visitors to our video series acquisition donation pages will achieve an increase in donor conversion because the smaller button will provide less of a competition for the donor’s attention as they’re making their gift, and this means that the friction will be reduced.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/11/2024 - 06/26/2024
The team at Focus on the Family (FOTF) has been testing various elements of their donation pages to determine which factors could improve donor conversion and revenue. One element that we wanted to test was the Express Checkout button that appears in the donation form below the gift array. Users who click this button can choose to donate through Google Pay and the idea is that this makes the donation process easier and thus increases donor conversion. However, we wondered if this button could serve as a distraction during the donation process and actually negatively affect donor conversion.
FOTF first tested removing the Express Checkout button on their main donation page and the results were very flat. They then tested whether reducing the Express Checkout button’s size would drive a different outcome. This test found that the smaller button size led to a 21.3% increase in transactions, with a 95.7% level of confidence.
The purpose of this experiment is to see if that lift in transactions will occur when we test the smaller button on FOTF’s video series acquisition donation pages.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of this test were completely flat; we didn't see any significant changes in revenue or transactions between the control and treatment. This means that, even though we didn't see any statistically-significant improvement on these metrics, the smaller button didn't hurt our donor conversion or average gift.

Because the smaller button saw a positive lift on Focus on the Family's other donation pages -- and did not harm their acquisition donation page conversion -- it's recommended that the smaller button treatment be rolled out on all of their donation pages.

experiments.title: How replacing a button in an email for a hyperlink achieved a higher click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that replacing a button with a raw hyperlink in an email for a huc campaign will achieve a higher click rate because it looks more personal and give the illusion of more trust because it feels like a 1-1 email.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/11/2024
For our first set of experiments for Moody Radio we wanted to test into a more personalized email instead of a heavily templated email.
For this we came up with several tests that we wanted to do.
HTML vs. Plain Text
Hyperlink vs. Button
1 CTA vs. 2 CTA
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:

- Replacing a button with a raw hyperlink in an email for a HUC campaign resulted in a 4.5% increase in Clicks for All Traffic.

- The treatment group showed a higher click rate, which could indicate that using raw hyperlinks in emails may appear more personal and trustworthy to recipients.

- However, the statistical level of confidence was only 50.8%, which means the results are not considered valid for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be important to:

- Conduct further tests with a larger sample size to increase the statistical confidence level.

- Consider testing other variables such as email copy, subject lines, or sender name along with the hyperlink vs. button format to understand the impact of each element on click rates.

- Continue experimenting with personalized elements in emails to see if this leads to higher engagement from recipients.

- Keep track of statistical significance to ensure that results are reliable and can be used for decision making.

experiments.title: Adding a Callout Above the Monthly Gift Frequency Button on the Campaign Giving Page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a brief but impactful message above the monthly gift frequency button on our sustainer campaign giving pages will increase the donor conversion rate because of the social proof and added exclusivity.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/22/2024 - 03/26/2024
By adding a brief but impactful message above the monthly gift frequency button on our sustainer campaign giving pages, will this element help add additional direction to those motivated to give and who will have already landed on the campaign giving page? By adding a donor countdown animation within the message, we hope to add an additional layer of “encouragement” to give during the giving process by showing donors can be part of the answer and help reach an impactful goal through their gift by “becoming one of” the donors needed. We believe adding a handwritten, donor countdown callout above the monthly gift frequency button will insight more donors to get involved and make a monthly donation.
experiments.keyLearnings: The addition of a handwritten, donor countdown callout above the monthly gift frequency button showed a 9.7% increase in recurring transactions at a 23% level of confidence. This test overall was pretty flat. In the future, we would like to rerun this test on a campaign-giving page for a longer amount of time to see if we can reach validity.

experiments.title: How adding a banner to a donation page with a button to accelerate the process impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a button to begin the donation for main donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion because there will be reduced friction present for visitors who are already motivated to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/06/2024 - 03/04/2024
For highly motivated traffic coming to a main donation page from the homepage donate button in the top right corner, we hypothesized that giving them a chance to skip over the copy on the donation form and go straight to the gift frequency and gift array selections without having to read the donation page would reduce friction in the process that we know they initiated by arriving on this page. We had tested into having shorter copy on this page to help get the form above the fold, but wondered how else we could escalate the process and reduce the work involved for visitors here.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a button to begin the donation process for main donation page visitors can potentially increase donor conversion rates. The treatment group saw a 6.7% increase in transactions compared to the control group. However, the statistical confidence level of 72.2% is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%, meaning that the results may not be reliable and should not be solely relied upon for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to further explore ways to reduce friction for motivated visitors on the donation page. This could include streamlining the donation process, providing clearer calls to action, or optimizing the layout of the page to make it easier for visitors to navigate and complete their donation. It is important to continue testing and iterating on different strategies to improve donor conversion rates, while also ensuring that the results are statistically valid before implementing any changes permanently.

experiments.title: How adding match-giving language to the call to action affects the click-through rate for members
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe adding match-giving language to the calls to action will increase the click-through rate because it provides urgency and a feeling of greater impact.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/25/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign.  The email asked members to give and in return, they would receive a Members Card. The email also announces a matching gift opportunity. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if members would click more if the matching gift offer was mentioned again in the call to action text. The control email described the matching gift opportunity once in the body of the email. The treatment mentioned the match in the body of the email and in the call to action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding match-giving language to the calls to action can potentially increase the click-through rate. The treatment group, which included the matching gift offer in both the body of the email and the call to action, experienced an 11.3% increase in clicks compared to the control group.
Note: The treatment email also had a higher conversion rate.
However, it's important to note that the confidence level of the results was only 70.1%, falling below the 85% threshold typically required for statistical significance. As a result, these results should not be considered valid for future decision-making.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue exploring the impact of emphasizing matching gift opportunities in calls to action. This can help to determine if this strategy consistently drives higher engagement from recipients. Additionally, it is crucial to ensure results have a high level of statistical significance before drawing any definitive conclusions or making changes based on the findings.

experiments.title: How adding match-giving language to the call to action affects the click-through rate for lapsed members
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe adding match-giving language to the calls to action will increase the click-through rate because it provides urgency and a feeling of greater impact.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/25/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign. The email asked lapsed members to give and in return, they would receive a Members Card. The email also announces a matching gift opportunity. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if lapsed members would click more if the matching gift offer was mentioned again in the call to action text. The control email described the matching gift opportunity once in the body of the email . The treatment mentioned in in the body of the email and in the call to action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that by adding match-giving language to the calls to action, there was a 14% increase in Clicks for All Traffic for the lapsed members campaign. This suggests that emphasizing the matching gift opportunity in both the body of the email and in the call to action can have a positive impact on engagement and response rates.

Note: While the click-through rate in the treatment email increased it had a slight decrease in the conversion rate.

However, the statistical level of confidence for this result is only 70.6%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85% for results to be considered valid. Therefore, these results should not be used for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue exploring the impact of emphasizing matching gift opportunities in both the body of the email and the call to action. However, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence in the results before making any conclusions or changes based on the findings.

experiments.title: How adding match-giving language to the call to action affects the click-through rate for non-members
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe adding match-giving language to the calls to action will increase the click-through rate because it provides urgency and a feeling of greater impact.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/25/2024
We conducted this email experiment for The Heritage Foundation’s Members Card campaign. The email asked non-members to give and in return, they would receive a Members Card. The email also announces a matching gift opportunity. The conclusion of the email includes a call to action to donate.
We wanted to know if non-members would click more if the matching gift offer was mentioned again in the call to action text. The control email described the matching gift opportunity once in the body of the email . The treatment mentioned in in the body of the email and in the call to action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding match-giving language to the calls to action can potentially increase the click-through rate. The treatment group, which included the matching gift offer in both the body of the email and the call to action, experienced an 11.3% increase in clicks compared to the control group.

Note: While the treatment email has a higher click-through rate it also had a slight decrease in the conversions.

However, it's important to note that the confidence level of the results was only 56.5%, falling below the 85% threshold typically required for statistical significance. As a result, these results should not be considered valid for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue exploring the impact of emphasizing matching gift opportunities in calls to action. This can help to determine if this strategy consistently drives higher engagement from recipients. Additionally, it is crucial to ensure results have a high level of statistical significance before drawing any definitive conclusions or making changes based on the findings.

experiments.title: How making a statement call to action with members in a fundraising email affects the click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making the call to action a statement instead of a question for members will achieve a higher click-through rate because the desired actin is clearer.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/11/2024
This email experiment was conducted for The Heritage Foundation’s Founder’s Day campaign. The email was a personal communication informing members about the challenges America faces today and how The Heritage Foundation is solving those issues. At the end of the email, they ask members to “activate” their membership.
We wanted to know if using a statement call to action would encourage members to click instead of a call to action that asked a question.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that changing the call to action from a question to a statement did not lead to a higher click-through rate among members. The treatment saw a decrease in clicks and the number of conversions, although the results were not statistically significant.

In future experimentation, it is important to note that small changes in wording or formatting may not always have the desired impact on member engagement. It is essential to conduct thorough testing with a significant sample size and statistical confidence to draw valid conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to explore other factors that could influence member behavior, such as the timing of the email, the content of the message, or the overall design of the communication.

Overall, the findings from this experiment should not be used to make decisions about future email campaigns, as the results do not provide a clear direction. Instead, it would be advisable to continue testing different strategies and elements to determine the most effective approach for encouraging members to take action.

experiments.title: How making a statement call to action with non-members in a fundraising email affects the click-through rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making the call to action a statement instead of a question for non-members will achieve a higher click-through rate because the desired action is cleaner.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/12/2024
This email experiment was conducted for The Heritage Foundation’s Founder’s Day campaign. The email was a personal communication informing non-members about the challenges America faces today and how The Heritage Foundation is solving those issues. At the end of the email, they ask non-members to “activate” their membership.
We wanted to know if using a statement call to action would encourage non-members to click instead of a call to action that asked a question.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the use of a statement as a call to action for non-members resulted in a slight increase in clicks compared to using a question. While the results showed a 0.3% increase in Clicks for All Traffic, the statistical confidence level was only 6.1%, which is not significant enough to make a valid conclusion.

The number of conversions for both emails was the same.

In future experimentation, it would be important to conduct further tests with a larger sample size to determine if using a statement call to action consistently leads to higher engagement from non-members. Additionally, it may be beneficial to explore other variations in the call to action to see what resonates best with the target audience. Overall, it is crucial to rely on statistically significant results before implementing any changes based on an experiment.

experiments.title: How adding a donate button to the header and navigation menu affects donor conversion rate

rows.name: Donate Button in Header and Navigation Menu

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donate button to the header and navigation menu for home page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because more people will click to view the donation page. because  more people will click to view the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/15/2023 - 12/11/2023
The Daily Signal is The Heritage Foundation’s multimedia news platform. On their website, the only call-to-action to donate and support the Daily Signal’s work is located at the very bottom in the footer of each page and doesn’t stand out.
In an attempt to increase the amount of traffic to the donation page from the home page, we wanted to test if adding a more prominently displayed donate button to the top of the home page led to an increase in donor conversion rate.
The donate button appeared in both the home page’s header and dropdown navigation menu (in both the desktop and mobile experiences) and was blue in color in order to stand out from the surrounding buttons. 50% of home page traffic saw the control and 50% saw the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for 27 days, in which time the donate button variant saw a 42.8% increase in donations for all traffic compared to the control. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this increase is 53.2%, meaning that the results cannot be considered valid or reliable for future decision making.
In terms of revenue, the treatment saw a decrease of 0.11% in average gift, but at a 6.52% level of confidence these results cannot be relied upon to predict a valid outcome.
Reasons for this may include the donate button appearing less hierarchically prominent than the email "Sign Up" button above it, or home page visitors not yet having experienced the value in the Daily Signal's articles before seeing a donate button.
Based on the results, it is clear that additional testing is needed to understand what motivates The Daily Signal visitors to donate to the organization. This could include adding value proposition copy next to the button, or testing different locations on the home page. It may also be worth testing an unobtrusive sticky donate button that follows the visitor through their journey sitewide.

experiments.title: How adding additional value proposition to navigation giving buttons impacts recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding statements below the main donate and give monthly buttons for the site navigation will achieve an increase in recurring donations because it increases the appeal and clarity of giving on a recurring basis.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/18/2024 - 02/23/2024
Care Net tested into adding a “Give Monthly” button in their main navigation next to their main donate button. While this increased clarity, we wondered if we could increase the appeal of giving while adding even more clarity by adding additional copy below the main button text.
For the Donate button, we added the text, “Save a baby today.” For the Give Monthly button, we added the text, “Save babies 365 days a year.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding statements below the main donate and give monthly buttons in the site navigation can potentially increase recurring donations by making the appeal and clarity of giving on a recurring basis more prominent. The treatment, which added the text "Save a baby today" below the Donate button and "Save babies 365 days a year" below the Give Monthly button, resulted in a 67.0% increase in donations for all traffic.

However, the statistical level of confidence for this result was 78.1%, which is below the commonly accepted threshold of 95% for valid results. Therefore, while the treatment showed a promising outcome, it cannot be considered statistically significant and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to further test the impact of adding additional copy below the main donation buttons to confirm if it consistently leads to increased donations. It is important to ensure that any findings have a higher level of statistical confidence before implementing changes based on the results. Additionally, exploring different variations of the copy or placement of the text could help optimize the impact on donation rates.

experiments.title: How adding a “Support Honest Journalism” Sticky Button to TDS during EOY
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a “Support Honest Journalism” sticky button for new visitors to The Daily Signal will achieve a lift in conversion rate and average gift because media consumers are hungry for trustworthy news and will support a media outlet who represents independent and reliable journalism.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/29/2024
We tested three different sticky buttons on The Daily Signal during EOY 2023. Two button’s were direct solicitations and one was a content offer for our Guide to the Constitution. The first button asked the visitor to “Support Honest Journalism” and the other asked them to “Donate”. Part of the experiment was to determine whether a more defined CTA like supporting honest journalism would create a lift in revenue over a tried and true content offer and a regular donate button. The donate button and Guide to the Constitution buttons resulted in a lower conversion rate and average gift, but the support honest journalism button saw a significant lift of lift in avg. gift and a slight lift in conversion rate. Looking at subsegments we identified that the primary lift came from New Visitors to the site which had an 18.77% increase in conversion rate, and a 98% increase in avg. gift. Returning visitors for the journalism button saw a net -58% drop in revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. A more defined call to action, such as "Support Honest Journalism," can result in a lift in both conversion rate and average gift.

2. The "Support Honest Journalism" button specifically resonated with new visitors to the site, leading to a significant increase in conversion rate and average gift.

3. However, returning visitors did not respond well to the "Support Honest Journalism" button, resulting in a net loss in revenue.

4. The overall increase in revenue for all traffic was substantial but did not reach a statistically significant level of confidence.

Based on these learnings, it is clear that the "Support Honest Journalism" button has potential as a strong call to action for new visitors. However, it is important to consider the impact on returning visitors, as the negative response from this segment had a significant impact on the overall revenue.

For future experimentation, it would be beneficial to further explore how to tailor the messaging and call to action for different visitor segments. Additionally, it is crucial to conduct experiments with a larger sample size to ensure statistical significance and validate the results before making any impactful decisions.

experiments.title: How call to action on a Facebook ad during a drive impacts donor conversions

rows.name: NA WUNC Spring Drive 2023 Learn More
NA WUNC Spring Drive 2023 Donate Now

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by using the Donate Now call to action for Facebook viewers will achieve a higher conversion of donors because the call to action is clear.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/27/2023 - 05/04/2023
WUNC is a North Carolina Public Radio Station. During the Spring Drive, we decided to test a Learn More vs Donate Now call to action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this A/B experiment is that using a clear call to action like "Donate Now" led to a 16.2% increase in purchases for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 65.4%, which is below the recommended 85% level, and thus, the results cannot be considered valid. Additionally, the experiment revealed that using "Donate Now" led to a significant increase in Cost per Click, with a 100% level of confidence. This indicates that while this clear call to action may lead to more conversions, it may also lead to higher costs.

In the future, WUNC should conduct experiments with larger sample sizes and higher levels of statistical significance before making any definitive decisions about changing their call to action. They should also consider the tradeoff between higher conversion rates and increased costs. It may be helpful to explore other variations of the call to action, such as "Donate Today" or "Make a Donation," to see if they lead to similar conversion rates without significantly increasing costs. Overall, this experiment demonstrates the importance of using data and statistical significance to validate results before making any changes to marketing strategies.

experiments.title: How dual donate buttons impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a second donate button increase donor conversions?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/28/2022 - 12/23/2022
WUNC is an NPR public news and radio station based in North Carolina.
WUNC has a large number of yearly web traffic but very few become donors. In an effort to convert more web visitors into donors, we decided to test a dual donate button.
Our hypothesis is that by adding a second donate button calling out becoming a monthly donor, we will see an increase in conversions, specifically monthly donors, due to the clarity of the ask.
We tested the dual donate button across the wunc.org site.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For this experiment, we could not validate the response as we only saw a 2.8% decrease in conversions at a 68.8% level of confidence for total donations.

For our recurring donor segment, we saw a 5.6% increase in donations with a confidence level of 34.4% so that segment did not see an impact either.

We are looking into alternate tests to encourage web traffic to convert. Future tests include a sticky donate button, dear readers, and content offered within articles.

experiments.title: How an animated donate button impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase donor conversion with an animated donation button in the primary navigation?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/02/2021 - 02/19/2021
With the high volume of traffic to the Save the Children website, we wanted to discover new ways to increase traffic to the donation page from the donate button in the primary navigation. To do this, we hypothesized that drawing more attention to the button might increase the number of people who would see the button and ultimately click and give. We set up a treatment version of the donate button that was animated. The animation was subtle with a motion going across the button coming from a heart icon. We tested this button against the control.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The animated donation button ran for three weeks. While the goal was to increase donor conversion from the animated donate button - which we weren't able to do with valid results, we did see a significant lift in clicks from it (11%). Essentially, more people clicked to the page but only a minimal amount more donated.

When analyzing the donor conversion, we saw a higher conversion rate from site visitors if they landed on the donation page and saw the animated button versus landing on the homepage.

We also saw the greatest increase in conversion from mobile users. This is most likely due to the button being much more visible on the small device.

After running this experiment and seeing that it was likely not going to validate, we came away with two questions:
Will the subtle animation be a novelty feature that will fade with time?
 	Can we present the subtle animation to people when they are at a point in their experience when they are more likely to convert? This question leads us to our next experiment for this feature where we will trigger the animated donate button at a higher motivation point in their conversion pathway so we can not only get more people to the donation page, but get more people to give.

experiments.title: How dual donate buttons impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a second donate button increase donor conversions?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/28/2022 - 12/23/2022
WUNC is an NPR public news and radio station based in North Carolina.
WUNC has a large number of yearly web traffic but very few become donors. In an effort to convert more web visitors into donors, we decided to test a dual donate button.
Our hypothesis is that by adding a second donate button calling out becoming a monthly donor, we will see an increase in conversions, specifically monthly donors, due to the clarity of the ask.
We tested the dual donate button across the wunc.org site.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For this experiment, we could not validate the response as we only saw a 2.8% decrease in conversions at a 68.8% level of confidence for total donations.

For our recurring donor segment, we saw a 5.6% increase in donations with a confidence level of 34.4% so that segment did not see an impact either.

We are looking into alternate tests to encourage web traffic to convert. Future tests include a sticky donate button, dear readers, and content offered within articles.

experiments.title: How a 2-Button CTA that Addresses Different Motivators Impacts Site Creations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a 2-button CTA for users on the CaringBridge homepage will achieve an increase in site creations because it better addresses both motivations for starting a site and ensures users who are creating a site for a loved one don’t feel excluded by the single CTA..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/26/2023 - 12/29/2023
On CaringBridge’s homepage, a user has two distinct motivations for creating a site. Either for themself or for a loved one. We hypothesized that a single CTA “Create Site” didn’t properly address both audiences and believed that by speaking to both “Create a Site” and a second a button “Create a Site for a Loved One” would result in increased site conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The introduction of a second CTA button for creating a site for a loved one did not result in an increase in site creations. In fact, there was a decrease of 9.8% in New Site Created for All Traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence for this decrease is 48.7%, which is below the threshold of 85% required for valid results.

Based on these learnings, it can be concluded that the addition of a second CTA button did not have the intended effect of increasing site creations.

In future testing, it would be beneficial to track clicks more closely and see how this impacts engagement even despite the relatively flat results in site creations.

experiments.title: How the placement of a call-to-action impacts donation conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that placing the CTA up higher for website visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion because  the CTA is more easily visible.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/15/2023 - 12/31/2023
America Needs Fatima has previously tested a slide out vs. a dear reader to feature different content offers. For this test, we wanted to test the slide out method for a direct appeal ask vs. a traditional sticky bar to see if the appeal placement impacted overall donation conversions.
For this test, both web elements contained the same copy and button CTA.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that placing the CTA higher on the website, using a slide out method, resulted in a 10.8% increase in one-time donations compared to a traditional sticky bar. However, the statistical level of confidence for this result is only 64.5%, which is below the accepted threshold of 85%. Therefore, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the statistical level of confidence is above 85% to have valid and reliable results. Additionally, it may be worth exploring other elements or factors that could impact donor conversions, such as the design or color scheme of the CTA button, the messaging used in the appeal, or the timing of the slide out or sticky bar. By testing these different elements and analyzing the results with a higher level of statistical confidence, the organization can make more informed decisions on how to optimize the website for increased donations.

experiments.title: How adding a donate button to the header and navigation menu affects donor conversion rate

rows.name: Donate Button in Header and Navigation Menu

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donate button to the header and navigation menu for home page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because more people will click to view the donation page. because more people will click to view the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/15/2023 - 12/11/2023
The Daily Signal is The Heritage Foundation’s multimedia news platform. On their website, the only call-to-action to donate and support the Daily Signal’s work is located at the very bottom in the footer of each page and doesn’t stand out.
In an attempt to increase the amount of traffic to the donation page from the home page, we wanted to test if adding a more prominently displayed donate button to the top of the home page led to an increase in donor conversion rate.
The donate button appeared in both the home page’s header and dropdown navigation menu (in both the desktop and mobile experiences) and was blue in color in order to stand out from the surrounding buttons.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for 27 days, in which time the donate button variant saw a 42.8% increase in donations for all traffic compared to the control. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this increase is 53.2%, meaning that the results cannot be considered valid or reliable for future decision making.

In terms of revenue, the treatment saw a decrease of 0.11% in average gift, but at a 6.52% level of confidence these results cannot be relied upon to predict a valid outcome.

Reasons for this may include the donate button appearing less hierarchically prominent than the email "Sign Up" button above it, or home page visitors not yet having experienced the value in the Daily Signal's articles before seeing a donate button.

Based on the results, it is clear that additional testing is needed to understand what motivates The Daily Signal visitors to donate to the organization. This could include adding value proposition copy next to the button, or testing different locations on the home page. It may also be worth testing an unobtrusive sticky donate button that follows the visitor through their journey sitewide.

experiments.title: How a sticky donate button affects donor conversion rates on main pages on a website
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an additional opportunity to give for site visitors will achieve an increase in donations because they are being prompted to give and reminded how The Navigators has helped them by using value proposition language. because they are being prompted to give and reminded how The Navigators has helped them by using value proposition language.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/21/2023
The Navigators wanted to take advantage of the traffic generated through their resource and blog pages. As people are being equipped/informed, we wanted to run a test where people would see a sticky donate button on the side that would prompt people to give. The treatment would slide out when someone scrolled halfway through the page. If they click “yes” they would be taken to a donation page to make their gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding an additional opportunity for site visitors to donate can potentially lead to an increase in donations. The treatment version, which had a sticky donate button that prompted visitors to give and reminded them of how The Navigators help others, saw a 28.0% increase in donations compared to the control version.

However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this result is only 70.2%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85%.

It would be beneficial to test different versions of the treatment to further optimize the effectiveness of the sticky donate button. By testing different variations and monitoring the impact on donations, the organization can continue to refine and improve their approach to increasing donations.

Overall, the key learning is that adding an additional opportunity for site visitors to donate can potentially have a positive impact on donations. However, further testing and optimization are needed to determine the most effective approach.

experiments.title: How adding a second link in an email impacted conversion for nondonors
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a second link for email subscribers will achieve a higher conversion rate because there are more opportunities to click . because there are more opportunities to click .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/31/2023 - 11/03/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
This is an experiment we ran for PTV a couple of years ago, but we wanted to test again to see if the results still held.
We tested a second link in the email to see if it would increase engagement and down funnel donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The addition of a second link in the email did not result in a higher conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in clicks for all traffic, implying that subscribers did not engage more with the email when presented with an additional link.

2. The control group, which did not have the second link, outperformed the treatment group in terms of clicks. Although this result is not statistically significant, it suggests that the original email format may be more effective in driving engagement.

3 Of those who received the treatment only 37% clicked the first link and 63% clicked the second link.

4 Donations saw a 0.9% increase with a level of confidence 4.5%.

 

Based on these results, the presence of a second link did not impact engagement or donations.

experiments.title: How Pulling Mobile Give Button Out of Menu Impacts Donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making the donate button visible in the navigation bar for for mobile users will achieve an increase in donations  because  the increased visibility eliminates friction for those who either might not have known where to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/30/2023 - 07/03/2023
RTB’s Mobile view hides the Donate button within the “hamburger” menu. So, unlike the desktop view, a user has to open the menu then select “Donate” from the button in order to make a gift rather than just clicking a button in the navigation. We believe that updating this, so that the give button is displayed for mobile view will increase donations.
Additionally, we ran this test during fiscal year end know that there would be more website traffic and motivation would be higher.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that making the donate button visible in the navigation bar for mobile users resulted in a 35.1% increase in donations. However, the statistical level of confidence is only 61.1%, which is below the threshold of 85% required for the results to be considered valid and reliable. Additionally, we did not meet the recommended 50 transaction minimum even after running this experiment for a little over a month.
Therefore, while there is some indication that making the donate button more visible could increase donations, further experimentation is needed to confirm these findings.
Given when this test was ran (during fiscal year end), it's also likely that the motivation to give was too high and this treatment made no difference.
It's recommended we retest this concept with a different approach. We want to highlight the donate call to action but may need additional value proposition language to give the site visitor more context as to why they should give.

experiments.title: How changing the donate button color on a homepage affected conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by changing the donate button color in the menu for potential donors will achieve an increase in conversion rate because people will have less friction finding where to donate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/25/2023 - 07/02/2023
In this experiment, we wanted to test changing the color of the donate button in the main navigation menu for this prison ministry client. The control color of the donate button is part of the color palette that this brand has. Our idea was to find a color that is different and would make the word “Donate” a lot more obvious to someone looking at the site. If the donate button stands out more, we believed that it would increase the conversion rate since the donor can see the button quicker and have less friction finding the donation button.
For this experiment, the only difference is that the control page has the original color donate button and the treatment has a new color. We split this test 50/50 as an A/B test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the color of the donate button in the main navigation menu resulted in a 29.9% increase in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 61.9%, which is not validated since it is below the 95% standard level of confidence.

In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to test a more radical design difference for the donate button to identify the most effective option. This could include shape, size, button animations, and font. By adding a more drastic design difference to the donate button, this could draw more donors which could increase the conversion rate. A/B testing should continue to be conducted, comparing different design elements or variations to optimize the website for increased donations.

experiments.title: How does reinforcing the value proposition of a donation with an impact statement on the donation button affect donation rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a brief statement that reinforces the value of giving will increase donations by helping to overcome hesitancy at the point of completing the transaction.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/04/2023 - 07/06/2023
Care Net is an organization that provides support and resources to women and men considering abortion.
They have been acquiring donors online for many years now but have seen the number of donations/donor conversion rate drop over the last several months.
Many donors feel hesitancy or spend the majority of time in the “consideration” stage of a transaction at the button. This is where the momentum most often stops. How would adding a short statement that reinforces the impact of the donation affect donation rates?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that including a brief statement that reinforces the value of giving can potentially increase donations and help overcome hesitancy at the point of completing a transaction. Although the treatment showed a 19.7% increase in revenue for all traffic, the statistical level of confidence of 72.3% is below the threshold of 85% needed for the results to be considered valid.
In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing the impact of including a brief statement that reinforces the value of giving. The results of this experiment provide promising insights that can be further explored and tested with larger sample sizes to ensure statistical significance. Additionally, it is important to evaluate different variations of the statement to identify the most effective messaging to drive donations.

experiments.title: How prompting people to give through a sticky donate button affects giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an additional opportunity to give for site visitors  will achieve an increase in donations because they are being prompted to give and reminded how CaringBridge has helped them by using value proposition language. because  they are being prompted to give and reminded how CaringBridge has helped them by using value proposition language.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/02/2023 - 06/19/2023
CaringBridge wanted to take advantage of the traffic generated through their resource pages. As people are being equipped/informed, we wanted to run a test where people would see a sticky donate button on the side that would prompt people to give. The first treatment would only slide out when people would click on it while the second treatment would slide out when someone scrolled halfway through the page. If they click “yes” they would be taken to a donation page to make their gift.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment version of the sticky donate button outperformed the control version, resulting in a 5.0% increase in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence is below the recommended threshold of 85% (LoC 24%), indicating that the results may not be valid and should not be used for future decision making. The second treatment resulted in a 1% increase in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 6%. Overall, it seems as if there is no impact but when seeing how many gifts were actually generated through each treatment, we saw 2 transactions generated by treatment 1 and 10 transactions generated by treatment 2. This shows us that treatment 2 is actually more successful in increase donor conversion rate.

experiments.title: How reducing the perceived investment of a call to action impacts account creation rate (Pt2)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that prompting people to create a “page” or “journal” instead of a “site” will increase site creation rate because there will be a reduced perception of work involved which will reduce anxiety as well.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/22/2023 - 06/26/2023
CaringBridge has been running ebook offers where they prompt the ebook downloader to create an account with their organization. They have previously used the call to action, “Create a site” to communicate the breadth of what CaringBridge offers when you create an account to share yours our your loved ones health journey. Our hypothesis was that the language to “create a site” may have the perception that they’re about to make a large commitment. Creating a site may sound tedious, laborious, or intimidating. We wanted to reduce any anxiety about the process by simply changing the word “site” to “page.” This phrase is still accurate but reduces the perceived investment and commitment they’re about to make.
When we ran the first iteration of this test we ran the experiment on one offer. We saw a valid lift when using “page.” Before rolling it out to all offers, we decided to test it among all offers and add another variant using “journal” to see if that would also reduce anxiety.
experiments.keyLearnings:
"Start a Page" resulted in a 27% decrease in site creation rate with a level of confidence of 75% while the "Start a Journal" treatment resulted in a 14% increase in site creation with a 40% statistical level of confidence, indicating a positive impact.
However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence is below the desired threshold of 85%.
Surprisingly, we did see a softness in response when we used "page." It's likely that some of the response is offer-specific but we did see a more positive response to using "journal." Which could indicate that journal lowers the barrier to entry for some reason.
It's worth to continue testing into this by reducing anxiety for the user by using supporting copy to highlight how easy it is to create a site.

experiments.title: How a pulsing heart sticky donate button affected donations on blog pages
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a side sticky button for blog page viewers will achieve a higher donor conversion because the pulsing heart is eye catching.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/13/2023 - 07/03/2023
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
In an effort to increase donations from the Spiritual Directions blog pages we decided to test adding a side sticky donate button with a pulsing heart.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a side sticky button with a pulsing heart on the blog pages resulted in a 44.0% increase in donations for all traffic. This suggests that the pulsing heart caught the attention of viewers and encouraged them to donate.
However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence is only 76.8%, which is below the standard threshold of 85% for validity. Therefore, these results should not be considered reliable and should not be used for future decision-making.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to conduct additional tests with a larger sample size and longer duration to ensure statistical validity. It would also be helpful to test different variations of the side sticky button design, such as different shapes or colors, to determine which design elements are most effective in increasing donations. Additionally, it may be worth exploring other factors that could influence donor conversion, such as the content of the blog posts or the placement of the donate button on the page.
When results of the blog page experiment is combined with the homepage results of the same test (https://app.winstonknows.com/experiment/6140) we find a 66% increase in donations with a 94% level of confidence

experiments.title: How adding privacy language below a survey sign up submit button impacted sign up rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a more formal privacy statement at the below the survey submit button will increase the education survey sign up rate because it will establish more credibility and trust in ACI and the survey itself.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/26/2023 - 06/24/2023
ACI’s Education Survey is producing a 43.3% sign up rate over the last month. While this metric is performing above-benchmark, we believe there is room for improvement for this offer to match the sign up rates of other offers such as the Cancel Culture Pledge (59.5% sign up rate).
We recently tested applying privacy language below the survey sign up form on the Education Survey landing page, as we’ve seen this tactic have success across other similar organizations.
