The historical learning is that privacy language builds trust with our audience as many people can feel vulnerable giving away their information, so the implementation of privacy language (your information is secure with us) provides users more comfort as they make the decision to submit their survey and personal information.
Though, our recent test proved unsuccessful, producing a 4.3% drop in email acquisition with a 99% level of confidence. You can review this experiment at this link: https://app.winstonknows.com/e/Ewwm
As a second iteration, we think placing the privacy language below the form AND survey submit button will be the key to seeing a lift in email sign ups – this opposed to our previous experiment which placed the language between the from and the button.
Placing it below the button will avoid disrupting the sign up process and avoid making users think that they should think twice about submitting their information as they get ready to press the submit button below. Placing the text below the button serves more so as a supportive reminder.
Let’s see how our second iteration performs…
experiments.keyLearnings:
Iteration #2 did not produce a valid drop; however, it did produce very flat results. The treatment saw a 0.07% decrease in email acquisition rate with only a 3.51% level of confidence. This means that the data does not support the addition of privacy language being a part of the Education Survey sign up page.
There was no notable lift downstream or through any alternative segments, either.
After two attempts, adding privacy language does not seem to move the needle with our audience. Ultimately, this is a good thing as users seem to already trust the organization and do not need to be told that their information is secure in order to feel comfortable submitting a form.
We should move our experiment brainstorming to another element on the page to see if we can uncover a desired life in email acquisition.

experiments.title: Site vs Page on Caring for Cancer eBook Confirmation
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that updating the call-to-action as Start a Page for users who’ve just signed up for an eBook offer will achieve an increase in site creations because creating a page has less perceived commitment than starting an entire site.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/21/2023 - 05/21/2023
CaringBridge’s primary ask is to have users create a site — either for themselves or for a loved one. After signing up to be sent an offer, the user is taken to a confirmation page that prompts them with creating a site. We hypothesized that this phrasing could be overwhelming to a visitor who perhaps isn’t familiar with what CaringBridge is but was just interested in the eBook. We believe that the treatment, which instead updates the page with a call to Start a Page will lessen the perceived barrier to entry and increase the number of site creations. This same treatment was used on a different CaringBridge offer with great results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The changed call-to-action from "Start a Site" to "Start a Page" did not result in an increase in website creations. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in website creations. However, the statistical level of confidence was below the recommended threshold of 85%, so these results cannot be considered valid.
Although this same treatment showed a huge increase in site creations on a different offer, the same did not result here. This might pose questions about how this offer and its audience may be different from the funnel where this treatment was last implemented. Ultimately, more testing is needed to create a valid result with a more significant sample size.

experiments.title: How emphasizing the impact of a donor’s gift before a call to action, affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that treating the pushdown headline to first answer “why” for users on the homepage will achieve a higher conversion rate because leading with “what” a donation accomplishes for the user and “why” will be more compelling than the command in the control.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/03/2023 - 05/14/2023
The Navigators created a pushdown/sticky bar for their Eagle Lake campaign. This asset targeted the main homepage, and the button would take users to the instant donation page for the campaign. At launch, the pushdown was underperforming compared to its effectiveness on past campaigns—we hypothesized that this was due to copy itself lacking value prop. In the control, the main headline starts with a call to give even before the user has been given any reasons as to why.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment aimed to improve the underperforming pushdown/sticky bar for The Navigators' Eagle Lake campaign by changing the copy of the headline to address the "why" of giving. The treatment resulted in a directional 13.9% increase in Revenue for All Traffic, but the statistical level of confidence was below the 85% threshold for validity.

Though not a valid result, we did see that the treatment captured

Ultimately, Navigator's audience seemed to respond similarly to both control and treatment versions of the copy. Of course, the next steps could be testing into new copy treatments but the question is whether this is the most likely angle to move the needle for Navigators' audience given the subdued reaction here. Instead, we'd like to turn our focus away from testing into copy and instead determine whether a visual change, like a goal thermometer, is what will prove most effective in communicating value.

experiments.title: How simplifying the value proposition in a call to action impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying and focusing the value proposition language for prospective donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it will add clarity to the call to action and reduce friction .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/04/2023 - 04/11/2023
CaringBridge has a tribute widget on authors’ journal pages. This is an opportunity to make a donation as a tribute to keep the author’s site running. When looking at the original copy used in this tribute widget, we suspected that the value proposition used in the call to action might have competing motivations. It asked the person to be one X people who have already given and were given the suggested dollar amount to power the person’s site for an entire month. Plus, it asks if you can keep the site on for you and the author. Our hypothesis was that this call to action was actually adding friction to the decision making process. We wanted to simplify the call to action. Additionally we wanted to test if the removal of “CaringBridge” in the CTA would make the ask less organizational centric and clearer. The second treatment also focuses on just making the gift in honor of the person.
Treatment 1: A $30 donation to CaringBridge powers a site like Jan’s for one month. Will you join the X donors who have made a donation in honor of Laura with your donation today?
Treatment 2: X donors have made a donation in honor of Laura. Will you join them with your donation today?
The first treatment simplifies the ask so that it does not ask the person to keep the site online for them and for you. The second treatment simplifies the CTA even further by removing mention of CaringBridge and how much is needed to power a site.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment resulted in no conclusive "winner" when looking at donor conversion rate. Treatment 1 resulted in a 2.8% increase in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 31% while treatment 3 resulted in a 3.4% increase in donations with a 38.4% statistical level of confidence.
These treatments resulted in no change in conversion rate but there does seem to be a valid increase in revenue. Treatment 1 produced a 33% valid lift in revenue with a level of confidence of 98%. Treatment 2 produced a 19.5% lift in revenue with a level of confidence of 92%.
This implies that while the language doesn't inspire more people to give, it does motivate people to give even larger gifts. It's likely that the first treatment provided a larger lift in revenue than treatment 2 because it kept the specific $30 ask in the copy.

experiments.title: How the Learn More vs. Donate Button on Facebook Led to a Higher Conversion Rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using the “Learn More” vs. the “Donate Now” button on Facebook for potential donors  will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because asking the potential donor to Learn More vs. immediately donate reduces friction..
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/14/2023
Prison Fellowship International works to transform the lives of prisons, their families and victims through a global network of community partners. In recent months we have been showcasing the support a child program for the children of prisoners that is more general than 1:1 child sponsorship.  Programmatically it costs less to maintain while allowing more children to participate.  The ads were not converting as many donors as we wanted to see. The current ads running on Facebook were asking the potential donor to select the “Donate Now” button to move to the Donation Page sharing additional information.  We have used the “Learn More” button in Facebook and achieved higher results.  We conducted an A/B test using the Learn More Button vs. Donate.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that using the "Learn More" button instead of the "Donate Now" button on Facebook for potential donors may lead to a higher donor conversion rate due to reduced friction. The treatment resulted in a 14.5% increase in donations for all traffic, although the statistical level of confidence was 22% and below the 85% necessary to validate the experiment. Because the overall traffic is low, we recommend moving forward with the treatment as it did produce a higher conversion rate and we have used this as a control button on child sponsorship.

experiments.title: How increasing urgency by including the word “now” to a donate button affects click-through rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the donate button copy to emphasize urgency for potential donors will achieve greater conversions because donors need to act immediately.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/29/2022 - 04/19/2022
If we change the donate button copy to emphasize urgency, there will be an increase in conversions because donors need to act immediately.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations:
• Click rate on the donate button was higher for the variant (11%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (77%).
• Overall donor conversion was higher for the variant (24%) at a statistically valid level of confidence (95%).
• RPV was slightly higher for the variant (1%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (53%).
• One-time donor conversion was higher for the variant (15%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (79%).
• One-time RPV was lower for the variant (-4%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (58%).
• Recurring RPV was higher for the variant (40%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (85%).
• Recurring donor conversion was higher for the variant (69%) at a statistically valid level of confidence (96%).
• As the hero already uses "Donate Now", we recommend not changing the main donate button despite the significant life in donor conversion.

experiments.title: How replacing an “emergency” banner with an “emergency” slide-in affects conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an emergency slider with a clear CTA to the homepage for potential donors will achieve a greater response than the emergency banner  because it moves the page and dynamically loads an equivalent goal.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/04/2022 - 03/09/2022
Adding an emergency slider with a clear CTA to the homepage will outperform the emergency banner because it moves the page and dynamically loads an equivalent goal.
Numbers in the slide-in module are dynamic and reset every hour. If a user clicks “donate now” and then returns to the homepage within the hour, the number will be lower than what they saw previously. The slide-in module is sticky and stays in view when the user scrolls down the page and can be removed by clicking on the “x” in the top right corner, similar to the emergency banner. Both of these CTA’s are driving to an undesignated GAF form. Copy approved by legal.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations:
• Overall increase in donor conversion on the homepage for the variant (2%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (50%).
• Overall increase in RPV on the homepage for the variant (4%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (80%).
• Undesignated donor conversion was lower for the variant (-11%) at a statistically valid level of confidence (99.8%).
• Undesignated RPV declined for the variant (-5%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (81%).
• Emergency donor conversion was higher for the variant (21%) at a statistically valid level of confidence (99.9%).
• Emergency RPV was higher for the variant (14%) at a statistically valid level of confidence (98%).
• A few days into the test, there was an uptick in call center donors who requested their gifts be assigned explicitly to Ukraine.
• We shifted 100% of test traffic to the variant, as these users were more likely to give an emergency gift (potentially reducing call center calls). Shortly after, we shifted from Ukraine designated CEF, which made it difficult to measure the impact of the shift.

experiments.title: How changing donate button copy to convey greater clarity and urgency affects donation rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the donate button copy to emphasize urgency for potential donors will achieve an increase in click rate because donors need to act immediately.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/03/2022 - 02/23/2022
If we change the donate button copy to emphasize urgency, clicks will increase because donors need to act immediately. We examined how different CTA’s affected the click rate on the main donation button. There were four variants with differing language; testing the verbiage of donate v. give v. help in addition to temporal urgency now v. today. This test exclusively measured click rate and did not delve into conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations:
• Test stopped for lack of significant results after 2 1/2 weeks.
• Lowest performing treatment was "give now" (-1.6%) with a confidence of (9%).
• Best performing treatment was "donate today" (12%) at a non-statistically valid confidence of (64%).
• Further tests will examine the impact on donor conversion and click rate.

experiments.title: How adding a privacy statement below the donate button on an instant donation page affects donation rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a privacy statement below the donation button for instant donation page visitors will increase donations by reducing anxiety around sharing sensitive data online.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/20/2023 - 02/10/2023
For a faith-based media organization, we wanted to know how including a privacy statement below the donation button would affect donation rates. We know that privacy continues to be a growing priority for users online and we wanted to test whether or not anticipating any reluctance to submit payment information online at the point of giving would reduce anxiety and encourage more people to give.
The privacy statement below the button on the treatment reads: “We take data and privacy seriously. Your information will be kept safe and will be used to process your gift.”
~50% of traffic received the control and ~50% of traffic received the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for just over two weeks, we observed that the inclusion of a privacy statement had very little impact on potential donors' motivation to give. Furthermore, the low sample size of donations prevented us from achieving an adequate level of statistical confidence in the experiment's results.
While the treatment produced a 5.3% decrease in donations (10% level of confidence), this amounted to just one less donation than the control. Because of the flat and inconclusive results of this experiment, we can determine that the statement's inclusion was not a significant enough motivating factor to move the needle, perhaps because the security of the transaction is already adequately conveyed with a visual indication shown around the payment fields.
Based on these results, we will look for other test ideas that may have a larger impact on potential donors' motivation to give.

experiments.title: How giving is impacted by the use of buttons in an email appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing donation buttons from an email appeal for email recipients will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/17/2023
RTB historically uses multiple “Give Now” buttons in email appeals. During calendar year end we decided to run an experiment where we removed the buttons from the email appeal. The idea is that the removal of the buttons would actually humanize the communication more, make the email seem less promotion-like, and encourage people to read the entire appeal/case for support before deciding to give. This in turn, has the potential to increase donor conversion rate. We chose one email in the campaign to test this concept.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Results in this experiment were flat. We saw .35% decrease in donor conversion rate with a .68% level of confidence. The average gift across both the control and treatment were also negligible (control: $250, treatment: $238) so the humanized treatment did not inspire people to give more either. A concern in removing the buttons from the email was that it would negatively impact click thru rate. However, results indicate that the removal of the buttons has no effect on click thru rate either. Click thru rate stayed the same at .4%.
Where we did see a noticeable difference was the impact on new donors. New donors experienced a 497% increase in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 94%.  More non donors were activated to give through the treatment (no buttons) than the control. While it's not a large number of transactions, the organization should consider using this new layout (no buttons) knowing that it will not impact giving overall but may active more new donors. It's likely that non donors saw the email as less transactional/promotional and decided to read more of the appeal and were therefore more motivated to give.

experiments.title: How adding an additional and different CTA in the PS of an email appeal impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an additional call to action in an email appeal for prospective donors will achieve a decrease in donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/10/2023
During calendar year end RTB wanted to be sure to let their constituents know about additional giving options for their 70+ aged donors. There was concern that too many CTA’s would interfere with donor conversion rate so we tested the P.S. message across two different emails in order to get more data around this decision. Our hypothesis was that an additional, different CTA would negatively impact donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we did not see a valid result from this experiment, we did see a 29% decrease in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 70%. It's recommended that before rolling this out to additional emails that this is tested across several more emails to get a large enough sample size as the data seems to indicate a decrease in donor conversion rate when adding the different CTA in the P.S. of the email.

experiments.title: How using a clear call-to-action focused on taking the next step of giving a donation in a high urgency campaign affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using clear call-to-action language  for subscribers and donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/27/2022 - 12/31/2022
As a part of Luther Seminary’s calendar year-end campaign, we wanted to test the concept of using a clear call-to-action to give a donation vs using a more vague call-to-action of “learn more” to see how it affects donor conversion from the email to the donation page.
We believe that using a clear call-to-action that focuses on taking the next step is most effective as focusing on something vague like “learn more” doesn’t set the right expectation on the next page (donation page).
For this experiment, we tested this concept throughout the final four appeal emails of this campaign in order to receive enough traffic to have the possibility of reaching a valid level of confidence.
The control emails used clear call-to-action language to make a donation and the treatment emails used vague call-to-action language around “learn more”.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment emails using the vague call-to-action saw a 14% increase in conversion rate with only a 35.3% level of confidence therefore the clear call-to-action did not have an effect on the users decision to give.
We believe this happened because the motivation for year-end giving is already higher than all other times of the year. Because of this, using vague or clear language for the call-to-action did not effect whether someone was going to make a donation or not. It's also safe to say that there was not confusion between the vague call-to-action in the treatment email and the donation page.
We would like to test this concept again on another appeal email outside of calendar year-end.

experiments.title: How a slide up CTA with a daily donor goal impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a slide-up CTA with a daily donor goal will increase donor conversion
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/26/2022 - 10/31/2022
Buckner International is a Christian nonprofit that has been serving children and families for more than one hundred years. They wanted to find ways of getting more visitors to become monthly donors. They had used sticky bars before and found success with these at acquiring donors from visitors to their website. They hypothesized that using a different style message, one that slid up from the bottom of the page, and that included a daily donor goal would increase donor conversion. They hoped that this message would, if not leading to more monthly donors acquired, at least lead to more donors acquired.
They used a bright yellow color design to catch the eye and a low appeal threshold of just $15/month.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the experiment for just over a month, we were unable to validate an impact on donor conversion from the slide-up. Our sample size of gifts was just too small. We do however want to test micro goals presented in either sticky bars or slide up CTAs during future campaigns, particularly during seasons of high urgency when we are more likely to get enough data to validate the result.

It is possible that a transition to iDonate that took place during this test may have caused us to be unable to track donations made on the main donation page that were influenced by the popup rather than made through the popup.

experiments.title: How emphasizing the ‘I’m new’ button for new website visitors impacted email acquisition rates for First Baptist Dallas
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlight a specific section for new visitors will achieve a change in click throughs.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/15/2022 - 09/16/2022
As one of the largest Baptist churches in the country, First Baptist Dallas drives a lot of new website traffic to it’s site every day. For this experiment, we wanted to test whether highlighting the ‘I’m New’ navigation menu in the header menu for new visitors only made an impact on email sign-ups on that specific page.
Our hypothesis was that by highlighting this section, any new visitor to the site would easily find information that was more relevant to them, instead of spending time searching through the site. New visitors were also asked to fill out a form on that page to receive more information; by reducing the amount of friction to find that form, our hypothesis was that email acquisition rates would be higher.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Highlight the 'I'm New' header navigation menu for new visitors to the site led to a small, 1.7%, increase in email acquisition rates, with only a 48.8% level of confidence.

While this shows a slight directional lift in email rates, this test warrants further refinement. For example, the form where people are asked to fill in their information is actually a plan your visit (for Sunday service) form; not all of the new visitors to the homepage went there with the intention of attending a Sunday service. The form itself is also at the bottom of the 'I'm New' page; further testing would place this form higher, potentially above the fold, and make it more appealing to new visitors by offering more than just Sunday service information in exchange for an email sign-up.

experiments.title: How a give now button at the top of the donation page anchored to the form impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a give now button at the top of the donation page anchored to the form will increase donor conversion
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/14/2022 - 10/07/2022
KCBI is a large Christian radio station in the DFW area with a worldwide listening audience. They wanted to see if the length of their donation page was causing friction for donors already motivated to give and simply looking for the donation form on the page. During their Fall Campaign, they decided to test the impact of a “give now” button at the top of the page that was anchored to the form. Those coming to the page motivated by the email they read or the on-air appeal they heard, could click that button and drop straight to the form. They hypothesized that this could increase donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We ran this experiment for three weeks and were unable to validate the impact of the anchor button. When digging into the results by channel (note: the T1 space in the results window above is actually the control without the anchor button), we found that there was a 17% or so decrease in conversion on direct traffic, but an 11.7% increase on email traffic conversion. While neither of these segments were statistically valid, we hypothesized that there is a possibility that email was showing a positive result because the email traffic was primed to give by the appeal they'd just read and thus wanted the button and the decreased friction it provided. We saw that returning traffic was also showing a higher conversion rate (though not valid) and we believe it would be for the same reason.
We will not roll out a give now button but may test a similar approach on a future offer.

experiments.title: How adding a monthly donation button to the website’s main navigation menu impacts onsite donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a monthly donate button to the website’s main navigation menu will increase donor conversion
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/10/2022 - 10/24/2022
KCBI is a large Christian radio station in the DFW area with a worldwide listening audience. They wanted to see if they could improve donor conversion on their website by adding a monthly donation button to their main navigation next to their one-time donate button. Monthly donors are an important part of their program and they hoped that this additional callout would increase monthly donor conversion.
When they first tested this approach (see prior experiment here), they found a significant increase in donor conversion. However, upon further analysis, that experiment changed multiple variables, so its results were not reliable. They then tested each of the individual variables:
Test the position of the one-time gift button (see experiment writeup here)
Test the copy on the one-time gift button (give vs donate) – see experiment writeup here
And finally, they tested the addition of a monthly donation button in the main navigation menu. This test ran more than two months.
experiments.keyLearnings:
At no point did the additional button increase donor conversion. For several weeks, it appeared that there would be a valid decrease in donor conversion measured, but that too never reached a point of statistical validity. Ultimately, we were unable to measure a positive impact from adding the button so we will not be changing the navigation menu.
For future tests, we will not let an experiment run as long as this did because we will begin experiencing data pollution past 4-weeks of testing. This experience also showed the importance of not just taking an initial win in an experiment at face value, but ensuring that we are measuring each variable correctly and, if not, intentionally testing each and every variable so we know what is in fact impacting conversion rates.

experiments.title: How Moving the Global Navigation Donate Button Onto Its Own Menu Line Impact Donor Conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using “Donate Now” on the global navigation donation button will increase donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/05/2022 - 07/24/2022
KCBI is a large Christian radio station in the DFW area with a worldwide listening audience. In a prior test, they’d found that the addition of a “monthly” donate button to their global site navigation menu increased donor conversion. However, there were some issues with that test that made the results unclear. We had several factors that changed in the experiment (the placement of the buttons, the wording on the buttons, and the addition of a monthly donate button) that clouded the results. In order to understand WHICH factor led to the increase in donor conversion, we decided to test simply moving the “give now” button out of the menu line with the other options and onto its own line. Since this would only impact Desktop traffic, that is the only source of donors we examined.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 50.9% decrease with a 94% level of confidence.
Direct visitors had a 56.2% decrease in donations with an 86% level of confidence.
 	All Traffic visitors had a 37.6% decrease in donations with a 91% level of confidence.
 	55-64 visitors had a 6.7% increase in donations with a 96% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How splitting out a monthly donate button impacted monthly donor donations
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that calling attention to a monthly donor program for potential donors will achieve a change in donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/18/2022 - 05/16/2022
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate serve poor and needy people in the United States and more than 70 countries around the world. The recruitment of monthly donors makes up a large part of their digital program; new monthly donors are offered the opportunity to have a candle lit at the shrine of our Lady of the Snows every month.
For this experiment, which we ran during the Easter season, we wanted to test whether splitting out a donate and monthly-donate-specific button would have any impact on new monthly donor conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment had a 23% increase in the number of new monthly donors who were converted using the monthly call out button, but only had a 46% level of confidence given the lower total number of new monthly donors that came in during that time period.
Despite this experiment running during what is a traditionally a high-impact giving season (Easter), the number of donations was not enough to give us a valid result.
Nevertheless, this is a concept that we will continue testing on the Oblate's main website, especially given the importance of new monthly donors on their program overall.

experiments.title: The impact on donor conversion of saying “Donate” vs “Give” on the global navigation menu donation button
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using “Donate Now” on the global navigation donation button will increase donor conversion
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/21/2022 - 08/12/2022
KCBI is a large Christian radio station in the DFW area with a worldwide listening audience. In a recent test, we added a monthly donation button to the global navigation menu and recorded a lift in onetime donor conversion. However, in examining the results of that test, we found that we had several changes between the control and the treatment, muddying the results. This prompted us to launch a series of new tests to determine what influence each of those changes had on donor conversion.
The last of these clarifying tests was to determine the impact of saying, “donate” instead of “give” on the button. In that original test, the control had “give now” and no monthly donation button while the treatment had “donate now” and “give monthly.”
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for approximately three weeks, we found no statistically significant impact on "donate now" vs the control, "give now" button. This showed us that the change from "give now" to "donate now" in the original test did not impact the donor conversion of visitors to the site. Now, we can retest the addition of a monthly donation button to see if it has a positive impact on donor conversion.

experiments.title: How adding more clarity to a landing page headline affects sign up conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more clarity into the headline for readers that land on this page will achieve a higher number of email conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/14/2022 - 08/14/2022
Focus on the Family has a number of free video series on topics around Faith, Marriage, and Parenting. The goal of putting out these free video series is to share free content around these topics and to collect names and email addresses of interested subscribers and then eventually nurture them towards a donation to the mission. The process is to promote the free video series on social media campaign, drive traffic to a landing page where they collect info like email address and name to send a link to access the free series, then upon a successful info collection, immediately send the subscriber to an instant donation page where they have the opportunity to provide a gift.
As we evaluate opportunities for optimization all along the marketing funnel, we look for metrics that show areas where we can make improvements in traffic conversion (CPMs, CTRs, CPCs), email conversions (Signup conversion rates), and instant donation conversions (ICRs). For this particular experiment, this email collection landing page is part of the overall campaign strategy and what we would refer to as the “middle of the marketing funnel”. The signup conversion rates on this campaign were lower than average, so we felt there was an opportunity to make improvements.
We decided to test if adding more clarity in the headline would result in a higher number of email signup conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for a short duration being promoted through a social media campaign to get traffic. This campaign (like all Focus on the Family campaigns) is evaluated on a weekly basis for performance against a lofty ROI equation that requires a 1.1 ROI in order to continue getting paid media allocation and thus traffic to potentially validate.  While there was not enough sample size to validate the experiment, we saw a decrease of around a 27% for the treatment, with a 27% level of confidence.
Due to the condensed timeframe for these experiments from media allocation, we experimented with two variants (headline clarity and increased emphasis on the call to action text - not shown in the screenshot).   While we typically like to limit experiments to 1 variable to isolate learnings, we opted for two variants in the same experiment to give a better opportunity for lift. With a low Level of Confidence (LoC - around 27%) and a small sample size, we cannot say definitively if the increased headline clarity or the increased emphasis on the CTA were the cause of the drop in conversions. We can say that we saw a decrease in conversions and gained learnings that we can continue to apply to future landing pages.
For future experiments with longer run times, we will try to isolate variants into single treatments for better learning.

experiments.title: How a “Learn More” CTA on a Facebook Ad Impacted Donor Conversion When Compared with “Donate Now.”
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a “Donate Now” CTA on a Facebook Ad will increase donor conversion
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/20/2022 - 04/29/2022
KCBI is a leading Christian radio station and ministry in Dallas, Texas. For their spring fundraising campaign, they wanted to see if changing the text on the call to action button on an ad would increase donor conversion. Specifically, if saying “donate now” instead of “learn more” would increase donor conversion on the ad. When we tested “Donate Now” with another organization, it increased donor conversion, so KCBI hoped this would work with their audience as well.
experiments.keyLearnings: Unfortunately, due to a low donor response, we really didn't have any statistical level of significance for donations or cost per click. We will keep this in mind and see if we can find further opportunities for testing this approach with their followers on Facebook.

experiments.title: How a passive call to action on a sticky bar impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a passive call to action in a sticky bar for site visitors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/22/2021 - 01/04/2022
During calendar year end, Avila added a site-wide sticky bar to bring attention to their high urgency campaign. We wanted to make it clear that there was an urgent need for the organization during this time but wanted to test how a more passive call to action in the button would impact donor conversion rate. Would audiences be more intrigued to “learn more” on how they can contribute or would the be more motivated to give by being given a clear call to action to “give now”?
experiments.keyLearnings: Results did not validate for this experiment. We saw a 10% decrease in donor conversion with a level of confidence of 65%. This experiment did not have a long enough run time to gather the necessary sample size. It is recommended that this concept is retested during the next high urgency campaign and launched earlier in the campaign in order to garner a larger sample size.

experiments.title: How reiterating the value proposition by the “submit” button affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition language by the submit button for prospective donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/29/2021 - 01/30/2022
TFAS was running an offer (Democratic Socialism eBook) that had a high sign-up rate and a high gross cost per donor. In an effort to inspire more donations on the instant donation page we added a sentence directly underneath the “submit” button on the donation form. This sentence was value proposition-focused and would serve as a reminder of what their gift is going towards.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of this experiment did not validate. There was no statistical difference between those who saw the value prop language underneath the button vs those who did not. Our hypothesis is that this may have been too small of a difference with the given traffic to produce a valid result. The next step is to test this among multiple instant donation pages in order to produce valid learning.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Desktop visitors had a 75.1% increase in donations with a 97% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding an impact statement under the donate button affects conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an impact statement under the donate button for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/18/2021 - 02/11/2022
Previous experiments have shown that showing donors the direct impact of their donations may inspire them to give, decreasing abandonment and improving conversions rates.
We wanted to leverage this learning in an effort to improve flat conversion rates on a donation page where visitors land after signing up for one of Focus on the Family’s video series. For this experiment, we developed a treatment that included an impact statement directly below the donate button to bring more attention to the value of their donation. We hypothesized that the inclusion of a short statement telling donors what their donations have achieved would motivate them to give, reducing abandonment and improving conversion rates by putting the donor center-stage. For this experiment, we ran an A/B test with traffic split 50/50.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 82 days, we observed a 11.9% lift in donor conversion rate with 69% level of confidence so the results are not valid. However, when we looked at how individual segments responses, we found that returning visitors had a 207% increase in conversions with 100% level of confidence, hinting that including an impact statement with donor-centric language near the donate button can provide donors with the extra inspiration they need to give by illustrating the importance and effectiveness of their donation.
It was also interesting to discover that while desktop visitors had a 181.4% increase in donations with 99% level of confidence, mobile visitors had a 14.9% decrease in donations with 79.0% level of confidence. This is an important observation and could indicate a shallower scroll-depth for mobile users. This may warrant a future test that moves the value proposition above the donation button on mobile devices.
The promising results of this experiment further reinforce that adding value proposition at the point of final decision making - when the donor clicks the make my gift button - can help highlight the impact of their donation and inspire them to complete the giving process.

experiments.title: How user focused messaging impacts donor conversion from the donate button
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a user focused message increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/15/2021 - 08/30/2021
After FamilyLife launched their new brand, they added some of their new brand messaging to the primary navigation header next to the donate button. The messaging read, “Because Family Matters Most”. We hypothesized that a message that was more user focused might help increase conversion from the donation button.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for six weeks, we weren't able to validate the overall results. That said, on desktop devices there was a 13.7% decrease in donor conversion with a 89% level of confidence, but on mobile devices there was a 22% increase in donor conversion with a 94% level of confidence. This difference in conversion results is why the experiment didn't validate overall.
These findings are interesting in that mobile traffic is much more motivated by user centric messaging. We hypothesize that the placement and presence of this copy is also so much more visible on mobile devices which resulted in mobile visitors who were maybe on the fence about donating, made the decision to donate after seeing the messaging that had a clearer, user focused call-to-action.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign of a landing page with a seasonal offer impacted email conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a radical redesign for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in email conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/12/2021 - 04/08/2021
In the weeks leading up to Easter, a sticky bar on the homepage was pointing visitors to a landing page where they could sign up for a free resource offer with Easter-focused family activities. We wanted to test a radical redesign of the landing page to see if we could increase overall email acquisition on this offer. We developed a treatment that removed the traditional footer with links to alternate pages on the site to avoid distraction from the intended call to action, which was to submit the email acquisition form and sign up for the free PDF.
We also revised the header on the page from “Adventures in Odyssey: Easter Faith Building Activities!” to “Get access to your free copy now and help your family celebrate Easter together with this free PDF of fun ideas for activities and devotionals to prompt meaningful conversations!” in an effort to make the offer less organization-focused and more focused on the page visitor.
The third element we changed for the treatment version of the page was enhancing the overall value proposition and added in language that helped address the why behind someone might benefit from the contents of the free guide rather than only sharing what was inside. We spoke directly to the felt needs of the audience and the emotions they might be experiencing as a parent and how this resource could alleviate some of the stress that often accompanies the anticipation of the Easter holiday.
Finally, we changed the CTA on the form from “To get your FREE download of Easter Faith Building Activities, fill out the form below” to “Tell us where to send your FREE copy of Adventure in Odyssey’s Easter Faith Building Activities:” and switched the button copy from “Download Here” to “Send my free copy »” to make this portion feel like less work for the user. We set up the control and the treatment to run as an A/B test and split traffic to each version.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Since this was a limited time offer, we didn't have enough run-time to acquire the number of visitors to the page needed to validate results and we only reached a 35% level of confidence on the experiment. However, what we saw was a few observations on specific visitor segments that are outlined below. Even though we did not reach statistical significance overall, we can use these learnings to guide the development of language for future tests that target display/tablet visitors and speak more relevantly to females and those who are 65+. We could also use this knowledge to our advantage by keeping this audience in mind for introducing social proof on a landing page by adding a testimonial quote from a 65+ female who found the resource valuable. We can also test each of these items from the radical redesign individually on other free offers in the future to have more insight into which changes led to these increases and decreases per segment.
Desktop visitors had a 31.7% decrease in outbound link click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Display visitors had a 42.1% increase in outbound link click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 34.9% decrease in outbound link click with a 94% level of confidence.
 	Tablet visitors had a 33.1% increase in outbound link click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Organic Search visitors had a 28.5% decrease in outbound link click with a 95% level of confidence.
 	Referral visitors had a 32.0% decrease in outbound link click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	Female visitors had a 39.5% increase in outbound link click with a 98% level of confidence.
 	Direct visitors had a 100.0% decrease in outbound link click with a 99% level of confidence.
 	Social visitors had a 100.0% decrease in outbound link click with a 97% level of confidence.
 	25-34 visitors had a 72.0% decrease in outbound link click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	35-44 visitors had an 86.2% decrease in outbound link click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	65+ visitors had a 1,211.5% increase in outbound link click with a 100% level of confidence.
 	18-24 visitors had a 34.0% increase in outbound link click with a 100% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How showing the effect of a gift on a child’s future and emotional health will impact sponsorship conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: That showing what the child’s life is and what life will be after the donors gifts, with all the emotions and impact of that gift, will increase child sponsorships.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/08/2021 - 04/09/2021
PFI updated their website and the full layout of their sponsorship pages. While driving higher traffic to these pages, we wanted to take advantage of that traffic to test the sliding doors theory—showing what is reality now versus what reality could be with the sponsors gift. Another key component to this theory is ensuring that the donor feels the impact of the loss on the child’s life and similarly feels the transformation emotionally that their gift will create for this child. We tested this for over a month on their child specific page, the decision point where the audience decides if they will sponsor this particular child or not.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Despite the increased traffic to these pages, there was no discernible difference in sponsorship. A 52% level of confidence is a veritable coin flip either way.
Our hypothesis is that copy alone is not enough to impact conversion one way or the other. Our next step is to test tactical page design test to keep folks on the child sponsorship page and make this the complete decision point. With the current design, users are presented the option to leave the page at several different points. In fact, twenty percent of the gifts that were made on the control were people who left this page to give a donation to the main PFI donation page.
Revenue did experience a 27.9% decrease with a 93% level of confidence. This is because more people were likely to give a one-time gift on the control.

experiments.title: How including a hot button issue in the subject line affects open rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will mentioning free speech in the subject line improve the open rate of the email?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/13/2018 - 07/14/2018
The Leadership Institute works with college students to help defend their free speech on campus. They wanted to see if including hot button terms like “free speech” and “the one percent” in the subject line would affect open rates. Their hypothesis was that the subject line about free speech would generate more opens.
experiments.keyLearnings: The open rates for each email were no more than .4% deviations away from 20%. This experiment was not conclusive. It’s likely that the hot button issues did not affect open rate as much due to the specified name of the school in the subject line. A further experiment without a specified name may render desired results to confidently conclude this experiment.

experiments.title: How a dropdown on a donate button affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a drop down navigation option for one-time and recurring gifts increase overall (or recurring) giving?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/21/2019 - 07/20/2019
For Caringbridge, the primary navigation has always had a prominent donation button in the top right corner. This button took all potential donors to a generic donation page that offered both one-time and recurring giving options.
There was a hypothesis that since a recurring donor and a one-time donor are two distinct segments, it may make sense to split this audience at the navigation level. Doing this would allow for targeted donation pages for each segment which may help increase the relevance and perceived value of the offer.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for two months with over 10 million visitors, we found that the drop down navigation offer had no statistically significant impact on overall giving or the likelihood for individuals to give a recurring gift. This could potentially suggest that we're bringing this split too high up into the conversion funnel. Instead of the navigation, it may make more sense to emphasize this segmentation split (with associated value proposition) on the donation page itself. Similar experiments have been run using tabs on donation forms with success. That would be a natural next step.

experiments.title: How a text link vs a button link affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Do people donate through a text link or a button link in an email?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/14/2016 - 04/19/2016
Traditionally in SBA List appeal emails, a “donate” button is be placed at the bottom of the email. In one of the emails during a SBA List moneybomb they wanted to see the effect that a text link vs a button link has on donor conversion.  The email to the entire file had the same copy and featured both a text link and a button link. Are people clicking on the donate button more than the text link in the email and giving? They tested this to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings: Though the test did not validate, in the initial findings more people gave through the text link than the button link. This could be attributed to a couple of things. First, the copy on the text link and button were different. The text link said, “Make a gift today” and the button said, “Donate”. The text link gave a more clear call-to-action. Second, the placement of the button was at the very bottom of the email after the senders signature. People may have already been motivated to give before they got to the button. Additional testing will be done to learn if continued use of button links is needed in appeal emails.

experiments.title: How the donation button design impacts conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation button will drive the most traffic to the donation page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/19/2016 - 08/12/2016
To optimize the donation funnel for the Susan B. Anthony List organization, we did an analysis to discover the biggest opportunities. What we found was that there were not enough organic visitors getting to the donation page to do any testing in that area. We decided to move our efforts forward in the funnel to try to drive more people to the page.
We had the hypothesis that if we made the donation page easier to find and put it into “first person” language, we could drive more visitors to the donation page. We decided to create an experiment to test the idea.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment had a nearly identical click through rate and donor conversion rate when compared to the control. The big take away from this experiment is that the button was not hard to find in the first place and that we did not add any significant value with the change in copy. Without those two factors influencing traffic, the treatment did nothing that would impact visitors making it to the donation page.

experiments.title: How a quick donate button at the top of a donation page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the “Quick Donate” button increase the number of donor conversions to the page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/30/2015 - 07/01/2015
Through prior testing, we had learned that the Jews for Jesus visitors were highly motivated donors. As their shortfall campaign was coming to the end, we wanted to lower the barriers to these donors making a gift.  We decided to create an experiment that would place a “Make My Gift Now” button at the top of the page to jump the visitor down to the donation form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Despite 28% of visitors clicking on the “Make My Gift Now” button having it didn’t significantly improve the overall conversion rate of the page.  However, it did seem to lower the average gift which may be attributed to roughly 1 out of 4 visitors skipping over the value proposition copy when they jumped down to the donation form.
By skipping over this copy, they did not read the reasons why their gift was needed which may have impacted how much they gave.  Again, this was not statistically proved and would require further experiments to validate the hypothesis.

experiments.title: How the addition of a button at the bottom of an email appeal affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a button improve click-through-rate in a monthly email appeal?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/10/2016 - 03/12/2016
Jews for Jesus wanted to see if they could improve their click-through-rate on their monthly eAppeal by adding a button to the bottom of the email as the CTA. They traditionally used text links only in their eAppeals, but wanted to test that against the addition of a button.
experiments.keyLearnings: The addition of the button increased the click-through-rate by 13%. This would suggest that the addition of a button in an email appeal brings clarity to the CTA. It visually drew a person’s attention and showed them where to click. Because this test didn’t validate, additional testing is recommended.

experiments.title: Web Donation Form (Buttons vs. No Buttons)
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding buttons with a predefined choice increase donations and donation/visitor?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/06/2015 - 11/12/2015
We were looking to increase donations on our main donation form. We believed that giving people a pre-defined choice helped decrease anxiety and thus lead to more donations & higher revenue/visitor.
experiments.keyLearnings: We did not achieve confidence on the increase in donations, although the numbers were clearly higher.  Donation/visitor was visibly higher during this test.

experiments.title: Blue Links vs Red Links (Color of CTA)
experiments.researchQuestion: Does the color of a link play a part in the number of click throughs?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/21/2016 - 09/23/2016
Different colors elicit different emotional responses. We are looking to find which colors increase click throughs for our different email audiences/topics.

experiments.title: How the navigation’s donate button copy impacted giving
experiments.researchQuestion: Which button’s call to action will drive the most donations?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/21/2017 - 08/02/2017
On the Live Action News website, we wanted to see if we could apply the learnings we had seen with the LiveAction.org navigation test. In this test, we had found that a button that communicated more value than a simple “Donate” but was still was clear on the purpose, was able to increase donations.
For this test, we decided to test out two options: “Support the Preborn” and “Support Live Action.”
experiments.keyLearnings: None of the treatments had significant impact on the donor conversion rate. It is our hypothesis that this is likely due to the motivation of the audience. With Live Action News, the audience is motivated to read news and see uplifting stories. As a result, the language used around donating did not see much of an impact. In future tests, we will need to lay out the case for support more thoroughly, most likely in the form of the Dear Reader treatment.

experiments.title: How the button text hurt email acquisition rate on a new offer
experiments.researchQuestion: Can something other than “get” in the button text improve email acquisition rates?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/11/2019 - 04/12/2019
When launching a new ebook offer, The Heritage Foundation wanted to test their way into improving email acquisition rates. For a long time, we’ve always used the term “get” in the titles and buttons within offer pages like this, so we decided to run an additional experiment to validate whether or not this old positioning statement still worked best.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 64.6% level of confidence, we saw a -18.6% decrease in email acquisition rate.
Although the experiment didn’t yet validate, we decided to turn off the experiment due to the directional validation that the word “get” does indeed still perform best.
Continued experimentation with the offer should be made to improve email registration rates, but it would appear that the term “get” in the button is not where the testing should be.

experiments.title: How using a button instead of a raw link impacts conversion in a cultivation email
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: Would a button instead of a raw link increase clicks in a cultivation email?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/31/2019
The Missionary Oblates had tested into using raw links for the hyperlinks in their email communication. This finding was over a year ago and they have continued to use raw links in all of their emails since then. As they were sending a cultivation email to their email file they hypothesized that maybe their file had grown used to the raw link and it wouldn’t have the same impact as it had when they had originally tested it. They developed a treatment that used a button instead of a raw link and split their file in half to see the impact on clicks.
experiments.keyLearnings: We saw a directional lift of 5% from the treatment that had the button instead of the raw link. Because of the volume of emails that the Missionary Oblates send, we’d recommend testing this approach again in an effort to validate the approach and track conversions all the way through. We ultimately want to see the impact this kind of experiment has not only on clicks, but donations as well since the cultivation email offered people a free content offer that had a donation ask on the end of it.

experiments.title: How a “Submit” button affects email acquisition on an email opt-in
experiments.researchQuestion: Will the word “submit” on a call-to-action button impact the likelihood of someone signing up for email updates?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/14/2018 - 01/14/2019
In this experiment, we wondered what affect the typical “submit” language on a call-to-action button would have compared to something that communicates more specifically what action is being taken. The control button said “Sign Me Up” and the treatment button said “Submit.”
experiments.keyLearnings: The “Submit” button had no significant impact on conversion. While we expected the submit button might lead to a decrease in conversion, it actually remained flat. It’s possible that neither of these call-to-actions are really communicating any distinct value. It’s also possible that we may have seen a more powerful result with a larger sample size. Follow-up testing will be needed to determine if a more value-focused call-to-action can increase conversion on this offer.

experiments.title: How different colored CTA buttons affect conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does the color of the CampusWire CTA button affect the number of people who click on the CTA?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/22/2018 - 09/07/2018
The Leadership Institute’s Campus Reform daily email, CampusWire, is sent from a template with bullet point lists of news stories. This email is a direct copy of what is sent each morning at 5am to major news outlets across the country. At the end of each email is a CTA button which leads to a donation page. They wanted to test different background colors on the CTA button because they didn’t want to deviate too far from the original template. They decided to test an orange button against the original blue button, and looked for the number of clicks on the CTA button itself rather than click through rate on the email, since there are several links in each email. The images below are just one of many emails that were sent during the experiment time frame. They hypothesized that the colors would make no real difference.
experiments.keyLearnings: With a .03% and .04% conversion rate on the CTA buttons, no real winner can be determined from this experiment when measuring only clicks. However, when comparing the number of clicks to the click through rate for each of the emails, the blue button did receive 35% more clicks with a 95% confidence rate. See the file “Test for CTA color on CampusWire stats” for details (https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1nFI5H606QoeCdA3Kql90NftVcw02kIFXW4dWpzJvOqA/edit#gid=1283200889). Going forward, the Leadership Institute will use a blue background on the CTA for CampusWire.

experiments.title: The Impact of Making Two CTAs in one Appeal Pt. 1
experiments.researchQuestion: Would appealing to donors to “cut the cord” by cutting their entertainment bill (and its support of pro-choice programming) in favor of becoming a monthly donor increase the number of donations we received? What would the impact of making two CTAs in the same appeal be on donations?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/10/2018
For months, Care Net has been brainstorming ways to increase the number of monthly donors, Caring Partners, that we have on file. We proposed testing our traditional appeal language against a new “Cut the Cord” campaign. This appeal would argue that money spent on entertainment providers like Netflix, Amazon Prime, Cable often supports pro-choice initiatives. By “cutting” some of our expenses on such entertainment and redirecting the money to Care Net’s work, lives would be saved and pro-choice orgs would lose funding. A win-win. We hoped that a pro-life person’s ire about pro-choice funding and programming would help motivate them to “cutting the cord” and saving lives.
The drawback to this approach would be that it would be essentially making two calls to actions: Cut expenses on entertainment and become a monthly donor.
experiments.keyLearnings: While the Cut the Cord email had the benefit of a new sender and a higher CTR, the donation rate was significantly lower. The results seemed to confirm that two CTAS in the appeal suppressed donor response as they had to agree to cutting back on something they were already doing and beginning something they weren’t yet doing. This was too much friction. However, we wanted to be sure. The variation had a 33% higher CTR. We wondered if we had too much copy on the donation page for the variation and that people dropped off who were prepared to make a gift. We decided to run an additional test to see if shorter copy on the donation page would cause Cut the Cord to perform better.

experiments.title: How value proposition copy affects clicks on a donate button in an email
experiments.researchQuestion: How will the additional value proposition copy affect clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/19/2018
Leadership Institute sent a daily email called CampusWire, updating their audience about developments through their campus network. At the bottom of the email, they had a simple button that asked the reader to support CampusReform. While this did produce donors, they wondered if adding value proposition to the button would increase clickthrough rate and thus increase donation page traffic. They crafted a treatment and sent it to half of the file for several weeks to see if it would increase conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After several weeks, there was no statistical difference between the two treatments. Clickthrough rate overall was low, since this is at the bottom of an email designed to get people to click through to the site, but it showed that the additional value proposition was not significantly increasing motivation—which suggests that readers were already motivated to give just through the content.
This experiment deserves further review to see if donation page conversion improved. It also suggests that fundraising might increase if this value proposition was broken out into its own email, away from the context.

experiments.title: How reducing friction and aligning the CTA with the end result affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will reducing friction and aligning the CTA with the end result affect conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/25/2018 - 04/26/2018
Compassion International has an option on their navigation bar to “select a child for me”. A popout picked a child from the database and gave them the “choose me” call-to-action. When the user clicked “choose me”, they were taken to the biography page of the child to learn more about them.
The CI team hypothesized that if they made “choose me” actually add the child to the user’s shopping cart, they would increase the number of people who followed through on their sponsorship.
They split their traffic and watched the data to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: Over a two month period, this experiment produced a 12% lift in conversions, though it didn’t achieve statistical validity due to low traffic. In this case, a lift of this magnitude is worth rolling out without statistical validity, as the total traffic through this funnel makes the test unlikely to validate at any time of year.

experiments.title: How instrumentation error affects a donation button test
experiments.researchQuestion: Which donation button will drive the most traffic to the donate page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/15/2015 - 11/18/2015
The Hillsdale online courses donation page currently averages a conversion rate of ~40%. While it is exciting to have this high of a conversion rate, what this implied to us is that we are only sending the most highly motivated visitors to the page.  As a result, we wanted to move our testing to earlier in the donor funnel to see if we could drive an increased volume of traffic to the donation page.
We decided to run a test on the donation button that is displayed across the online courses website. The current button uses “Donate” as the call to action and the color blends into the background a bit. We had the hypothesis that we could improve the click rate by making the button stand out more and changing the CTA to be a bit softer of language.
experiments.keyLearnings: The results of this test are invalid due to instrumentation error. It would appear that the VWO snippet was removed from many of the pages and blocked from several others.

experiments.title: How clarity of navigation buttons impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will shortening the Renew Membership link text in the navigation impact donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/04/2016 - 02/06/2017
Prior to the launch of the new Heritage homepage, we wanted to test out if the new proposed navigation would have an impact on donor conversion. The navigation was trying to limit the number of words for each link to just one. As a result “Renew Membership” would be shortened to just “Membership.”
Our concern was that this change may decrease the motivation of the people clicking on the link which would, in turn, decrease the total donor conversion from the homepage. We set up a test to validate this hypothesis.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Shortening the navigation link to just one word did not have any significant impact on the overall donor conversion of visitor coming from the home page. We did see a 53% increase in traffic from the shortened link but since these were less motivated visitors, the additional traffic did not impact the conversion in either direction.
The good news is that it does not appear that changing the navigation will significantly impact overall donor conversion.

experiments.title: How a button to receive the “incentive” impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will “accepting the challenge” button increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/18/2016 - 07/28/2016
During the 2016 Heritage Board Challenge moneybomb, we wanted to test a tactic that we had observed on several large ecommerce sites. In these sites, the visitors had to click a button to receive an incentive. Our belief was that this action increased the perceived value of the offer that they were receiving which would positively impact conversion.
During the Board Challenge, the incentive is the match from the board. We created a button above the donation form that was a way for the visitor to “Accept the challenge.” Once pressed, it told them their gift would be doubled.
experiments.keyLearnings: We did not see a statistically significant impact from the button after running for a full week. This would indicate that it did not increased the perceived value of the matching incentive as we hypothesized that it would.

experiments.title: How the call-to-action of the button impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a more direct call to action increase the motivation and subsequent donor conversion of website visitors?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/31/2017 - 04/14/2017
A previous analysis of the Dear Reader experiment found that we were able to drive a significant volume of donor traffic to the donation page but were seeing an 8% conversion of those visitors. Given the low conversion of the traffic, we developed a hypothesis that the call-to-action “Support Illinois Policy Institute” may not be clear enough. We decided to test the language on the button using a more direct “Donate Today” call-to-action. Our hypothesis is that this direct statement would receive less overall traffic but more donor motivated traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for 2 weeks, we had not yet reach statistical validity. Based upon the progress of the test, we expected it would take another full month before we would know with certainty the impact of the experiment. We decided to move on to another experiment that we felt could have a higher potential impact and roll out the “Donate Today” language for future iterations.

experiments.title: How increasing perceived security on a donate button affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will moving the donate button inside the secure box increase conversions?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/14/2017 - 08/15/2017
CaringBridge had performed extensive testing on their “tribute” donation form, which produced the majority of their online fundraising revenue. One experiment was able to increase the conversion rate on the page by increasing perceived security, adding a box around the credit card information. However, this box left the donate button outside of the box. Their team wondered if this was leaving conversions on the table due to the button being perceived as “insecure”.
They launched a version of the page that moved the donate button into the “secure box” and launched an A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: This test failed to produce a valid result in either direction. While the secure box produced a lift, simply moving the donate button inside the box was not enough of a difference-maker to affect conversion positively or negatively.

experiments.title: How taking a softer call to action affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: A softer call-to-action within the email will drive more traffic to the website which will, in turn, convert a greater number of donors.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/26/2014 - 12/27/2014
As it was getting closer to the end of the year, Heritage wanted to test a messaging approach with their fifth email in their year end campaign. Toward the end of urgency-based marketing campaigns, it is common practice to make a direct ask in the email.  The question was how early can that direct ask be made and have it still be effective.
experiments.keyLearnings: The softer language in the email increased the number of visitors by 41% but it actually converted fewer total donors in the test.  At this stage in the campaign, there does not seem to be any impact to response rate by utilizing a softer call to action.

experiments.title: How the “Next Article” button impacted engagement – V2
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a “Next Article” button to the blog increase the percent of people that explore the ADF site?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/20/2016 - 01/03/2017
We had previously validated an experiment that proved a “Next Article” button at the end of a blog actually decreased the amount of people that read the next article. We had doubts about the validity of this experiment due to a technical issue so we decided to run the experiment again to see if the same results would be achieved.
experiments.keyLearnings: This proved what we expected about the previous test; there was a technical issue that had invalidated the results. This experiment proved the simple act of adding the button did not impact the likelihood of the visitor to read the next article.

experiments.title: How adding a Donate Now button at the top affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding a donate button at the top of the page for returning visitors increase their likelihood to give?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/12/2015 - 07/01/2015
For their fiscal year end campaign, Hillsdale College wanted to find a way to make it easier for their donors to make a gift.  The landing page being used contained a large amount of copy in order to fully communicate the college’s unique value proposition however this copy also added friction to the page.
We had a hypothesis that if we could add a way for returning visitors to skip this copy, by placing a Donate Now button at the top, we could reduce the overall friction of the page.  The concern with this approach is that this may ultimately reduce the average gift of the donations since they are not seeing the full value proposition.
We decided to run an experiment targeting just return visitors to the page and testing two different copy versions of the button versus no button at all.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Showing the buttons to returning visitors did not make a significant impact on either conversion rate or revenue.
When digging deeper into the results, we found that only 2% to 5% of returning visitors even clicked on the buttons.  This may speak more to their location outside of the eye path of the page than their effectiveness at reducing friction for the donor.  It may be worth running this experiment again but placing the button more in the eye path of the visitors.

experiments.title: How taking a softer call to action affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: A softer call-to-action within the email will drive more traffic to the website which will, in turn, convert a greater number of donors.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/26/2014 - 12/27/2014
As it was getting closer to the end of the year, Heritage wanted to test a messaging approach with their fifth email in their year end campaign. Toward the end of urgency-based marketing campaigns, it is common practice to make a direct ask in the email.  The question was how early can that direct ask be made and have it still be effective.
experiments.keyLearnings: The softer language in the email increased the number of visitors by 41% but it actually converted fewer total donors in the test.  At this stage in the campaign, there does not seem to be any impact to response rate by utilizing a softer call to action.
